gmw_00003889.name: PDF J011055.8 453920
gmw_00003889.overview: PDF J011055.8 453920 is an unclassified galaxy with asymmetric optical isophotes that suggest a three-armed spiral or a second nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413]. Its emission spectrum and radio morphology indicate that the galaxy is an H II region type undergoing star formation, possibly induced by a merger [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413].

sources.summary: The paper describes PDF J011055.8 453920 as an unclassified, asymmetric galaxy whose emission spectrum and radio morphology point to star formation, possibly merger-induced.

quotes.quote: PDF J011055.8 453920: This galaxy is present but unclassified in Dressler's catalogue. The optical isophotes hint at a three-armed spiral or the presence of a second nucleus. The radio contours, centred on the peak of the optical emission, show a slight extension in the direction of the optical asymmetry. The spectrum shows emission features which clearly define the galaxy as an H ii region type in spectral diagnostic diagrams. The radio emission is probably due to star formation.
If so, this is the only galaxy in the cluster whose unusual optical morphology gives possible evidence of merger-induced star formation.

gmw_00003890.name: PDF J011119.1 455554
gmw_00003890.overview: PDF J011119.1 455554 is an Sb spiral galaxy identified as ESO 243 G 051 [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413]. Its radio contours trace the optical emission, and its spectrum indicates both active galactic nucleus activity and star formation [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PDF J011119.1 455554 with ESO 243 G 051, an Sb galaxy whose radio/FIR and emission-line evidence suggest both AGN and star formation contributions.

quotes.quote: PDF J011119.1 455554: This galaxy is ESO 243 G 051. Dressler's classification is Sb. The radio contours are centred close to the optical nucleus and show extension tracing the optical emission. The spectrum shows absorption features with weak H and [ N ii ] emission, with [ N ii ] stronger and indicative of an AGN. However, the 60 flux density is 0.598 Jy, giving S_ 60 /S_ 1.4 GHz =90.0 , consistent with the well-established radio/FIR correlation for star-forming galaxies.

Both AGN and star-formation processes may be present. The radio and H luminosities can be used in this case to provide an upper limit to the SFR, giving SFR _ 1.4 <3.5 M_ yr ^ 1 and SFR _ H <0.009 M_ yr ^ 1 .

gmw_00003891.name: PDF J011153.5 455847
gmw_00003891.overview: PDF J011153.5 455847 is an early-type galaxy with a radio source displaced from its optical nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413]. Its radio emission may originate from both star formation and nuclear activity, and a faint optical companion lies near the direction of the radio displacement [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413].

sources.summary: The paper describes PDF J011153.5 455847 as an early-type galaxy with displaced radio emission, ambiguous spectral diagnostics, and a nearby possible companion aligned with the radio offset.

quotes.quote: PDF J011153.5 455847: This galaxy has early-type optical morphology. The radio source is displaced from the optical nucleus. The spectrum shows emission features and the galaxy's location in spectral diagnostic diagrams is ambiguous. The radio emission may be coming from both star formation and nuclear activity. The large nearby edge-on spiral galaxy is unrelated, being at a redshift of 0.089, but there is a faint apparent optical companion lying close to the direction of displacement of the radio emission.

gmw_00003892.name: PDF J011219.3 455322
gmw_00003892.overview: PDF J011219.3 455322 is an Scd spiral galaxy that exhibits knotty or dusty features [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413]. It has optical companion galaxies, asymmetric radio emission, and spectral diagnostics that suggest it may host both star formation and active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PDF J011219.3 455322 with ESO 244 G 001, an Scd galaxy with asymmetric radio emission, knotty features, companions, and ambiguous star-formation/AGN diagnostics.

quotes.quote: PDF J011219.3 455322: Catalogued as ESO 244 G 001, this galaxy has an Scd morphology, and shows knotty or dusty features. The radio emission is asymmetric with respect to the optical nucleus, and extended southward where no optical emission is present. The apparent optical companions show no radio emission, and a low-level radio companion is also present with no optical counterpart. The spectrum shows emission features, and has an ambiguous classification between diagnostic diagrams. This galaxy may be hosting both star formation and AGN activity.

gmw_00003893.name: host galaxy of SN2007bg
gmw_00003893.overview: The host galaxy of SN2007bg is a very faint detected source located near the position of the supernova [arXiv:1806.10613]. Because of its faintness, the galaxy does not allow for reliable magnitude and size measurements [arXiv:1806.10613].

sources.summary: The paper treats the SN2007bg host as a very faint detected source near the supernova, too faint for reliable size or magnitude measurement, with only limits reported.

quotes.quote: SN2007bg: There is a very faint source near the position of the SN, which we assume to be the actual host galaxy. The other two sources nearby are offset by such an amount that the projected offsets, normalized by the size of the galaxy (i.e. r_ 50 ), when computed, are in a completely different regime as for the rest of the sample. The presumable host is too faint to allow for a reliable magnitude and size measurement. We only provide a magnitude upper limit and an estimate of the projected offset. We note that Kelly12 presumably assumed the nearby bright source to be the host galaxy.

gmw_00003894.name: host galaxy of SN2007ce
gmw_00003894.overview: The host galaxy of SN2007ce is a compact, blue region [arXiv:1806.10613]. It remains unclear whether this region is an independent dwarf galaxy or part of a larger complex [arXiv:1806.10613].

sources.summary: The paper discusses the SN2007ce host as a compact blue region whose membership in a larger complex is uncertain, so both interpretations are analyzed.

quotes.quote: SN2007ce: The SN occurred near the position of a compact, blue region. It is unclear whether the region is a part of the larger complex (see Figure fig1 ). We conduct the analysis for both scenarios (see Table tab2 ). For subsequent analysis we conservatively assume that the compact region is a galaxy on its own.

gmw_00003895.name: host galaxy of iPTF15dld
gmw_00003895.overview: The host galaxy of iPTF15dld is a bright, compact starburst region [arXiv:1806.10613]. It remains unclear whether this compact region is an independent galaxy or part of a larger system [arXiv:1806.10613].

sources.summary: The paper discusses the iPTF15dld host as a bright compact starburst region that may be part of a larger system, adopting it as a galaxy for conservative analysis.

quotes.quote: iPTF15dld: The SN occurred at the position of a bright, compact starburst region (see Fig. fig1 ). It is unclear whether the region is a part of the whole system or not (see also Pian2017 ). We conduct the analysis for both scenarios (see Table tab2 ). For subsequent analysis we conservatively assume that the compact region is a galaxy on its own. In the alternative scenario the SN is found at a much larger normalized offset.

gmw_00003896.name: host galaxy of GRB 161219B
gmw_00003896.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 161219B has been imaged using both the Hubble Space Telescope and PanSTARRS [arXiv:1806.10613]. While the galaxy is much better resolved in the Hubble Space Telescope images, the PanSTARRS data is preferred for photometric analysis [arXiv:1806.10613].

sources.summary: The paper compares HST and PanSTARRS imaging of the GRB 161219B host, preferring PanSTARRS while noting HST resolves the host better and gives similar offsets.

quotes.quote: GRB 161219B: An image of the host has been taken with the HST/WFC3 camera using the F200LP filter Cano2017b . Because this filter covers much broader wavelength range compared to the other filters in this study, we prefer to use the PanSTARRS image for the analysis. We note that the host is much better resolved in the HST image. The analysis of the HST image results in very similar offsets and slightly larger sizes ( r_ 50 = 3.75 0.08 kpc) compared to the values measured from the PanSTARRS image.

gmw_00003897.overview: The high-redshift galaxy 1255-0, also known as MUSYC 1252-0, is a quiescent galaxy located at a redshift of z=2.186 [arXiv:1204.3099, arXiv:1410.5048]. It features an old, evolved stellar population with a compact structure and very little ongoing star formation [arXiv:1204.3099, arXiv:1410.5048].

sources.summary: MUSYC 1252-0 is presented as a z=2.186 evolved quiescent galaxy with high velocity dispersion, compact size, old stellar population, and little ongoing star formation.

quotes.quote: MUSYC 1252-0 report a = 510^ +165 _ 95 km s^ 1 derived from a deep Gemini/GNIRS spectrum of a z=2.186 galaxy selected from the MUSYC survey gawiser2006 . It has H=21.31 , K=21.03 , corresponding to a L=7L _K^* galaxy marchesini2007 . Based on surface brightness fits to HST/NIC2 F160W observations, they find r_e=0.78 0.17 kpc and n= 3.2 0.9 vandokkum2008 .

From these numbers, and assuming =5, we calculate M_ dyn =2.4^ +1.9 _ 0.8 10^ 11 M_ which is identical to the value quoted in . The shape of the GNIRS spectrum is consistent with old, evolved, quiescent stellar population, with little ongoing star formation (SFR 1-3 M_ yr^ 1 ) and age 1.3--2.0 Gyr, corresponding to a formation redshift of z_f 4-7 kriek2009 , quite different from the younger, post starburst spectra of the other three z 2 galaxies considered. This galaxy also has a significantly larger velocity dispersion and smaller size than the other three galaxies (see Figure fig.sdss ).

sources.summary: The paper treats 1255-0 as a quiescent high-redshift galaxy, fitting weak line ratios, low flux and luminosity, and its continuum SED with AGN and starburst models.

quotes.quote: 1255-0 . We constrained the models (Table 2) of this `quiescent' galaxy (Kriek et al 2009) by the relatively low [ OIII ] 5007+/ [ OII ] 3727+, [ OII ] / , and [ OIII ] +/ (Table 2, col. 5). The modelling results appear in Table 2, col. 6 and 7 (m _ AGN , m _ SB ). The relatively low calculated flux ( 3 _ mod ) partly explains the low observed flux (1.29 ^ +0.16 _ 0.18 10 ^ 17 ) and luminosity (0.46 ^ +0.06 _ 0.06 10 ^ 42 erg s ^ 1 ) confirming the low SFR observed by Kriek et al. In Fig. 2 (left diagram) we model the continuum SED presented by Kriek et al. (2009, fig. 2). The data are normalized to unity at 1 .

gmw_00003898.name: A773
gmw_00003898.overview: A773 is an active high-redshift galaxy that exhibits characteristics typical of starbursts [arXiv:1410.5048]. It features an unusually high starburst temperature of approximately 200,000 Kelvin, geometrically thick emitting clouds, and a possible active galactic nucleus contribution [arXiv:1410.5048].

sources.summary: The paper includes A773 among active high-redshift galaxies, finding an unusually high starburst temperature, possible AGN contribution, and geometrically thick emitting clouds.

quotes.quote: A1835, A773, and MACS0717 . The results for the galaxies from the Belli et al. sample are presented in Table 3. Although the observed line ratios are typical of SBs, we have searched for AGNs in each object. The results show that AGN could be present (Fig. 1). is relatively high, in particular for A773 (2 10 ^5 K). The emitting clouds are geometrically thick ( D 10 ^ 19 cm) which seems unusual for AGNs in this z range ( D 10 ^ 16 cm, Contini 2013, fig. 3).

The high starburst temperature ( 2 10 ^5 K) suggesting activity in the two galaxies CDFS-695 (van Dokkum et al. 2005) and A773 (Belli et al 2013) is not seen in the other galaxies of the modelled samples.

gmw_00003899.name: 1RXSJ171405.2-202747
gmw_00003899.overview: 1RXSJ171405.2-202747 is an active galactic nucleus located in a crowded region near the Galactic center [arXiv:1807.04299]. It is classified as a BL Lacertae object with a spectral energy distribution resembling an extreme BL Lac [arXiv:1807.04299].

sources.summary: The paper scrutinizes 1RXSJ171405.2-202747 because of field confusion near the Galactic center, identifies likely radio/X-ray/IR counterparts, and finds an extreme-BL-Lac-like SED.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: An X-ray source was observed and detected with Swift several times at celestial coordinates: = 17 14 05.4, =-20 ^ 27' 49'', with an error of 3'', coincident with the position of 1RXS J171405.2-202747 and of NVSS J171405-202748.

quotes.quote: The case of 1RXSJ171405.2-202747 The study of 1RXSJ171405.2-202747 needed of a careful analysis because of the position of the source. It is in fact very close to the galactic center and therefore in a region full of stars and other sources (see Fig. fig:position1RXS ). For this reason we check carefully every data related to this source to be sure the effective association with our source. Within the 3FGL gamma-ray catalog (Acero et al. 2015) the accuracy in the position of 3FGL J1714.1-2029 is 3.6 arcmin (95 .l.). 1RXS J171405.2-202747 is identified as its X-ray counterpart.

The concave X-ray spectrum from XRT, modelled with a log-parabolic fit (see section 3.5 for detail), suggests a peak around 1 keV. Such a large synchrotron peak frequency resemble a characteristic feature of the so-called extreme BL Lacs (e.g. Costamante et al. 2001, Bonnoli et al. 2015, Costamante et al. 2018). Besides a peak in the X-ray band, these peculiar sources display a quite hard gamma-ray continuum, often peaking in the TeV band. The optical band, instead, is dominated by the emission from the host galaxy. The data for 1RXS J1714-20 are consistent with both characteristics.

gmw_00003900.name: UGC 7899
gmw_00003900.overview: UGC 7899 is a galaxy that exhibits hints of outer radial flows and an outer disk warp [arXiv:2111.01806]. It also displays inner non-circular motions within the central 5 kiloparsecs, which are modeled as inner radial flows [arXiv:2111.01806].

sources.summary: The paper finds hints of outer radial flows, inner non-circular motions, and an outer warp in UGC 7899, making it the strongest late-accretion candidate in the HIghMass sample.

quotes.quote: We present H velocity maps for the HIghMass galaxies UGC 7899, UGC 8475, UGC 9037 and UGC 9334, obtained with the SITELLE Imaging Fourier Transform Spectrometer on the Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope, to search for kinematic signatures of late gas accretion to explain their large atomic gas reservoirs. The maps for UGC 7899, UGC 9037, and UGC 9334 are amenable to disc-wide radial flow searches with the DiskFit algorithm, and those for UGC 7899 and UGC 9037 are also amenable to inner-disk kinematic analyses. We find no evidence for outer disc radial flows down to V _r 20 \ in UGC 9037 and UGC 9334, but hints of such flows in UGC 7899. Conversely, we find clear signatures of inner ( 5 kpc) non-circularities in UGC 7899 and UGC 9037 that can be modelled as either bisymmetric (which could be produced by a bar) or radial flows. Comparing these models to the structure implied by photometric disc-bulge-bar decompositions, we favour inner radial flows in UGC 7899 and an inner bar in UGC 9037.

With hints of outer disc radial flows and an outer disc warp, UGC 7899 is the best candidate for late accretion among the galaxies examined, but additional modelling is required to disentangle potential degeneracies between these signatures in and H velocity maps.

gmw_00003901.name: UGC 8475
gmw_00003901.overview: UGC 8475 is an Scd-type spiral galaxy with a diameter of approximately 76 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1103.2910, arXiv:2111.01806]. Its spectrum displays weak emission lines of hydrogen, nitrogen, sulfur, and oxygen superimposed on a strong absorption-feature continuum [arXiv:1103.2910].

sources.summary: The paper observes UGC 8475 with SITELLE but finds Moon-affected, lower-sensitivity maps, requiring extra filtering and binning and preventing detailed non-circular-flow searches.

quotes.quote: From the 10 HIghMass galaxies that fit these criteria huang2014highmass , we were able to observe UGC 7899, UGC 8475, UGC 9037 and UGC 9334. SITELLE Observations UGC 7899 and UGC 9037 were observed during photometric conditions in queue observing mode with SITELLE on 2016 March 6, while UGC 8475 and UGC 9334 were observed on 2017 July 3rd and 2019 April 5th respectively. SITELLE is an Imaging Fourier Transform Spectrometer (IFTS), which produces spectra for each pixel in its field of view drissen2019sitelle . The observations were configured to detect with a resolution R 1500 in the SN3 filter. The corresponding number of = 0.45 nm moveable mirror steps and reference wavelength for each observation are given in Table table:observationdetails . The spectra contained the [ N II ] 6548 and 6583 doublet, in addition to the brighter line, which was fit along with to extract line-of-sight velocities. Because the N II doublet is weaker than the emission line, the effect on the fits is mild and we do not consider the N II maps in this analysis drissen2019sitelle . Table table:observationdetails also contains the Moon's illumination fraction f _ illum and its mean angular separation _ sep from the target during the observations. Distributed photon noise from the Moon limits the sensitivity of SITELLE drissen2014imaging , making dark sky conditions ideal. While this was the case for the UGC 7899, UGC 9037, and UGC 9334 runs, the Moon phase and position were more problematic for our run on UGC 8475. We compensated for the higher lunar photon noise by increasing the integration time per step on this target. Even so, the resulting data quality is lower for this galaxy (see Section subsec:finalmaps ).

We note that for the noisier UGC 8475 data, we additionally retained only velocity map pixels at locations where the H flux exceeded = 4.8 \ \ 10^ 17 \ . This is suboptimal, as a low-flux pixel can still give an accurate measure of gas velocity so long as the line peak is measured precisely. We did not bin pixels spatially for UGC 7899, UGC 9037, or UGC 9334, but used a bin size of 2 pixels for UGC 8475 to increase SNR at the cost of angular resolution.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 008475 as NGC 5162/NGC 5174, a 76 kpc Scd galaxy with emission lines and nearby small star-forming physical neighbors.

quotes.quote: 008475 (6835 km s ^ 1 ): This object is NGC 5162=NGC 5174=UGC 08475=CGCG 072-087=MCG +02-34-018, an Scd:, according to NED. The double NGC assignment is mentioned in UGC as `Listed in CGCG as ``double nebula'', which is incorrect. Star superimposed south center.' The SDSS isophotal major axis is 123 .16 at PA=165 ^ ; at a plate scale of 27.63 kpc arcmin ^ 1 this implies a 76 kpc wide galaxy. The SDSS spectrum shows weak H + [ NII ] , [ SII ] and [ OII ] emission lines over a strong, absorption-feature continuum.

gmw_00003902.name: UGC 9037
gmw_00003902.overview: UGC 9037 is a galaxy characterized by an axisymmetric outer disk with no evidence of outer radial flows [arXiv:2111.01806]. However, it exhibits inner non-circular motions within the central 5 kiloparsecs, which are best modeled as an inner bar that may efficiently transport gas inward [arXiv:2111.01806].

sources.summary: The paper finds no outer radial flows in UGC 9037 but detects inner non-circular motions best interpreted as an inner bar that may help move gas inward.

quotes.quote: We present H velocity maps for the HIghMass galaxies UGC 7899, UGC 8475, UGC 9037 and UGC 9334, obtained with the SITELLE Imaging Fourier Transform Spectrometer on the Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope, to search for kinematic signatures of late gas accretion to explain their large atomic gas reservoirs. The maps for UGC 7899, UGC 9037, and UGC 9334 are amenable to disc-wide radial flow searches with the DiskFit algorithm, and those for UGC 7899 and UGC 9037 are also amenable to inner-disk kinematic analyses. We find no evidence for outer disc radial flows down to V _r 20 \ in UGC 9037 and UGC 9334, but hints of such flows in UGC 7899. Conversely, we find clear signatures of inner ( 5 kpc) non-circularities in UGC 7899 and UGC 9037 that can be modelled as either bisymmetric (which could be produced by a bar) or radial flows. Comparing these models to the structure implied by photometric disc-bulge-bar decompositions, we favour inner radial flows in UGC 7899 and an inner bar in UGC 9037.

Our preferred kinematic model for UGC 9037 - that of an axisymmetric outer disc with an inner bar - may be consistent with this basic picture, since bars can efficiently shuttle gas into the galaxy interior oh2011bar .

gmw_00003903.name: UGC 9334
gmw_00003903.overview: UGC 9334 is a galaxy with a large atomic gas reservoir [arXiv:2111.01806]. Its inner disk exhibits coherent non-circular flows that likely stem from a bar aligned along its major axis, though no convincing outer disk radial flows are detected [arXiv:2111.01806].

sources.summary: The paper finds UGC 9334 suitable for disc-wide radial-flow searches, detects no convincing outer non-circular flows, and links its inner coherent flows to a major-axis bar-like feature.

quotes.quote: We present H velocity maps for the HIghMass galaxies UGC 7899, UGC 8475, UGC 9037 and UGC 9334, obtained with the SITELLE Imaging Fourier Transform Spectrometer on the Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope, to search for kinematic signatures of late gas accretion to explain their large atomic gas reservoirs. The maps for UGC 7899, UGC 9037, and UGC 9334 are amenable to disc-wide radial flow searches with the DiskFit algorithm, and those for UGC 7899 and UGC 9037 are also amenable to inner-disk kinematic analyses. We find no evidence for outer disc radial flows down to V _r 20 \ in UGC 9037 and UGC 9334, but hints of such flows in UGC 7899.

We therefore find no convincing signature of non-circular flows for 3 kpc for UGC 9334, with the models suggesting that < 20 on average across the disc. There is strong evidence that < 0 for 3 kpc in all of the models, which implies coherent non-circular flows in that region. We speculate that they stem from a bar aligned along the major axis of UGC 9334 willett2013galaxy that is visible in the bottom panel of Figure fig:quadimageSDSSDR14 .

gmw_00003904.name: 3CR 34
gmw_00003904.overview: 3CR 34 is an extended Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy located at the center of a compact galaxy cluster [arXiv:2302.01357, arXiv:1807.10774]. Optically classified as a high-excitation line radio galaxy, it features a curved western radio jet and a double hotspot in its western lobe [arXiv:1807.10774, arXiv:astro-ph/0109125].

sources.summary: The paper images 3C 34 as an FR-II radio galaxy in a compact cluster, detecting low-frequency lobe filaments, spectral flattening, banding, and hotspot structure tied to magnetic fields and jet behavior.

quotes.quote: 3C 34 is an extended FR-II radio galaxy with a 178-MHz luminosity of L_ 178 =2.7 10^ 27 W Hz^ 1 lain83 , at a redshift of z=0.69 spin85 . It lies at the centre of a rich compact cluster, with extended emission line gas from the host galaxy overlapping the radio lobes mcca95 . An [ O iii ] emission line image was reported where the emission is elongated along the jet axis, a commonly found phenomenon for ( z>0.6 ) powerful radio galaxies known as the alignment effect, suggested as describing jet-induced star-formation mcca87 .

For 3C 34 we find a spectral flattening at the location of the filament, suggesting that enhanced magnetic fields are responsible for its formation. The resulting particle populations, that may be re-energized, continue to be driven perpendicular to the jet axis to form low surface brightness tails that can mix with the ICM, and provides support to radio galaxy lobes providing the seed particles for the existence of diffuse cluster sources. We report the first case of large-scale periodic banding in total intensity and spectral index in the lobes of radio galaxies, evident in the eastern lobe of 3C 34.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3CR 34 as an FR II HERG in a compact galaxy cluster, with radio hotspots, jet-cloud interaction signatures, and a detected X-ray core.

quotes.quote: 3CR 34 . This is an FR II radio galaxy at z =0.69 and optically classified as a high-excitation line radio galaxy HERG; mullin06 . It lies near the center of a compact cluster of galaxies mccarthy95 appearing as one of the reddest members and being surrounded by fainter companions. There is a double hotspot in the western lobe. Best et al. (1997) reported the detection of a strong jet-cloud interaction at 120 kpc distance from the core and HST images also show a narrow region of blue emission orientated along the radio axis and directed towards a radio hotspot. In the \ observation analyzed here we only detected the X-ray core.

sources.summary: The paper uses 3C 34 as an example of a bent radio-galaxy jet whose small inferred curvature should avoid terminal and internal shock formation.

quotes.quote: In a previous paper mendoza00 , we discussed the possibility of a bent jet in the radio galaxy 3C 34 using the observations of best97a . According to these observations, the radio source lies more or less in the plane of the sky, and so, if the western radio jet is curved, this must be of the order of 10 . The value 10 is well below the upper limit of 50 calculated in eq. eq.4.41 , so that no terminal shock would be produced by the deflection of the jet. From the lower plot of Fig. fig.4 and because the angle _ 1 for a high relativistic flow, it follows that if the trajectory of the jet in 3C 34 is circular, then in order not to produce an internal shock at the onset of the curvature, the ratio D / R has to be less than 0.08 .

gmw_00003905.name: 3CR 41
gmw_00003905.overview: 3CR 41 is a high-redshift Fanaroff-Riley class II radio galaxy classified as a narrow-emission-line galaxy [arXiv:1801.01751, arXiv:1807.10774]. Its radio structure features two visible lobes and a southeastern jet pointing toward a hotspot, with both the core and the southern hotspot detected in X-rays [arXiv:1807.10774].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C41 as a high-redshift classical FR II source whose KDA EXT fit looks reliable, though statistical comparison with the KDA model is inconclusive.

quotes.quote: 3C41 Radio source 3C41 shows classic extended FR II-type morphology and lies at relatively high z . It is worth noting that its KDA EXT model fit, presented on Figure 3, seems very reliable, with a resulting ^ 2 value almost ten times less than the corresponding value for KDA model. The likelihood ratio test, however, suggests that there is no significant preference of the KDA EXT model over KDA, or at least the results of both models are statistically comparable, with slightly higher than the assumed significance level. The spectra calculated from both fitted models are also given in Table 4. Interestingly, in the case of 3C41, the flat low-frequency part of the KDA EXT radio spectrum visually fits the observed data well, regardless of the poor statistical result.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3CR 41 as an FR II narrow-emission-line radio galaxy whose radio lobes and southeastern jet/hotspot are visible, with both core and southern hotspot detected in X-rays.

quotes.quote: 3CR 41 . This is an FR II radio galaxy with z= 0.79, classified as a narrow emission line radio galaxy. In the radio image at 8.45 GHz there is no detection of a jet in the northern lobe while extended radio emission associated with the jet pointing towards the south-eastern hotspot is detected see e.g. mullin08 . Both radio lobes are also visible even at 8.45 GHz. At higher energies, in our \ snapshot observations, the southern hotspot is detected as well as the core of the radio galaxy .

gmw_00003906.name: 3CR 44
gmw_00003906.overview: 3CR 44 is a quasar at a redshift of z = 0.66 that is associated with an optical galaxy cluster [arXiv:1807.10774]. It exhibits a radio morphology similar to a Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy and contains a nuclear X-ray source [arXiv:1807.10774].

sources.summary: The paper treats 3CR 44 as a quasar with FR II-like radio morphology in an optical cluster, noting possible jet/dust-lane structure and clear nuclear X-ray detection.

quotes.quote: 3CR 44 . This quasar, with a radio morphology similar to that of an FR II radio galaxy , is at z =0.66 being associated with a galaxy cluster visible in the optical image odea09 . Kharb et al. (2008) reported a hint of a jet-like structure extending towards the southern hotspot in the 5GHz radio map while the HST image shows this radio galaxy to be either composed of two structures oriented north-south or, more likely, bisected by a dust lane running east-west mccarthy97 . We clearly detected the nuclear emission in the X-rays and we also found an excess of X-ray counts associated with the northern lobe but at < 3 significance.

gmw_00003907.name: 3CR 54
gmw_00003907.overview: 3CR 54 is a Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.8274 [arXiv:1807.10774]. It features close companion galaxies and an asymmetric lobe morphology, with its southern radio lobe being more extended than the northern one [arXiv:1807.10774].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3CR 54 as an FR II radio galaxy with asymmetric lobe morphology, nearby companions or possible tidal/jet structure, and X-ray detections of its core and southern hotspot.

quotes.quote: 3CR 54 . This is an FR II radio galaxy at redshift z =0.8274. The lobe morphology on the southern side of the source is more extended than the northern one kharb08 . In the HST image, the galaxy has close companions and the source appears extended towards the southwest side but it is unclear if it is a bridge, a tidal tail, or a jet feature mccarthy97 . In the X-ray image the radio core is clearly detected together with the southern hotspot.

gmw_00003908.name: 3CR 107
gmw_00003908.overview: 3CR 107 is a Fanaroff-Riley class II high-excitation radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.785 [arXiv:1807.10774]. It exhibits nuclear emission and extended X-ray emission spanning tens of kiloparsecs, which is associated with its southern radio lobe [arXiv:1807.10774].

sources.summary: The paper reports 3CR 107 as an FR II HERG at z=0.785 with detected nuclear emission and extended X-ray emission associated with its southern radio lobe.

quotes.quote: 3CR 107 . This FR II - HERG radio galaxy lies at z =0.785 mccarthy97 . In our \ snapshot observation we clearly detected the nuclear emission as well as X-ray extended emission associated with the southern lobe (see Fig. fig:3c107lobe and Table tab:features ), as also indicated by the low value of the ``ext ratio'' in Table tab:cores .

We also report the discovery of extended X-ray emission, on tens of kpc scale, around the radio nuclei of 3CR 107 and 3CR 293.1, for the former source due to the X-ray counterpart of the southern radio lobe .

gmw_00003909.name: 3CR 114
gmw_00003909.overview: 3CR 114 is a Fanaroff-Riley class II radio galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.815 [arXiv:1807.10774]. It is classified as a low-excitation radio galaxy with weak optical emission lines, a clumpy radio nucleus, a northern jet-like feature, and double hotspots [arXiv:1807.10774].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3CR 114 as an FR II LERG with weak optical lines, clumpy radio nucleus, northern jet-like feature, double hotspots, and a detected X-ray nucleus.

quotes.quote: 3CR 114 . This FR II radio galaxy is at z =0.815 showing fairly weak emission lines in its optical spectrum that led to a LERG classification strom90 . A faint compact nucleus with several clumps within the few arcseconds is observed at radio frequencies and a jet-like feature appears on the northern side in the 1.4GHz image kharb08 while single hotspots are detected both in the southern and in the northern lobes. Storm et al. (1990) claimed that this radio galaxy could lie in the core of a rich, distant cluster with some signatures of a merger on the basis of their optical observations. In the X-ray image the nucleus is detected but there is no evidence of hotspots and no signatures of X-ray emission from intracluster medium was found.

gmw_00003910.name: 3CR 154
gmw_00003910.overview: 3CR 154 is a nearby lobe-dominated quasar located at a redshift of z = 0.58 [arXiv:1807.10774]. It features a bright radio core that appears as a point-like source in optical images, surrounded by extended X-ray emission on a kiloparsec scale [arXiv:1807.10774].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3CR 154 as a lobe-dominated quasar with a bright X-ray core, spectral analysis, and significant extended X-ray emission around the radio nucleus.

quotes.quote: 3CR 154 . This is a nearby lobe-dominated quasar bogers94 at z =0.58 sokolovsky11 appearing as a point-like source in the HST optical image. In the \ snapshot observation only the relatively bright radio core is detected, for which X-ray spectral analysis was performed . As in 3CR 34 and 3CR 93, 3CR 154 shows extended X-ray emission on kpc scale detected above 5 level of confidence, measured adopting the same method previously described (see Fig. fig:clusters ) . We measured 243 X-ray photons in a annular region of radii 2 and 40 , centered on the location of the radio core, while 70.1 are expected in the background region. This X-ray extended emission has a luminosity L_X= (2.2 0.3) 10 ^ 44 erg/s, measured using an annular region of inner radius 2 and outer radius 40 , centered on the location of radio nucleus of 3CR 154.

gmw_00003911.name: 3CR 169.1
gmw_00003911.overview: 3CR 169.1 is a classical Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.633 [arXiv:1807.10774]. It is classified as a narrow-emission-line radio galaxy that hosts a detected X-ray nucleus, though it lacks radio jet or hotspot detections in 8 GHz radio maps [arXiv:1807.10774].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3CR 169.1 as a classical FR II narrow-emission-line radio galaxy with no radio jet or hotspot detection in the available map but with an X-ray nucleus.

quotes.quote: 3CR 169.1 . This classical FR II radio galaxy is at z =0.633. There is no detection of the jet and/or of the hotspots in the radio map at 8 GHz . Harvanek et al. (1998) classified this source as a narrow emission line radio galaxy according to its optical spectrum. In the X-ray snapshot image the nucleus is detected but there is no detection of the extended radio structure .

gmw_00003912.name: 3CR 217
gmw_00003912.overview: 3CR 217 is a high-redshift Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy with narrow emission lines [arXiv:1702.01939, arXiv:1807.10774]. It is a relatively young, small source featuring a strongly bent radio spectrum and an eastern and western lobe structure [arXiv:1702.01939, arXiv:1807.10774].

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C217 as a young, small high-redshift FR II radio galaxy with a bent radio spectrum better reproduced by the KDA EXT model.

quotes.quote: 3C217 This high-redshift radio galaxy was included in Sample 3 (3CRR FR II type sources with z>0.5 and D<400 kpc ) in Kuligowska et al. (2009). 3C217 is another example of a relatively young and small (D=94 kpc ) source with a strongly bent radio spectrum. The spectra resulting from the KDA and KDA EXT models and overlain the data points are shown in Figure 14.

Similarly to 3C184, the KDA EXT model spectrum is much more bent than that predicted by the KDA model. The ^ 2 _ red values at the bottom of Table 3 clearly indicate a significantly higher goodness of fit in the case of the KDA EXT solution.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3CR 217 as an FR II narrow-emission-line radio galaxy with an eastern/western lobe structure, a detected X-ray core, and undetected western hotspot.

quotes.quote: 3CR 217 . This is a FR II radio galaxy with narrow emission lines at z =0.898. In the radio image at 8 GHz there are no jet signatures, in either the eastern or the western lobe mullin08 and only the western lobe is detected. In the X-ray band the radio core is detected but the \ image is not registered because we were not able to locate precisely its position . Only a single photon is associated with the western hotspot being undetected in the X-rays.

gmw_00003913.name: 3CR 225B
gmw_00003913.overview: 3CR 225B is a Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.582 [arXiv:1807.10774]. Optically classified as a narrow-emission-line galaxy, it features hotspots within its radio lobes and shows a hint of diffuse X-ray emission near its nucleus [arXiv:1807.10774].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3CR 225B as an FR II narrow-emission-line radio galaxy with radio-lobe hotspots, possible diffuse nuclear X-ray emission, and no hotspot X-ray detections.

quotes.quote: 3CR 225B . This is a FR II radio galaxy at z =0.582, optically classified as a narrow emission line galaxy. It does show only hotspots within its radio lobes . In the X-ray there is a hint of diffuse emission near the nucleus but no detection for the two hotspots.

gmw_00003914.name: 3CR 237
gmw_00003914.overview: 3CR 237 is a compact steep-spectrum radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.877 [arXiv:1807.10774]. It exhibits a Fanaroff-Riley II radio morphology with a physical size under 15 kiloparsecs and possesses a clear X-ray counterpart [arXiv:1807.10774].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3CR 237 as the sample’s only compact steep-spectrum source, with FR II morphology, sub-15 kpc radio size, and a clear X-ray counterpart.

quotes.quote: 3CR 237 .This is the only compact steep spectrum (CSS) radio source in our sample. It shows an FR II radio morphology and is at z =0.877. The radio source size is less than 2 \ (i.e., less than 15 kpc at z= 0.877) and it has a clear X-ray counterpart.

gmw_00003915.name: 3CR 247
gmw_00003915.overview: 3CR 247 is a Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.75 [arXiv:1807.10774, arXiv:1801.01751]. Optically classified as a narrow-emission-line galaxy, it lies in a crowded field with a close companion and features elongated radio lobes with bright hotspots [arXiv:1807.10774, arXiv:1801.01751].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3CR 247 as an FR II narrow-emission-line radio galaxy in a crowded field, with a close companion, nearby galaxies, measured radio age, and core/northern-hotspot X-ray detections.

quotes.quote: 3CR 247 . This is an FR II radio galaxy at z =0.75, showing two hotspots on both the eastern and the western side , optically classified as a narrow emission line radio galaxy. The host galaxy associated with the radio source 3C247 lies in a very crowded field. In the HST optical image it appears as a symmetrical central galaxy with a close companion lying about 0.8 arcsec to the south. Two nearby galaxies also lie within the envelope of the infrared emission see e.g. best98,best00 .

Radio spectral ageing analysis of this source has yielded an age of 3-5 million years, corresponding to a hotspot advance velocity of about 0.1c liu92 . In the \ X-ray image the radio core and the northern hotspot are detected.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C247 as a high-redshift FR II radio galaxy with a weak-core parent galaxy, elongated lobes, hot spots, and only visual support for KDA EXT modeling.

quotes.quote: 3C247 3C247 is another example of a FR II-type radio galaxy at rather high redshift. It was previously studied by Laing et al. (1983) who suggested that its parent galaxy coincides with a weak radio core. Its Multi Element Radio Linked Interferometer Network (MERLIN) radio map (Chidi et al. 1994) shows rather thin and elongated lobes with bright hot spots. The spectra resulting from the predicted flux densities for KDA EXT and KDA models are presented in Figure 5, and the exact flux density values, along with the goodness of the fit to the data and the likelihood ratio analysis for the models, are listed in Table 6. Although visually the KDA EXT model predicts the observed spectra of 3C247 with better accuracy, the resulting value does not confirm a preference for this model over the KDA model.

gmw_00003916.name: 3CR 272
gmw_00003916.overview: 3CR 272 is a classical double Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy located at a redshift of 0.944 [arXiv:1807.10774]. It features a high-excitation radio galaxy optical spectrum and a relatively faint radio core compared to its two hotspots [arXiv:1807.10774].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3CR 272 as a classical double FR II radio source with a faint nucleus relative to its hotspots, HERG optical spectrum, and only core X-ray detection.

quotes.quote: 3CR 272 . A classical double FR II radio source at z =0.944, showing a relatively faint nuclear component with respect to the two hotspots at radio frequencies. Its optical spectrum shows a typical HERG spectrum with high ionization emission lines strom90 . There is nothing to report in the \ snapshot image other than the core detection.

gmw_00003917.name: 3CR 277
gmw_00003917.overview: 3CR 277 is a giant Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy classified optically as a low excitation radio galaxy [arXiv:1807.10774]. It features a radio core, a jet-like structure in its eastern lobe, and double hotspots in both lobes [arXiv:1807.10774].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3CR 277 as a giant FR II LERG whose radio core, jet-like eastern-lobe structure, double hotspots, offset nucleus, and X-ray core are reported.

quotes.quote: 3CR 277 . This is a giant FR II radio galaxy at z =0.414 strom90 , optically classified as a low excitation type radio galaxy (i.e., LERG). At 1.4 GHz the core and a jet-like structure are detected in the eastern lobe. Both radio lobes show double hotspots in high resolution radio images see e.g., harvanek98 . According to McCarthy et al. (1997) the source reveals a basic double morphology with the nuclear component slightly offset from the geometrical center. In the X-ray image the radio core is detected while while neither double hotspot has an X-ray counterpart .

gmw_00003918.name: 3CR 288.1
gmw_00003918.overview: 3CR 288.1 is a lobe-dominated quasar located at a redshift of z = 0.961 [arXiv:1807.10774]. It features a point-like optical counterpart, a clear X-ray nucleus with an absorbed power-law spectrum, and possible extended emission on a kiloparsec scale [arXiv:1807.10774].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3CR 288.1 as a lobe-dominated QSO with a point-like optical counterpart, clear X-ray nucleus, absorbed power-law spectrum, and possible kpc-scale extended emission.

quotes.quote: 3CR 288.1 . This is a lobe-dominated QSO lying at z =0.961 having a clear point-like optical counterpart gendre08 . In the X-ray image the nucleus is clearly detected and given the high number of photons with respect to the majority of the other targets, we also performed its spectral analysis finding its X-ray spectrum consistent with an absorbed power-law (see Table tab:spectra ). Extended emission on kpc scale seems to be present around the core (see Table tab:cores ) but no evidence of a galaxy cluster or a group of galaxies is present in the optical images.

gmw_00003919.name: 3CR 293.1
gmw_00003919.overview: 3CR 293.1 is a faint Fanaroff-Riley class II radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.709 [arXiv:1807.10774]. Its radio core and hotspots are weakly detected, and the galaxy exhibits extended X-ray emission spanning tens of kiloparsecs around its nucleus [arXiv:1807.10774].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3CR 293.1 as a faint FR II radio galaxy with weakly detected radio structures, a detected radio/X-ray core, and reported tens-of-kpc extended X-ray emission around its nucleus.

quotes.quote: 3CR 293.1 . This faint FR II radio galaxy is at z =0.709. Its radio structures , i.e, the nucleus and both hotspots, are barely detected in the radio image available to us at 4.9 GHz and the comparison with the X-ray image indicates that the radio core is detected with possible extended X-ray emission around it (see Table tab:cores ) .

We also report the discovery of extended X-ray emission, on tens of kpc scale, around the radio nuclei of 3CR 107 and 3CR 293.1, for the former source due to the X-ray counterpart of the southern radio lobe .

gmw_00003920.name: 3CR 323
gmw_00003920.overview: 3CR 323 is a Fanaroff-Riley class II radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.679 [arXiv:1807.10774]. It features irregular, structurally different outer radio lobes and exhibits extended X-ray emission surrounding its detected nucleus on scales of tens to hundreds of kiloparsecs [arXiv:1807.10774].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3CR 323 as an FR II radio galaxy with irregular, bent outer lobes, detected nucleus, and significant extended X-ray emission on tens-to-hundreds of kpc scales.

quotes.quote: 3CR 323 . This is a FR II radio galaxy at z =0.679. McCarthy et al. (1997) pointed out that the outer lobes are irregular and rather different in structure, the northern one undergoing a sharp bend to the east and ending in what may be a hotspot. In the X-ray we only detected the nucleus and there is also significant X-ray extended emission surrounding it both on scales of few tens and 300 kpc (above 5 significance), potentially due to its intergalactic medium (see Fig. fig:clusters ). There are 329 X-ray photons in a annular region of radii 2 and 75 , centered on the radio core position, while 269.3 background photons are expected in this region.

This does not affect the significance of the detection of the X-ray extended emission since only 8 and 20 photons were measured for the two nearby objects. We estimated an X-ray luminosity of L_X= (4.7 1.6) 10 ^ 43 erg/s for the extended emission surrounding 3CR 323, this was measured over an annular region of inner radius 2 and outer radius 21 , centered on the location of its radio core. A lower value of the outer radius for the annular region has been chosen to avoid contamination due to the presence of nearby foreground/background X-ray point-like sources.

gmw_00003921.name: UNCOVER 18407
gmw_00003921.overview: UNCOVER 18407 is a massive, quiescent galaxy at a spectroscopic redshift of 3.97 [arXiv:2402.05664]. The galaxy features a compact, dusty red core with a low star formation rate, surrounded by a less attenuated stellar envelope [arXiv:2402.05664].

sources.summary: The paper presents UNCOVER 18407 as a moderately lensed, massive quiescent galaxy at z=3.97 with a dusty red core, strong color gradients, compact structure, and early core formation.

quotes.quote: We report the spectroscopic confirmation of a massive ( =10.38 _ 0.09 ^ 0.08 ), HST-dark ( m_ F150W m_ F444W = 3.6 ) quiescent galaxy at z_ spec =3.97 in the UNCOVER survey. NIRSpec/PRISM spectroscopy surprisingly reveals that the galaxy core is consistent with a low ( 3 M_ \ yr^ 1 ) star formation rate despite evidence for moderate dust attenuation. The F444W image is well modeled with a two component fit that favors a compact, r_e 200 pc, n 2.9 component and a more extended, r_e 1.6 kpc, n 1.7 component. The galaxy exhibits strong color gradients: the inner regions are significantly redder than the outskirts.

Spectral energy distribution models that reproduce both the red colors and low star formation rate in the center of UNCOVER 18407 require both significant ( A_v 1.4 mag) dust attenuation and a stellar mass-weighted age of 1 Gyr, implying 50 of the stars in the core already formed by z=8 . Using spatially resolved annular mass-to-light measurements enabled by the galaxy's moderate magnification ( =2.12 _ 0.01 ^ 0.05 ) to reconstruct a radial mass profile from the best-fitting two-component model, we infer a total mass-weighted r_ eff = 0.74 _ 0.17 ^ 0.22 kpc and log ( _ 1 kpc \ [ M_ /kpc^2 ] ) = 9.65 _ 0.15 ^ 0.12 . The early formation of a dense, quiescent, and dusty core embedded in a less attenuated stellar envelope suggests an evolutionary link between the earliest-forming massive galaxies and their elliptical descendants.

gmw_00003922.name: PKS 0040 005
gmw_00003922.overview: PKS 0040 005 is a radio-loud, double-lobed FR II broad absorption line quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0608014, arXiv:1101.4928] located at a redshift of approximately 2.8 [arXiv:astro-ph/0608014, arXiv:1806.02016]. The system contains five narrow absorption line systems, some of which exhibit variability over multiple epochs [arXiv:1806.02016].

sources.summary: The paper classifies PKS 0040 005 as a radio-loud FR II BAL quasar and uses VLT spectropolarimetry to argue its BAL outflow is not edge-on.

quotes.quote: We have used the Very Large Telescope (VLT) to obtain spectropolarimetry of the radio-loud, double-lobed broad absorption line (BAL) quasar PKS 0040 005. We find that the optical continuum of PKS 0040 005 is intrinsically polarized at 0.7 with an electric vector position angle nearly parallel to that of the large-scale radio axis. This result is naturally explained in terms of an equatorial scattering region seen at a small inclination, building a strong case that the BAL outflow is not equatorial.

We classified SDSS 004323.43 001552.43 as an extended, radio-loud FR II BAL quasar. This quasar was previously detected by many radio surveys and is better known as PKS 0040 005. PKS 0040 005 ( z = 2.806 ) has also been recently classified as a FR II BAL quasar by Gregg et al. (2006) and Zhou et al. (2006). We targeted PKS 0040 005 for spectropolarimetry to test the geometry of BAL quasars.

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J0043-0015 as the only extended radio source in the sample, with double-lobed FR II structure and roughly perpendicular radio and polarization position angles.

quotes.quote: Only one of these radio sources shows extended structure, SDSS J0043-0015 (also known as PKS 0040-005). This object has a double lobed FR II structure, with the lobes at a position angle of 51 . Brotherton et al. (2006) discussed the radio and polarimetric properties of this object, however it has since been discovered that the polarization position angle presented in that paper is incorrect due to a reduction error causing the position angle to be rotated by 90 .

In the one extended source in this sample, SDSS J0043-0015, the radio position angle is 51 , while the polarization position angle is about 128 (both in white-light and in the continuum) The radio and polarization position angles are roughly perpendicular, which we would expect if the source is seen from an edge-on perspective.

sources.summary: This z=2.8200 quasar has five narrow absorption line systems; the paper reports variability in systems at 2507 and -1113 km/s over SDSS epochs.

quotes.quote: Quasar SDSS J004323.43-001552.4 with z_ em =2.8200 . It was spectroscopically observed by the SDSS on MJD = 55184 , 55186, 55444 and 57016. In the quasar spectra, we detect 5 \ NALs with z_ abs =2.4501 , 2.7882, 2.7993, 2.8145 and 2.8342, and _ =30 448 , 2507 , 1630 , 432 and 1113 \ from the quasar system. A system with _ =2507 \ was enhanced first by W_r^ 1548 =2.6 _ W_r^ 1548 from MJD=55184 to 55444, and then was weakened by W_r^ 1548 =-3.4 _ W_r^ 1548 from MJD=5444 to 57106. The other one system with _ =-1113 \ also showed a variation of W_r^ 1548 =-2.2 _ W_r^ 1548 from MJD=55184 to 57106, whose weakened timescale was longer than that of the system with _ =2507 .

gmw_00003923.name: SIG 2246
gmw_00003923.overview: SIG 2246 is an almost face-on spiral galaxy that is isolated from other systems [arXiv:1510.08179]. The galaxy's central region contains older stellar populations than its outskirts, indicating a radial star-formation history that supports inside-out disk growth [arXiv:1510.08179].

sources.summary: The pilot MaNGA analysis treats SIG 2246 as an isolated face-on spiral whose radial star-formation history supports inside-out disk growth.

quotes.quote: In particular, we show preliminary results for SIG 2246, an almost face-on spiral isolated galaxy that has been already observed with MaNGA (see left panel in Fig. fig:isol ). To study the radial distribution of the SFH we consider the stacked spectra in eight radial bins within 2 arcsec major axis elliptical annuli, as it is shown in the middle panel of Fig. fig:isol , over-plotted in the H _ map of the galaxy, which traces star formation.

SIG 2246 Following the methodology mentioned above, we constraint the SFH of the isolated galaxy SIG 2246. As it is shown in the right panel in Fig. fig:isol , we estimate the mean age of the average stellar population (blue points), in each radial bin considered, and the corresponding time-scale (error bars in the Y-axis). In average, the central part of the galaxy is composed by older stellar population ( 2 Gyr) than in the outskirts ( 5 Gyr younger), where the most recent episode of star formation is not younger than 12 Gyr. Also, the time-scale is gradually larger from 1 Gyr in the inner part to 3 Gyr in the outer regions of the galaxy.

gmw_00003924.name: Mrk 848
gmw_00003924.overview: Mrk 848 is a nearby major galaxy merger featuring two distinct nuclei and two well-defined tidal tails [arXiv:1510.08179, arXiv:1801.04860]. Classified as a luminous infrared galaxy, it hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1801.04328] and exhibits intense, interaction-triggered star formation within its core regions [arXiv:1801.04860, arXiv:2206.06797].

sources.summary: The paper studies Mrk 848 as a MaNGA-observed merger with two tails and two nuclei, finding older central stellar populations and interaction-enhanced central star formation.

quotes.quote: In order to test the methodology when considering galaxies in interaction, we selected Mrk 848, an observed galaxy merger listed in the MaNGA ancillary program on observing close pairs, which will be the largest IFU sample for pair/interacting galaxies in the nearby Universe. This galaxy merger shows two well defined tails (see the left panel in Fig. fig:merger ), one in the north and other in the south, where the two nuclei are well identified in the central part. As it is shown in the left panel of Fig. fig:merger , the MaNGA IFU do not cover the full object, we therefore divided the merger into 6 regions.

Mrk 848 We apply the same methodology to study the evolution of SIG 2246, to constraint the SFH in the galaxy merger Mrk 848. The left panel in Fig. fig:merger shows the estimated SFH in each considered region of the object. The yellow and green Gaussian distributions correspond to the SFH in the centre (regions 3 and 4). The resulted SFH for regions 2, 5, and 6 correspond to the red, blue, and magenta distributions. The stellar population in the central region is older than in the tails.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk848 as a nearby major merger with tidal tails, studied through UV-to-IR photometry and MaNGA IFU spectra to compare core and tail star-formation histories.

quotes.quote: Mrk848 is a major merger of two galaxies with stellar masses (M_ * /M_ )=10.44 and 10.30 yang2007 in the nearby universe ( z=0.041 ). Its morphologies, with obvious tidal tails, indicate strong interaction between the two galaxies. In Figure 1, we divide Mrk848 into six regions according to its tail and core features.

The plentiful data compiled both from the UV-to-IR multiwavelength photometry and from the MaNGA IFU spectroscopy enable us to study the galaxy merger Mrk848 in terms of the spatially resolved star formation properties. We divide the merger into six regions, among which two regions belong to the core area where the star formation is very intense and others belong to the tail area. In each region, we use the UV-to-IR SED and the IFU spectrum respectively to analyze the dust attenuation and the star formation of the merger.

By investigating the SFH of the core and tail regions in Mrk848, we find that both the spectral fitting and the SED fitting methods indicate very recent starburst ( 100 Myr) in core regions, especially in region 4 which has the most intense star formation. The tail regions also show certain sign of recent starburst, but the burst seems to have happened earlier ( 500 Myr) than in the core regions.

sources.summary: The software paper uses Mrk 848 as a real MaNGA demonstration, describing it as a major merger and showing PCA components that recover its core, tails, and star-forming regions.

quotes.quote: We showcase our approach by decomposing the IFU data of the galaxy merger Mrk 848 (MaNGA ID:12-193481). Mrk 848 is a major merger with strong interaction between the two galaxies with estimated stellar masses (M_ /M_ ) = 10.44 and 10.30 Yang2007 at z = 0.041. The MaNGA cube consists of an array of 74*74*4563 array.

fig:PCA_MaNGA shows the first four PCs, where we can see different aspects of the merger structure. The first PC correlates with the overall merger structure, including the core and tail regions. The second PC isolates the core region of the central galaxy, while the third and fourth PCs discriminate the star-forming regions triggered by the merging process Yuan2018 . The galactic structures illuminated by this simple decomposition are broadly consistent with the different aged stellar populations revealed by detailed spectral energy distribution fitting Yuan2018 , but further scrutiny of this object is beyond the scope of this work.

sources.summary: The paper describes VV 705 as a LIRG with a 25 percent modeled AGN contribution, corroborated by prior work, and red mid-infrared colors indicating an AGN.

quotes.quote: VV 705 : VV 705 is a LIRG. We measure an AGN contribution of 25 . This is corroborated by the results of dal14 , who measured an AGN luminosity fraction of 25 . The red 5.8--24 \ SED implies an AGN is present.

gmw_00003925.name: G1
gmw_00003925.overview: G1 is a low-redshift field galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.21441 [arXiv:1802.00026]. Spectroscopic observations of the galaxy reveal emission lines, indicating it is a low-luminosity system with a luminosity of 0.03 L_B^* [arXiv:1802.00026].

sources.summary: The paper identifies G1 as a low-redshift, low-luminosity field galaxy with emission-line spectroscopy and rules it out as relevant to the z ~0.93 absorber.

quotes.quote: Galaxy G1, at an impact parameter of 31 kpc, was spectroscopically identified in our Keck ESI spectrum, using the emission lines [ ] 5008, H and a sky-line blended [ ] 5685, shown in the right panel of Fig. fig:HSTimage . We used our own fitting program FITTER: see cwc-thesis to compute best fit Gaussian amplitudes, line centers, and widths, in order to obtain emission line redshift. We determined the redshift of G1 to be z=0.21441 0.00002 .

A weak, low ionization metal-line absorber is known to be at a redshift of z=0.21439 (Muzahid et al., in preparation), consistent with this lower redshift. At this redshift, galaxy G1 is found to have a luminosity of 0.03L_B^* . Galaxy G1 is clearly at a smaller redshift, and thus irrelevant to the present study.

gmw_00003926.name: G2
gmw_00003926.overview: G2 is a disk galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.9289 [arXiv:1802.00026]. It features a bright nucleus and possible ring structures that suggest a merger history, alongside spectral hints of active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:1802.00026].

sources.summary: The paper identifies G2 as the confirmed z = 0.9289 disk galaxy near the absorber, with merger-like structure and possible AGN activity.

quotes.quote: One of the four galaxies, G2, was confirmed to be at z =0.9289 0.0005 based on the [ ] 3727 emission line at 7190 2 \ in a KPNO 4-m CryoCam spectrum (D03), which corresponds to an impact parameter of 68 kpc . A higher S/N spectrum of this same galaxy was analyzed by T11, who confirmed that it is close to the redshift of the absorber, and suggested that it has properties consistent with a post-starburst galaxy. Though T11 did not quote a precise redshift value, inspection of their Fig. S2 gives a value consistent with the D03 value of z_ gal =0.9289 . An expanded view of galaxy G2 is shown in Fig. fig:HSTimage . The galaxy shows a bright nucleus and possibly two rings, suggesting that there has been a merger.

This galaxy shows signs that it has been influenced by a merger and has hints of AGN activity in its spectrum Tripp2011 . Another galaxy, G4, with a tenth the luminosity of G2 may also be at a similar redshift, but that cannot be confirmed without further observations.

gmw_00003927.name: G3
gmw_00003927.overview: G3 is a field galaxy that was tentatively detected in a Fabry-Perot image [arXiv:1802.00026]. Although initially interpreted as being at a redshift of approximately 0.93, it is more likely situated at a lower redshift to avoid implausibly large size and luminosity estimates [arXiv:1802.00026].

sources.summary: G3 is treated as a tentative Fabry-Perot detection whose z ~0.93 interpretation would imply implausible size and luminosity, so a lower redshift is favored.

quotes.quote: Galaxy G3 was tentatively detected in a Fabry-Perot image tuned to [ ] 3727 at z 0.93 that was published in , as mentioned by D03. At that redshift the impact parameter of G3 would be 74 kpc , similar to that of G2. However, at z=0.93 G3 would be a L_B = 0.44L* galaxy with a half-light radius of 5.5 kpc . This is implausibly large, thus G3 is more likely to be at a smaller redshift, despite the positive suggestion based on the Fabry-Perot image.

gmw_00003928.name: Indus 1
gmw_00003928.overview: Indus 1 is a dwarf satellite galaxy located in the Local Group [arXiv:2007.05011]. Recent astronomical analyses have successfully derived the first systemic proper motion measurements for this stellar system [arXiv:2007.05011].

sources.summary: The Gaia DR2 analysis derives the first systemic proper motion for Indus 1 and treats its satellite membership signal as one of the robust new detections.

quotes.quote: In addition to the 49 satellites shown in Figure literature which have previous estimates from the literature, there are an additional 5 satellites for which we derive estimates of their systemic proper motions for the first time, namely Indus 1, DES J0225+0304, Leo T, Cetus 2 and Pictor 2. The latter three are less robust than for the other systems.
For the satellites in the top row of Figure fsat , it is clear that the most likely value for f_ sat is greater than zero (the peak of the PDF is resolved). This is especially clear for Indus 1.
We conclude from this analysis that the measurements for Indus 1 and DES J0255+0304 are robust.

gmw_00003929.name: DES J0225+0304
gmw_00003929.overview: DES J0225+0304 is an old, metal-poor stellar system located at a heliocentric distance of approximately 25 kpc [arXiv:1608.04033]. Its physical size, luminosity, and ellipticity make it a candidate dwarf galaxy that is potentially associated with the Sagittarius dwarf stream [arXiv:1608.04033].

sources.summary: The paper reports DES J0225 + 0304 as an old, metal-poor stellar system at about 25 kpc whose size, luminosity, ellipticity, and Sagittarius-stream association make it a dwarf-galaxy candidate.

quotes.quote: The objects, DES J0111 1341 and DES J0225 + 0304, are located at a heliocentric distance of 25 kpc and appear to have old and metal-poor populations. Their distances to the Sagittarius orbital plane, 1.73 kpc (DES J0111 1341) and 0.50 kpc (DES J0225 + 0304), indicate that they are possibly associated with the Sagittarius dwarf stream. The half-light radius ( r_ h 4.55 pc ) and luminosity ( M_V +0.3 ) of DES J0111 1341 are consistent with it being an ultrafaint stellar cluster, while the half-light radius ( r_ h 18.55 pc ) and luminosity ( M_V 1.1 ) of DES J0225 + 0304 place it in an ambiguous region of size--luminosity space between stellar clusters and dwarf galaxies.

DES J0225 + 0304 is elongated ( 0.61 ) and has a physical size similar to an extended GC or a very small faint dwarf galaxy. In fact, the physical size, luminosity and ellipticity of DES J0225 + 0304 are comparable to the properties of the Tucana V stellar system ( r_ h 17 pc , M_V 1.6 and 0.7 ; Drlica2015 ).

If all of our hypotheses are confirmed, DES J0225 + 0304 would be the first ultrafaint dwarf galaxy associated with the Sagittarius dwarf stream. It would also be the first confirmed case of an ultrafaint satellite of a satellite.

sources.summary: The paper derives a first systemic proper motion for DES J0225+0304, uses it as a likelihood example, and concludes the measurement is robust.

quotes.quote: We derive systemic proper motions for the first time for Indus 1, DES J0225+0304, Cetus 2, Pictor 2 and Leo T, but note that the latter three rely on photometry that is of poorer quality than for the rest of the sample. We cannot resolve a signal for Bootes 4, Cetus 3, Indus 2, Pegasus 3, or Virgo 1.

An example of the resulting likelihood function is shown in the left panel of Figure spatial for DES J0225+0304. For comparison, the right panel shows what the spatial likelihood function would look like if the uncertainties had not been considered. DES J0225+0304 has relatively poorly defined structural parameters; for systems with well measured stuctural parameters, the distinction between the two panels becomes negligible.
The left panel of Figure cmdlikelihood shows an example of the resulting satellite likelihood model for color-magnitude space for the case of DES J0225+0304.

gmw_00003930.name: Pictor 2
gmw_00003930.overview: Pictor 2 is a dwarf galaxy located within the Local Group [arXiv:2007.05011]. Systemic proper motion measurements for this satellite system have been estimated using Gaia data, though these measurements remain tentative due to photometric uncertainties [arXiv:2007.05011].

sources.summary: The paper reports a first, especially cautious systemic proper motion for Pictor 2, noting that looser Gaia cuts favor a nonzero satellite fraction.

quotes.quote: The estimates for Cetus 2, Pictor 2 and Leo T are derived using looser cuts on the Gaia data in order to identify more member stars, and these systems are also discussed in more detail in Section atthelimits .
For Pictor 2, the situation is more ambiguous, but even here it appears that the favored model has f_ sat > 0 .

We present the measurements for Leo T, Cetus 2 and Pictor 2 using the looser cuts on the data alongside the other measurements in Tables pmresults and preferred . We stress that the proper motions for Leo T, Cetus 2 and Pictor 2 are based in part on photometry which may not be reliable. Any analyses that use these three measurements should proceed with caution, and we expect that future data releases from Gaia - potentially including the imminent EDR3 - may contain sufficently improved photometry to improve these three measurements.

gmw_00003931.name: NGC 1375
gmw_00003931.overview: NGC 1375 is a lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1906.08187] featuring a boxy or peanut-shaped bulge [arXiv:1105.4809, arXiv:1906.08187] and a distinct bar-like component [arXiv:astro-ph/0607434]. Its nuclear star cluster contains ionized gas [arXiv:2104.06412] and a young stellar population [arXiv:0710.5529, arXiv:2104.06412] that indicates ongoing or recent in-situ star formation [arXiv:2104.06412].

sources.summary: The paper groups FCC 148 with galaxies showing young central populations, linking young NSC stars and, for some systems, ionised gas to ongoing or recent in-situ nuclear growth.

quotes.quote: Galaxies with young central populations FCC 119 and FCC 148 are the only two sample galaxies that show the presence of ionised gas in their NSC as traced by the presence of H , H , [ NII ] , [ SII ] and [ OIII ] lines in the MUSE spectra (see also the emission line maps in Iodice2019 ). Our analysis uncovered young stellar populations ( < 3 Gyr) in both NSCs, while their host stellar bodies are older. The same was found for VCC 2019 although without the clear presence of ionised gas.

This ongoing or recent star formation is a clear sign of in-situ star formation building up the NSCs. However, FCC 119 also shows an older, more metal-poor component in its NSC and is on average more metal-poor than the host galaxy. This drop in metallicity could indicate additional accretion of metal-poor GCs from the galaxy outskirts to the centre.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 1375 as an S0 Fornax galaxy with a comparatively young central population and a substantial young stellar mass fraction.

quotes.quote: When compared to simple stellar population models, three galaxies (NGC 1316, NGC 1344 and NGC 1375) appear to have a significant contribution from young stars resulting in luminosity weighted ages between 1 and 3 Gyrs (see Figure fig:fornax_pops ).
NGC 1375 - Kuntschner (2000) concluded that the mean age of the central population of this S0 galaxy is significantly lower than for the other galaxies in our sample.

One component was old (11 Gyr) with metallicity set to [ Z/H ] = 0.35 for NGC 1316 and NGC 1344 and 0.0 for NGC 1375. The other component had the same metallicity but a young (1 Gyr) age. The relative mass fractions of these components were varied until the model strength matched the observed strength. The resultant young mass fractions (where total mass = 1) were 0.135, 0.075, and 0.140 for NGC 1316, NGC 1344, and NGC 1375, respectively.

sources.summary: NGC 1375 is described as a flat, rotating S0 with discy isophotes, a peanut bulge, core emission, and central stellar-population changes.

quotes.quote: NGC 1375 = FCC 148 This flat S0 galaxy ( 0.56 ) has discy isophotes, strong rotation ( V_ max 80 ) and V_ max / > 1 . On the ACS images a clear peanut bulge is seen. A weak nebular emission is detected in the core. In the central 7 arcsec ( 17 arcsec) the stellar population changes significantly. The metallicity decreases from 0.2 to -0.2 dex and the age rises from 1 Gyr to 2.5 Gyr.

sources.summary: FCC 148 is described as a boxy-bulge lenticular recent infaller near FCC 147, with disc-like rotation and a peaked central stellar velocity dispersion.

quotes.quote: FCC 148 (NGC 1375) -- Fig. fig:FCC148map FCC 148 is a lenticular galaxy with a prominent boxy bulge located on the western side of the cluster at R_ proj =0.7^ from the centre. It is close in projection to FCC 147 (NGC 1374), but there are no signs of interaction between the two objects. This is consistent with FCC 148 being a recent infaller. The stellar velocity map resembles that of a typical disc with a maximum value of v_ max 100 km s ^ 1 at R 50 arcsec. The stellar velocity dispersion is peaked in the centre ( 80 km s ^ 1 ) with a steep decrease outwards ( 20 km s ^ 1 at R 30 arcsec).

sources.summary: The paper reports NGC 1375 rotation consistent with prior data, lower reliable dispersion than older measurements, and small-radius h_3 and h_4 structure suggesting a distinct bar-like component.

quotes.quote: NGC 1375 Once again, Figure fig:comp2 shows that the measured rotational motion for this galaxy agrees well with previous data, while the disagreement with earlier velocity dispersion data seems to arise from the limited spectral resolution of the older data. Support for this interpretation of the inconsistency comes from Kuntschner's (2000) value for the central dispersion of 56 10 km s ^ 1 , in agreement with the new data. A further issue with the earlier analysis is revealed by Figure fig:1375dat , as the structure in h_3 and h_4 at small radii would indicate that there is more information in these dynamics than would be revealed by the earlier Gaussian fit. Once again, this structure indicates the presence of a distinct kinematic component such as a bar. Photometric support for this conclusion comes from the work by Lorenz et al. (1993), who found that NGC 1375 possesses a complex structure characterised by boxy isophotes within a radius of 15 arcsec ; such boxy isophotes are often associated with the kinematic signature of an edge-on bar (Kuijken Merrifield 1995).

sources.summary: The paper flags FDS16DWARF000/FCC148 as a dwarf with mixed early- and late-type traits, including a reddish outer spheroid, bluish embedded disk, and central star formation.

quotes.quote: The two galaxies with differing classifications (FDS22DWARF244/FCC46 and FDS16DWARF000/FCC148) are both dominated by reddish spheroid component in their outer parts, but have embedded bluish disks. Their colors within the effective radii are also clearly bluer than the ones of the red sequence galaxies. These galaxies clearly have properties of both late-type and early-type galaxies. Since they have signs of recent or ongoing star formation in their central parts we classify them as late types, consistently with BCDs.

sources.summary: The paper finds FCC 148 has low rotation inside its NSC radius, stronger velocity aligned with flattened isophotes outside it, evidence for a nuclear disk, and a central dispersion peak.

quotes.quote: FCC 148 shows a low level of rotation within the NSC's effective radius. The maximum velocity of 35 is reached outside of the NSC and overlaps with flattened isophotes, which is indicative of the presence of a nuclear disk. Indeed, turner12 showed that the best fit to this galaxy's light is obtained when a second large-scale disk component is added to the bulge and nucleus Sersi models. The velocity dispersion map has a peak of 70 in the centre, which is less prominent than the peak in the nucleus of FCC 47.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 1375 to demonstrate spectroscopic bulge-disc decomposition, deriving clean component spectra and inferring a young metal-rich bulge versus an old metal-poor disk.

quotes.quote: For example, Fig. spectra shows the spectra derived in this way for NGC 1375. The high signal-to-noise ratio of the spectra obtained using this integrated light approach is immediately apparent, such that one can see that both the H line and the magnesium triplet appear stronger in the bulge than in the disc; since these features are used as age and metallicity indicators respectively, this difference already hints that the bulge contains younger stars with a higher metallicity than the disc in this galaxy, and we will make a more quantitative assessment of this impression in Section sec:ssp . Application of this technique to the full sample of galaxies described in Section sec:data revealed the kind of data required to implement this analysis successfully. In fact, of the nine galaxies, only two (NGC 1381 and NGC 1375) could be reliably decomposed into bulge and disc spectra in this manner.

gmw_00003932.name: NGC 1380A
gmw_00003932.overview: NGC 1380A is an edge-on lenticular galaxy located in the Fornax cluster [arXiv:1904.01260, arXiv:1906.08187]. It features a simple rotational structure [arXiv:astro-ph/0607434], a small bright bulge [arXiv:1906.08187], and a nuclear star cluster built up partly by in-situ star formation [arXiv:2104.06412].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes FCC 177 as an edge-on Fornax S0 with old thick disks, younger nuclear stars, and extended thin-disk formation history.

quotes.quote: We used deep MUSE observations to perform a stellar-kinematic and population analysis of FCC 153 and FCC 177, two edge-on S0 galaxies in the Fornax cluster. The geometrical definition of the different structural components of these two galaxies allows us to describe the nature of their thick disks. These are both old, relatively metal poor and [ Mg/Fe ] enhanced, and their star formation history (SFH) reveals a minor younger component whose chemical properties suggest its later accretion. Moreover, the outer regions of these geometrically defined thick disks show higher values of metallicity and lower values of [ Mg/Fe ] . These stars probably formed in the thin-disk region and they were dynamically heated to form the flares present in these two galaxies.

We have measured ages down to 4 Gyr in FCC 153, and down to 2 Gyr in FCC 177 in their nuclear regions (hereafter "nuclei"), which include not only the pure stellar populations of the NSC but all components observed in the line of sight. On the other hand, their thin disks appear approximately coeval with one another ( 4-5 Gyr old in their youngest regions).

sources.summary: The paper identifies FCC 177 as a lower-mass edge-on S0 whose NSC is older than the immediate surroundings, highly metal-rich, and likely built substantially by central star formation.

quotes.quote: Lower-mass edge-on S0 galaxies FCC 153 and FCC 177 are edge-on S0 galaxies like FCC 170, but at significantly lower galaxy mass (see e.g. Pinna2019b, Poci2021 for a detailed analysis using the F3D MUSE data). and the Voronoi binned radial profiles presented in this work generally find young ages toward the galaxy centres. However, our background-subtracted NSC spectra find the NSCs to be older than their immediate surrounding although at a similar high metallicity, but with a minor young stellar population.

The NSCs are more metal-rich than any GCs found in the galaxies. The high NSC metallicity and the presence of young stars indicate a large contribution from in-situ star formation to their build-up. This star formation might have happened directly in the NSCs or in clustered star formation in the central regions of these galaxies.

sources.summary: NGC 1380A is an S0 with NGC 1380-like metallicity behavior, measured rotation and dispersion, a slightly younger center, and no strong central magnesium over-abundance.

quotes.quote: NGC 1380A = FCC 177 This S0 shows the same metallicity behaviour as NGC 1380. We find V_ max 90 \ and _0 = 45 . The age is slightly younger (by 1 Gyr) in the centre than in the outskirts. We do not detect central any strong Mg over-abundance.

sources.summary: FCC 177 is described as an edge-on lenticular ancient infaller with a small bright bulge plus old metal-rich thin disc and younger metal-poor thick disc.

quotes.quote: FCC 177 (NGC 1380A) -- Fig. fig:FCC177map FCC 177 is an edge-on lenticular galaxy located in the high-density region on the northern side of the cluster at R_ proj =0.8^ . It is an ancient infaller in the north-south clump of galaxies. It is characterised by a small and bright bulge, an extended metal-rich and old thin disc, and a metal-poor and younger thick disc ( Iodice2018 , Pinna2019a ). The detailed analysis of the kinematics and properties of the stellar component of FCC 177 from F3D data is presented by Pinna2019a .

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 1380A has featureless, simple rotational structure, unlike earlier strange-profile measurements, with new dispersion values below older data because of spectral-resolution limitations.

quotes.quote: NGC 1380A The well-constrained measurements of rotational motion for this galaxy are fairly featureless, and, as Figure fig:comp2 shows, do not reproduce the rather strange profile found by D'Onofrio et al. \ (1995). The similar absence of peculiarities in the h_3 and h_4 profiles shown in Figure fig:1380Adat suggest that the velocity structure in this galaxy is quite simple, so the smooth mean velocity curve is not unexpected. Once again, the new measurements of velocity dispersion lie systematically well below those in D'Onofrio et al. \ (1995), presumably due to the spectral resolution of the latter; Kuntschner's (2000) published central dispersion of 55 9 km s ^ 1 fits rather better with the new measurements, but may also be running into resolution issues.

gmw_00003933.name: ESO 359-G002
