In this work, we have applied a KDE with a first order boundary correction and a bandwidth selected according to Scott's factor Scott92 . . The posterior for _ los indicates the data favors more line of sight structure, but the preference is not statistically significant. The parameters _ los and _ sub are anti-correlated, as one would expect as one can, to a certain degree, remove lens plane subhalos and replace them with line of sight halos while keeping the total amount of flux perturbation constant. This is not a perfect degeneracy, however, since lensing efficiency and the relative number of subhalos and line of sight halos changes with redshift. Thus, a larger sample of quads at different redshifts could break the covariance between _ sub and _ los . Constraints on the subhalo mass function assuming CDM We perform a suite of CDM simulations using the same priors listed in Table tab:lenspriors , minus the WDM parameter m_ hm , with the aim of inferring _ sub . We marginalize over _ los , and over a theoretical-motivated prior on (between -1.95 and -1.85) based on predictions from N-body simulations Springel++08,Fiacconi++16 . The inference on _ sub is shown in Figure fig:cdmsigmasub . We infer _ sub = 0.055 kpc^ 2 , with a 1 confidence interval 0.029 < _ sub < 0.083 \ kpc^ 2 . At the 2 level we obtain _ sub > 0.008 kpc^ 2 . We do not quote an upper 2 bound on _ sub as it is prior dominated. To put these numbers in physical units, the mean value of _ sub corresponds to a mean projected mass in substructure for the lenses in our sample between 10^6 - 10^ 9 of 4.0 10^ 7 kpc^ 2 , and the 1 confidence interval corresponds to 2.0 - 6.1 10^ 7 kpc^ 2 . At 2 , the projected mass constraint is _ sub > 0.6 10^ 7 kpc^ 2 . To convert into the average projected mass, we have computed the average of the projected masses for each of the eight lenses in our sample, using the scaling of the halo mass function with redshift in Equation eqn:scaling while assuming a halo mass of 10^ 13 . In this section we review the main results of this work and discuss the implications for cold and warm dark matter. In Section ssec:mainsummary we summarize our main results, and in Section ssec:comparison we compare our results with those obtained in previous works. In Section ssec:systematics we discuss the sources of systematic uncertainty in our analysis, and we conclude in Section ssec:implications by discussing the implications of our result for cold and warm dark matter. Summary of the analysis and main results We have carried out a measurement of the free-streaming length of dark matter and the subhalo mass function using a sample of eight quadruply-imaged quasars. The methodology we use to constrain the dark matter parameters of interest has been tested and verified with simulated data Gilman++19 . Lenses that show evidence for morphological complexity in the form of stellar disks are excluded from our analysis. We model halos both in the main deflector and along the line of sight, including correlated structure around the main deflector through the two-halo term, and account for evolution of the projected subhalo mass function with redshift and halo mass using a suite of simulations using the semi-analytic modeling code galacticus . We compute image flux ratios by ray-tracing to finite-size background sources, which correctly accounts for the sensitivity of image flux ratios to perturbing halos. We also marginalize over the macromodel parameters for each system, including the power-law slope of the main deflector, and simultaneously constrain the lens macromodel and dark matter hyper-parameters to account for covariance between these quantities. In addition to the turnover in the halo mass function, we model WDM free-streaming effects on the mass-concentration relation, accounting for the effect of reduced central densities of WDM halos on lensing observables. The main results of this analysis are summarized as follows: We constrain the half-mode mass (thermal relic dark matter particle mass) to < 10^ 7.8 ( m_ DM > 5.2 keV ) at 2 . Since the confidence intervals depend on the prior used for both and _ sub , we also quote likelihood ratios relative to the peak of the posterior distribution for : we disfavor = 10^ 8.2 ( m_ DM = 4 keV ) with a likelihood ratio of 7:1, and with = 10^ 8.6 ( m_ DM = 3.0 keV ) the relative likelihood is 30:1. These bounds are marginalized over the amplitude of the subhalo mass function, the amplitude of the line of sight halo mass function, the power-law slope of the subhalo mass function, the parent halo mass, the background source size, and the parameters describing the main deflector mass profile. Assuming cold dark matter, we infer a value of the global amplitude of the subhalo mass function _ sub = 0.055_ 0.027 ^ 0.032 kpc^ 2 at 1 , and _ sub > 0.008 kpc^ 2 at 2 . In our lens sample, these values correspond to an average projected mass density in substructure between 10^6 - 10^ 9 of 4.0_ 2.0 ^ +2.1 10^ 7 kpc^ 2 and a lower bound of 0.6 10^ 7 kpc^ 2 , respectively. At fixed redshifts, for a 10^ 13 halo at z=0.2 ( z=0.6 ) the 1 constraint corresponds to a projected mass in substructure of 1.9_ 0.9 ^ +0.9 10^7 kpc^ 2 ( 4.1_ 2.0 ^ +2.0 10^7 kpc^ 2 ) in the subhalo mass range 10^6-10^ 9 . The 2 constraint corresponds to a projected mass in substructure of greater than 0.3 10^7 kpc^ 2 ( 0.6 10^7 kpc^ 2 ) in the same mass range. Discussion and comparison with previous work Constraints on dark matter warmth and the amplitude of the CDM subhalo mass function The first comprehensive analysis of multiply-imaged quasars was carried out by D+K02 (hereafter DK2), who inferred a projected mass fraction in substructure Throughout this section, we will use to refer to the average mass fraction in substructure inferred from a sample of multiple lenses in halos of different masses at different redshifts, and to refer to the mass fraction in substructure implied by a certain _ sub value at a specific redshift and halo mass. between 0.006 < < 0.07 at 2 modeling only lens-plane substructure, and assuming CDM. Recently, Hsueh++19 (hereafter H19) improved on the analysis of DK2 by including the effects of line of sight halos, measuring 0.006 < < 0.018 at 1 with a mean of 0.011 assuming CDM, and also constrained the free-streaming length of dark matter to < 10^ 8.4 ( m_ DM > 3.8 keV ). The 2 bound from H19 of < 10^ 8.4 is weaker than the constraint from this work < 10^ 7.8 . One possible reason for this difference is that unlike previous work Birrer++17a,Gilman++18,Gilman++19 H19 did not model the suppression of the mass-concentration relation in warm dark matter scenarios, which suppresses the lensing signal more than one order of magnitude above the position of the turnover in the mass function. This is of particular relevance for flux ratio studies because the effect of a perturbing dark matter halo depends on its central density profile. Free-streaming effects on the mass-concentration relation therefore increase the relative differences between CDM and WDM on the scales relevant for substructure lensing, which leads to greater constraining power over WDM models. Finally, we note that in a future analysis modeling the tidal evolution of substructures from the time of infall to the time of lensing may introduce additional constraining power over WDM models by coupling the structural parameters of subhalos at the time of lensing to their structural properties, such as concentration, at the time of infall. To facilitate direct comparison between this analysis and that of DK2 and H19 regarding the constraints on the subhalo mass function assuming CDM, we convert our _ sub values into estimates of by computing the projected mass density , and then using the fact that _ crit = 0.5 near the Einstein radius, where _ crit is the critical surface mass density for lensing. In these conversions, we also assume a halo mass of 10^ 13 , and take care to compute using the same mass range 10^6 -10^9 used by H19. Our 2 bounds on _ sub correspond to an average mass fraction in substructure > 0.005 with a mean of = 0.035 . At 1 0.018 < < 0.056 . This result is statistically consistent with the constraints from H19, and also with those of DK2. There are several key differences between our analysis and those of H19 and DK2 that pull in opposite directions in terms of constraining power over dark matter models. As mentioned previously we model free-streaming effects on the mass-concentration relation, and include the contribution from the two-halo term to account for correlated structure near the main deflector. These pieces of additional physics add information and increase our constraining power over WDM models. On the other hand, accounting for finite-size background sources decreases the expected magnification signal caused by dark matter halos and subhalos, and we expect to infer a higher normalization of the subhalo mass function in our analysis as more substructure is needed to produce the same degree of flux perturbation. Explicitly, by ray-tracing to finite-size background sources we find that the peak of the magnification cross section for a 5 10^7 halo is reduced by a factor of two for a 15 pc background source relative to a 5 pc background source, and by a factor of three for a 40 pc source. The simplifying assumption of point-sources for the background quasar invoked by H19 and DK2 introduces signal from low-mass halos whose effects would otherwise be washed out by an extended source. The tidal truncation of lens plane subhalos that we model may also reduce the overall impact of subhalos on lensing observables. We also marginalize over the power-law slope of the main deflector and simultaneously sample the macromodel parameters and the dark matter hyper-parameters. These processes introduce additional covariances in the posterior distributions, and should lead to weaker constraints on _ sub and . Other lensing studies, primarily those using the technique of gravitational imaging, have also sought to measure the subhalo mass function. Veg++14 inferred = 0.0064_ 0.0042 ^ 0.0080 at 1 in the mass range 4 10^6 - 4 10^9 assuming a prior on the slope of the subhalo mass function centered on = -1.9 , while Hezaveh++16 constrained the normalization of subhalo mass function assuming = -1.9 , inferring f _ fsub values comparable to the median f _ sub = 0.02 result from DK2 (and our constraint), but with larger uncertainties. To compare with the analysis of Veg++14 , we assume a halo mass of 10^ 13 at a lens redshift z_d = 0.25 and a source at z_ src = 0.7 , characteristic values for the lens sample analyzed by Veg++14 . Using these values with our expression for the subhalo mass function in Equation eqn:subhalomfunc , we obtain = 0.014_ 0.007 ^ +0.008 between 4 10^6 and 6 10^9 at 1 , in the same mass range used by Veg++14 . This result is consistent with that of Veg++14 Although Veg++14 did not model line of sight halos, the low lens/source redshifts their sample lessen the impact of line of sight halos on the inferred subhalo mass fraction such that we may compare our results, which include line of sight halos, with theirs. . We quote constraints on f_ sub to make comparisons with previous work, but we caution that the conclusions derived from inferences of f_ sub should be interpreted with care. The physical meaning of this parameter depends on specific assumptions regarding the subhalo mass range and the contribution from dark substructure to the convergence near the Einstein radius, which may change with halo mass and redshift. Comparing our results with semi-analytic simulations of massive 10^ 13 hosts, our results in terms of the projected mass in substructure is consistent with the galacticus simulations used to calibrate the evolution of the subhalo mass function with halo mass and redshift. We stress that our model was not tuned to match the normalization predicted by galacticus , it only made use of the trends of projected substructure mass density with host halo mass and redshift. Our results are also consistent with N-body simulations of 10^ 13 halos by Fiacconi++16 , who predict projected substructure mass densities of 2.0-2.8 10^ 7 kpc^ 2 after accounting for baryonic contraction of the halo. We infer roughly triple the predicted mass in substructure than the amount predicted by Xu++15 , who simulated 10^ 13 halos by rescaling Milky Way size and cluster size hosts to halo masses of 10^ 13 . Finally, we note that our results arrive on the heels of several works that examine numerical features of N-body simulations that may result in the artificial fragmentation of subhalos vandenBosch++18,ErraniPenarrubia19 . Taken at face value, these results suggest that N-body simulations may underpredict substructure abundance in dark matter halos. We may also compare our constraints with the projections from Gilman++19 . With a sample of ten quads, they projected a 2 bound on with _ sub = 0.022 kpc^ 2 of 10^ 7.7 with 2 uncertainties in image fluxes, and 10^ 8.6 with 6 uncertainties. Our constraint of < 10^ 7.8 is broadly consistent with these predictions The conversion between the half-mode mass and the mass of the corresponding thermal relic dark matter particle used by Gilman++19 is off by a factor of h=0.7, but the comparison between the half-mode masses is robust. , given the higher mean _ sub value of 0.055 kpc^ 2 we infer in this analysis, and the flux uncertainties in the lens sample which are 6 on average. The overall scaling of the line of sight halo mass function _ los is unconstrained with our sample size and choice of prior. This is likely because the prior on _ los spans a relatively limited range of 20 around the Sheth-Tormen mass function prediction, and with the current sample size of only eight quads we cannot constrain departures from the Sheth-Tormen prediction at the level of 10-20 . Sources of systematic uncertainties The lens macromodels Several works Gilman++17,Hsueh++18 have investigated the ability of smooth isothermal mass models plus external shear to fit the smooth mass component of galaxy scale strong lenses. These works reach similar conclusions, determining that isothermal models predict image flux ratios to better than 10 unless a stellar disk is present, in which case explicit modeling of the disk is required e.g. Hsueh++17,Hsueh++18 . Each of these analysis restricted the smooth lens models to exactly isothermal mass density profiles. The deflectors in our sample show no evidence for morphological complexity that would require explicit modeling beyond a power-law ellipsoid model. Specifically, we exclude all lens systems with known stellar disks to avoid any bias they may introduce. To account for remaining uncertainties associated with the lens macromodel, we highlight two features of our lens modeling implemented in an effort to mitigate this source of systematic uncertainty. First, we note that flux ratios are highly localized probes of the surface mass density in the immediate vicinity of the lensed images, and therefore the main requirement for this work is to accurately predict the mass profile in these four small isolated regions. By relaxing the strictly isothermal mass profile assumption and marginalizing over the logarithmic slope of the main deflector mass profile, we allow for the local mass profile in the vicinity of the lensed images to vary. The additional degree of freedom added in the lens macromodel increases our uncertainties, but accounts for deviations from power-law ellipsoids limited to exact ^ 2 mass profiles. Second, we note that smooth power-law models predict a distribution of flux ratios, rather than single values (for example, see Figures A1-A8 in Nierenberg++19 ). Following common practice, Gilman++17 and Hsueh++18 identified flux ratio `anomalies' with respect to a single smooth model fit to lensed images, a procedure that does not account for the distribution of flux ratios predicted by smooth lens models that is marginalized over in the full forward modeling analysis we perform. In this work, we also take care to explore the macromodel parameter space and the dark matter hyper-parameter space simultaneously, which accounts for additional covariances that contribute to the model-predicted flux uncertainties. Modeling of the dark matter content We assume specific functional forms for the halo and subhalo mass functions (Equations eqn:subhalomfunc and eqn:losmfunc ), and the mass-concentration-redshift relation (Equation eqn:cmrelation ). We acknowledge that there are other parameterizations in the literature for both of these quantities e.g. Schneider++12,Benson++13 , but in this work we implement only one parameterization of WDM effects on the mass function (Equation eqn:mfuncwdm ) and halo concentrations (Equation eqn:cmrelation ), which corresponds to one specific WDM model. We note that additional physics, such as the velocity dispersion of dark matter particles in the early universe, can alter the shape of the mass function, but with the current sample size of lenses it is unlikely we have enough information to constrain these additional features if they were included in the model. It is possible that free-streaming effects on the halo mass function near the half-mode mass scale may become more pronounced at high redshifts. This could affect both the location and shape of the turnover in the mass function. However, in the absence of a specific prediction for the evolution of the turnover with redshift, we apply the parameterization in Equation eqn:mfuncwdm through the relevant redshift range z = 0 - 3.5 . We note that since the lensing efficiency of halos decreases approaching source redshift, systematic errors from possible redshift evolution of the WDM turnover will be correspondingly down-weighted. We note that the mass-concentration-redshift relation for WDM calibrated by Bose++16 that we implement does evolve with redshift, as does the CDM mass-concentration relation from DiemerJoyce18 . Implications for WDM models Galaxy-galaxy strong lensing provides a useful compliment to the strongest existing probe of the free-streaming length of dark matter from the Lyman- forest Viel13,Irsic++17 . Our 2 bound on the thermal relic mass of m_ DM > 5.2 keV surpasses than the 3.3 keV constraint from Viel13 and matches the 5.3 keV constraint from Irsic++17 , who invoked additional assumptions regarding the relevant thermodynamics. The key point of this comparison, however, is not so much which method achieves the most precision, but the fact that both methods provide stringent limits and that they are completely independent of each other in observational data and astrophysical assumptions. Independently and in combination, the results from lensing and the Lyman- forest support the following statement: the halo mass function extends down in a scale-free manner to mass scales of 10^ 8 , where halos are mostly, if not completely, dark. There appears to be little room left for a viable warm dark matter solution to the small-scale issues of cold dark matter. The approximation of the true posterior obtained in Approximate Bayesian Computing (ABC) algorithms converges to the true posterior distribution as the acceptance criterion becomes increasingly more stringent. In our framework, changing the acceptance criterion is equivalent to reducing the number of forward model samples while keeping the number of total accepted realizations fixed. We exploit this property to test for convergence of the posteriors. In Figure fig:convergence , we compare the posterior constructed from the full set of forward model samples (the same as Figure fig:mainresult ) with a second posterior derived from a depleted set of forward model samples, where we have discarded one-third of the realizations and accepted the same rejection criterion (accept the realizations corresponding to the 800 lowest values of S_ lens ) to those that remain. The mass of the posterior distributions remains relatively unchanged, and the 1 and 2 contours are nearly identical. We conclude we have generated enough realizations of dark matter structure to reliably construct posterior distributions using the ABC rejection algorithm described in Section sec:inference . The quads PS J1606 and WGD J0405 do not have measured spectroscopic redshifts, so we use photometry from Shajib++18 to obtain photometric redshift estimates. The photometry from Shajib++18 comes in three bands: F160W, F814W, and F475X with magnitude uncertainties of 0.1-0.3 dex. We use the software package eazy Brammer++08 , and restrict the templates to only consider the SEDs for early-type galaxies, which are 90 of galaxies acting as strong lenses. We verify this procedure is accurate by applying it to other deflectors in sample analyzed by Shajib++18 that have measured spectroscopic redshifts, and then proceed to derive PDFs for deflector redshifts in the systems PS J1606 and WGD J0405. The results are shown in Figure fig:photozs . The top row shows four quads from the sample analyzed in Shajib++18 with measured spectroscopic redshifts, and the bottom row shows the pdfs output by eazy for the systems PS J1606 and WGD J0405. The system WFI 2026 does not have a photometric redshift, and the photometry available in the literature comes in only one or two bands with larger uncertainties. For this system, we use the equation for isothermal mass profiles relating the Einstein radius R_ Ein , source redshift z_s , lens redshift z_d , velocity dispersion and speed of light R_ Ein = 4 ^2 D_ ds z_d, z_s D_ s z_s where D_ ds and D_ s are angular diameter distances between the lens and the source, and the observer and the source, respectively. We sample a Gaussian distribution of velocity dispersions typical of early-type galaxies 240 30 km s^ 1 , evaluate the right hand side of Equation eqn:iso , and numerically solve for the lens redshift that yields the resulting angular diameter distance. The resulting PDF showin the bottom right panel of Figure fig:2026z peaks around z_d=1 , for the measured values R_ Ein = 0.67" , z_s = 2.2 . We have experimented with placing WFI 2026 at various specific redshifts, but find the posteriors for _ sub , _ los , , and are unchanged within the uncertainties.

sources.summary: The paper uses multi-season microlensing of the lensed quasar WFI 2026--4536 to measure accretion-disk size and black-hole mass, finding a disk larger than thin-disk theory predicts.

quotes.quote: We use thirteen seasons of R-band photometry from the 1.2m Leonard Euler Swiss Telescope at La Silla to examine microlensing variability in the quadruply-imaged lensed quasar WFI 2026--4536. The lightcurves exhibit 0.2 mag of uncorrelated variability across all epochs and a prominent single feature of 0.1 mag within a single season. We analyze this variability to constrain the size of the quasar's accretion disk. Adopting a nominal inclination of 60 , we find an accretion disk scale radius of (r_s/ cm ) = 15.74^ +0.34 _ 0.29 at a rest-frame wavelength of 2043 , and we estimate a black hole mass of (M_ BH /M_ ) = 9.18^ +0.39 _ 0.34 , based on the C 4 line in VLT spectra. This size measurement is fully consistent with the Quasar Accretion Disk Size -- Black Hole Mass relation, providing another system in which the accretion disk is larger than predicted by thin disk theory.

sources.summary: WFI2026 4536 receives first black-hole mass estimates from H and H Balmer lines, contributing to the paper’s enlarged lensed-quasar black-hole mass sample.

quotes.quote: We obtain M _ BH using the single-epoch method from the H and/or H broad emission lines for 14 lensed quasars, including the first estimates for QJ0158 4325, HE0512 3329 and WFI2026 4536. The masses are typical of non-lensed quasars of similar luminosity, and the implied Eddington ratios are typical. We have increased the sample of lenses with estimates of M _ BH by 60 .

There are the first M _ BH estimate obtained for the systems QJ0158 4325 (log _ 10 ( M _ BH /M _ ) = 8.05 0.58, 8.51 0.25 and 8.32 0.46 for C IV , Mg II and H , respectively), HE0512 3329 (log _ 10 ( M _ BH /M _ ) = 8.14 0.25) and WFI2026 4536 (log _ 10 ( M _ BH /M _ ) = 8.28 0.25 and 7.83 0.35, for H and H , respectively).
These are the first M _ BH estimates for the systems QJ0158 4325, HE0512 3329 and WFI2026 4536.

gmw_00004082.name: e491
gmw_00004082.overview: The high-redshift galaxy e491 is classified as an extremely red galaxy candidate [arXiv:astro-ph/0401047]. Photometric redshift estimates place the object between z = 4.58 and z = 5.22, with an estimated stellar mass of 5.8 times 10 to the power of 10 solar masses [arXiv:astro-ph/0401047].

sources.summary: The paper accepts e491 as a z>4 ERG candidate, with hyperz and BPZ redshifts above 4, no secondary redshift solution, high luminosity, large mass, and no B/V detections.

quotes.quote: e491 : `hyperz' primary solution is z_ phot =4.58 (with an associated probability P 98 ). The BPZ code estimates a redshift of z_ phot =5.22 . There are no significant secondary solutions for the redshift of this source in `hyperz'. The K_s band k-corrected absolute magnitude for this galaxy is M_ K_s =-25.6 and we estimate a mass of 5.8 10^ 10 M_ . This object has (I_ 775 z)= 0.6 0.3 and it is not detected in either the B or V bands.

gmw_00004083.name: e1703
gmw_00004083.overview: The high-redshift galaxy e1703 is classified as an extremely red galaxy candidate [arXiv:astro-ph/0401047]. It is not detected in the B or V bands, but it exhibits a very high K-band luminosity and an estimated stellar mass of 2.7 billion solar masses [arXiv:astro-ph/0401047].

sources.summary: The paper accepts e1703 as a z>4 ERG candidate, citing high hyperz and BPZ redshifts, no secondary solution, very high K-band luminosity and mass, and no B/V detections.

quotes.quote: e1703 : this source has a `hyperz' primary solution z_ phot =4.74 ( P 90 ) and no significant secondary solution. The BPZ redshift is z_ phot =5.38 . The estimated K_s band k-corrected absolute magnitude for this source is M_ K_s =-27.3 and the estimated mass is 2.7 10^ 11 M_ . It has (I_ 775 z)= 0.97 0.14 and it is not detected in either the B or V bands.

gmw_00004084.name: J153419+291359
gmw_00004084.overview: J153419+291359 is an active galactic nucleus host galaxy detected as a compact radio source [arXiv:2212.01874]. At spatial scales of 9 parsecs, the source shows little to no jet structure, while sub-kiloparsec radio jets emerge at lower resolution [arXiv:2212.01874].

sources.summary: J153419+291359 is a compact VLBI-detected SPARCS radio source, undetected in tapered and VLASS images, and identified among AGN-host sources whose sub-kpc jets emerge at lower resolution.

quotes.quote: J153419+291359 The source is detected in the notaper image at a 5.5 but remains undetected in the tapered image due to the increased noise levels. The rms in the tapered image is higher at 73 compared to the notaper rms at 53 . The source has an integrated flux density of 273 13 Jy and a peak brightness of 264 7.4 Jybeam^ 1 in VLBI and 594.8 5.7 and 550.1 3.1 in with R_ notaper = 0.46 . The source remains undetected in VLASS, with the peak brightness 5 the VLASS .m.s.

In principle, this survey permits the inclusion of galaxies with AGN hosts which would normally be excluded from such calculations, as evidenced by sources J153419+291359, J153406+292056 and J153354+291431. This is absolutely necessary if we are to obtain the tightest ever constraints on the SF measurements and to resolve out the total contribution of AGN to the cosmic SF history of the host galaxies e.g., Macfarlane2021 . At spatial scales of 9 pc, the sources show little to no jet structure whilst at 0.3 kpc one-sided and two-sided radio jets start to emerge on the same sources, indicating a possible transition from pure AGN emissions to AGN plus star-formation systems.

gmw_00004085.name: J153406+292056
gmw_00004085.overview: J153406+292056 is a faint, compact active galactic nucleus host galaxy [arXiv:2212.01874]. The source is detected in high-resolution VLBI observations but remains undetected in tapered and VLASS images, which may be due to active galactic nucleus variability [arXiv:2212.01874].

sources.summary: J153406+292056 is a faint compact VLBI radio source undetected in tapered and VLASS images; the paper groups it with AGN-host sources used to separate compact AGN from star-formation emission.

quotes.quote: J153406+292056 The source is detected in the notaper image only at 5.2 . The VLBI integrated flux and peak brightness are 197 52 and 307 29 while the integrated flux and peak brightness are 211 23 and 301 16 with R _ notaper = 0.9 . The non-detection in the tapered image can be attributed to the fact that this is a very faint source and the noise levels in the tapered image ( 77.5 ) are higher than in the notaper image ( 57 ). The source is also remains undetected in the VLASS image, with the peak brightness 2 times the VLASS noise level. This non-detection in VLASS could be attributed to AGN variability, see Section sec:Varibiality .

In principle, this survey permits the inclusion of galaxies with AGN hosts which would normally be excluded from such calculations, as evidenced by sources J153419+291359, J153406+292056 and J153354+291431. This is absolutely necessary if we are to obtain the tightest ever constraints on the SF measurements and to resolve out the total contribution of AGN to the cosmic SF history of the host galaxies e.g., Macfarlane2021 . At spatial scales of 9 pc, the sources show little to no jet structure whilst at 0.3 kpc one-sided and two-sided radio jets start to emerge on the same sources, indicating a possible transition from pure AGN emissions to AGN plus star-formation systems.

gmw_00004086.name: J153354+291431
gmw_00004086.overview: J153354+291431 is an active galactic nucleus host galaxy that exhibits a double-lobed radio structure [arXiv:2212.01874]. This structure is well-aligned in a northeast-southwest direction, indicating the presence of highly collimated, galactic-scale radio jets [arXiv:2212.01874].

sources.summary: J153354+291431 shows two VLBI compact cores, a single tapered-image core, and aligned double-lobed radio structure interpreted as collimated galactic-scale jets from an AGN-host source.

quotes.quote: J153354+291431 The source shows binary VLBI compact cores in the notaper image and a single core in the tapered image (see Fig. fig:source410 ). In the notaper image, the brighter core is detected at S/N 18.4 , while the second fainter core is detected at 5.5 . In the tapered image, the brighter core is detected at 14.7 , while the second fainter core remains undetected. The brightest VLBI core has a flux density of 268.0 17.0 and peak brightness of 282.0 10.0 in the notaper image with R _ notaper = 0.45 , and a flux density of 254.8 6.6 and peak brightness of 278.0 4.0 in the tapered image with R _ tapered = 0.42 . The fainter VLBI core has R _ notaper = 0.46 with a flux density of 74 13 and 75.6 7.4 in the notaper image.

The source J153354+291431 shows two compact VLBI cores in the notaper image and one compact VLBI core in the tapered image that are located within a double-lobed structure in both the VLASS and the images as shown in Figure fig:source410 . The fainter (northern core) is detected at 5.5 in the notaper image only, while the brightest (southern core) is detected at 18.4 and 14.7 across both the notaper and tapered images, respectively. The double-lobed structures are well aligned in the north-east south-west direction across both images, indicating the presence of highly collimated radio jet structures at galactic scales.

gmw_00004087.name: J153342+291625
gmw_00004087.overview: J153342+291625 is an active galactic nucleus and radio galaxy [arXiv:2212.01874]. The source is detected in both tapered and non-tapered VLBI images, though it remains undetected in the VLASS survey [arXiv:2212.01874].

sources.summary: J153342+291625 is detected in both VLBI images but remains undetected in VLASS, with measured notaper and tapered fluxes used in the paper's compact-source comparison.

quotes.quote: J153342+291625 The source has a detection in both the notaper and the tapered images but remains undetected in VLASS. It was detected at S/N 11.1 in the notaper image and 9.2 in the tapered image. The notaper VLBI integrated flux density is 117 11 Jy and a peak brightness of 127.1 6.9 with R _ notaper = 0.33 . The integrated flux and the peak brightness flux across the tapered image are 126 18 Jy and 122.4 respectively, with R _ tapered = 0.33 . The has a flux density of 352.5 6.4 and a peak brightness flux of 320.4 2.5 with the peak brightness 3 the VLASS .m.s.

AGN variability 3/11 VLBI sources, J153419+291359, J153406+292056 and J153342+291625 remain undetected in VLASS, see section sec:interestinfsources . For a VLBI source to be detected in VLASS, we assume that the peak brightness must be >5 the local rms in VLASS. With a rms sensitivity of 120 , the source peak brightness must be >600 for a detection in VLASS. All the 8/11 VLBI sources with VLASS counterparts have peak brightness >600 in VLASS.

gmw_00004088.name: companion galaxy of HE 0450--2958
gmw_00004088.overview: The companion galaxy of HE 0450--2958 is a strongly disturbed ultraluminous infrared galaxy [arXiv:0801.1494, arXiv:1003.3746]. It is interacting with a nearby quasar [arXiv:0801.1494, arXiv:1003.3746], hosting an intense starburst fueled by a large molecular gas reservoir [arXiv:0801.1494] and potentially harboring a second, dust-obscured active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1003.3746].

sources.summary: The paper presents the companion galaxy of HE 0450--2958 as part of a QSO/galaxy interaction where CO-rich gas and an intense starburst lie in the disturbed companion, not in the luminous QSO host.

quotes.quote: We report on interferometric imaging of the CO J=1--0 and J=3--2 line emission from the controversial QSO/galaxy pair HE 0450--2958. The detected CO J=1--0 line emission is found associated with the disturbed companion galaxy not the luminous QSO, and implies M_ gal (H_2) (1-2) 10^ 10 M_ , which is 30 of the dynamical mass in its CO-luminous region. Fueled by this large gas reservoir this galaxy is the site of an intense starburst with SFR 370 M_ yr^ 1 , placing it firmly on the upper gas-rich/star-forming end of Ultra Luminous Infrared Galaxies (ULIRGs, L_ IR >10^ 12 L_ ). This makes HE 0450--2958 the first case of extreme starburst and powerful QSO activity, intimately linked (triggered by a strong interaction) but not coincident.

The CO J=1--0 emission overlaid with the 8.4 GHz radio continuum is shown in Figure 2 where a good correspondence with the region C 2 associated with the companion galaxy (Feain et al. 2007) is evident. Regriding the observed CO emission and its deconvolved model, and then overlaying them onto the HST/ACS optical frame of HE 0450--2958 further underlines its association with the companion galaxy rather than the QSO (Figure 4).

Using X_ CO 1 M_ (K km s^ 1 pc^2)^ 1 (deduced for local ULIRGs, Downes Solomon 1998), we obtain M_ gal (H_2) 2.3 10^ 10 M_ , which places the companion galaxy in this galaxy/QSO pair firmly in the ULIRG category, at its most gas-rich upper end, and similar to starbursts found at high redshifts (e.g. Greve et al. 2005).

sources.summary: The companion galaxy is described as a disturbed, dusty ULIRG interacting with HE0450-2958, hosting most star formation and possibly a second obscured AGN.

quotes.quote: The only significant extended emission arose from two objects in the nearby field: first, from a strongly disturbed companion galaxy 7 kpc distant in projection from HE0450-2958, and also from a cloud of highly ionized gas showing no trace of stellar light, centered at 0.9 kpc NW from the QSO. Radio observations of the CO molecule by implied that the bulk of star formation was located in the companion galaxy and not around the QSO, and that both the QSO and galaxy have the properties of Ultra Luminous Infrared Galaxies (ULIRGs). and showed that the center of the companion galaxy is highly obscured by dust. also presented evidence that a second AGN lies in the companion galaxy, hidden by the thick dust cloud.

These findings support the hypothesis already presented by , involving a collision between a gas rich galaxy (the companion), and a smaller one (the blob). Two galaxies have collided, each of them probably harbouring an active BH, the interaction enhancing the star formation in at least one of the galaxies and disrupting the pre-existing structures.

gmw_00004089.name: RXJ1347-1216
gmw_00004089.overview: RXJ1347-1216 is a normal, star-forming Lyman-break galaxy with a spectroscopically confirmed redshift of approximately 6.76 [arXiv:1610.02099, arXiv:1504.02099]. The galaxy is strongly lensed and magnified by a factor of five by the massive foreground cluster RX J1347.1-1145 [arXiv:1610.02099].

sources.summary: The paper reports ALMA observations of RXJ1347:1216, a lensed z~6.77 sub-L* star-forming galaxy, measuring its redshift confirmation, magnification, and line detection.

quotes.quote: In this paper we report on ALMA observations of RXJ1347:1216, a normal ( L < L^* ) star forming galaxy with emission at z_ Ly =6.7659 ^ +0.0030 _ 0.0005 . It was first reported by bradley14 and smit14 as a photometrically selected z 7 galaxy from the and CLASH data (Cluster Lensing And Supernova survey with Hubble, postman12 ). We have spectroscopically confirmed its redshift using Keck DEIMOS data huang15a and grism data from GLASS treu15,schmidt16 . In huang15a we also measured its integrated stellar properties using deep SURFSUP data (Spitzer UltRa Faint SUrvey Program, surfsup ).

Our magnification model shows that the massive foreground galaxy cluster RX J1347.1 1145 magnifies RXJ1347:1216 by a factor 5.0 0.3 . Taking into account magnification, the galaxy's intrinsic rest-frame UV luminosity is 0.18^ +0.07 _ 0.05 L^* (assuming characteristic magnitude at z 7 of M_ UV ^*=-20.87 0.26 from bouwens15 ) making RXJ1347:1216 the first galaxy detected with ALMA at z 7 having a luminosity characteristic of the majority of galaxies at z 7 .

We have detected emission in RXJ1347:1216 with emission first reported by huang15a and schmidt16 . The line is detected at 5 (peak line flux S_ line = 1.25 0.25 mJy using 30 resolution, Fig.  fig:cii ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies RXJ1347-1216 as a z~7 LBG candidate with robust Ly emission at z=6.76, matching its photometric redshift and showing strong nebular-emission signatures.

quotes.quote: In Figure fig:spec_rxj1347_1216 we show the two-dimensional spectra of RXJ1347-1216 from both observation runs (in the top and middle panels) and the combined one-dimensional spectrum (in the bottom panel). We detect an extended emission feature with FWHM _ obs 16.5 \ in the 2013 run, and although the blue side of the feature is severely contaminated by sky line residual, its asymetric profile with a tail to the red side of the spectrum strongly suggests that it is Ly . Using the centroid of the sky line residual at 9439 \ as the peak of of line profile, we determine its Ly redshift as z_ Ly =6.76 0.003 (the uncertainty corresponds to the width of the sky line residual). The Ly redshift is in excellent agreement with its photometric redshift z_ phot =6.8 0.1 , lending additional support to the identification of the Ly feature.

From our Keck spectroscopic observations, we identify one secure Ly emitter at z = 6.76 (RXJ1347-1216) and one likely Ly emitter at z = 6.32 (MACS0454-1251). The line equivalent widths, assuming they are both Ly , are 26 4 \ (RXJ1347-1216) and 6.8 1.7 \ (MACS0454-1251, averaged over two nights). We infer lower limits of their star formation rates from their Ly line fluxes and find them to be consistent with the star formation rates from SED fitting.

gmw_00004090.name: MACS0454-1251
gmw_00004090.overview: MACS0454-1251 is a high-redshift galaxy likely situated at a redshift of z = 6.32 [arXiv:1504.02099]. It is classified as a Lyman-alpha emitter with an estimated star formation rate of approximately 17 solar masses per year [arXiv:1504.02099].

sources.summary: The paper treats MACS0454-1251 as a likely z=6.32 Ly emitter, with DEIMOS line detections on two nights, measured line fluxes, equivalent widths, and SFR lower limits.

quotes.quote: We show the reduced two-dimensional spectra of MACS0454-1251 in Figure fig:macs0454_1251 , and an extended emission feature is clearly detected on both nights. However, a bright sky line residual cuts through the middle of the emission feature in the spectral direction and makes the line interpretation ambiguous. Given the width of the emission line, it could be either Ly at z = 6.32 or [ OII ] at z = 1.39 , but the sky line residual makes it difficult to either confirm or rule out Ly or [ OII ] based on line shape alone. However, as we will show in Section subsec:model_results , this line is more likely to be Ly than [ OII ] because its fluxes (and upper limits) are much better fit by a galaxy template at z=6.32 than at z=1.39 (Section subsec:model_results ), and its photometric redshift probability density function P(z) has very a low probability at z=1.39 (see the P(z) curve in Figure fig:sedfits_all2 ).

We measure the line fluxes from both nights to be 1.2 0.2 10^ 17 \ erg \ cm ^ 2 \ s ^ 1 (first night) and 8.0 1.5 10^ 18 \ erg \ cm ^ 2 \ s ^ 1 (second night), and these translate to Ly luminosities of 5.5 0.9 10^ 42 \ erg \ s ^ 1 (first night) and 3.6 0.3 10^ 42 \ erg \ s ^ 1 (second night). From the line fluxes, we estimate its rest-frame equivalent widths (assuming Ly ) using the continuum on the red side of Ly (estimated from its F110W flux density) from both nights to be 8.2 1.4 \ and 5.4 1.0 . We also infer star formation rate lower limits to be 2.3 0.4 \ M_ \ yr ^ 1 (first night) and 1.5 0.3 \ M_ \ yr ^ 1 (second night) based on Ly fluxes, also fully consistent with being lower limits to the value derived from SED fitting in Section sec:sedfitting , 17.0^ +18.0 _ 4.1 M_ yr ^ 1 .

gmw_00004091.name: Abell 1068 cD galaxy
gmw_00004091.overview: The Abell 1068 cD galaxy is a brightest cluster galaxy [arXiv:1005.1211] that hosts a central starburst [arXiv:astro-ph/0310033] and has undergone regular periodic bursts every 80 million years [arXiv:1504.00921]. It is a strong mid-infrared source [arXiv:1005.1207] with active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:1005.1207, arXiv:1005.1211] and possible companion galaxy interactions [arXiv:astro-ph/0310033].

sources.summary: The paper treats A1068 as a FIR-bright BCG target with strong MIR and CO emission, some AGN contribution, and detected atomic cooling lines.

quotes.quote: The two clusters observed have quite contrasting multiwavelength properties. A1068 is a strong MIR source (O'Dea et al 2008) with a bright CO detection (Edge 2001) but a weak radio source (McNamara et al 2004). A1068 lies just below the luminosity threshold of a ULIRG (10 ^ 12 L _ ) and exhibits some contribution from an AGN (Crawford et al. 1999, O'Dea et al. 2008). On the other hand, A2597 is a relatively weak MIR source (Donahue et al. 2007) with a weak CO detection (Salom e, priv. comm.) but a powerful radio source (Sarazin et al. 1995). The implied FIR luminosity of A2597 of 8.8 10^ 9 L _ is a factor of around 30 below that of A1068 (3.5 10^ 11 L _ ) and, in addition, the fractional contribution from an AGN in the MIR is also lower. We have observed the [ C ii ] and [ O i ] lines at 157.74 m and 63.18 m for A1068 and A2597 with the PACS spectrometer on Herschel .

The [ C ii ] 157 m and [ O i ] 63 m lines are detected at a signal to noise greater than 30 for both A1068 and A2597. The much weaker [ N ii ] 122 m and [ O i b ] 145 m lines are detected at the 3--5 level for A2597. The [ O iii ] 88 m line was not detected in A2597, an upper limit for this line is given in Table 2. The line spectra are fitted by a model consisting of; (i) a linear function to determine the continuum flux, and (ii) a single gaussian function to determine the line flux. Continuum subtracted line spectra are shown for the central spaxel in Fig. 1 for A1068 and Fig. 2 for A2597. The fitted line centers agree well with the redshift of CO in the BCG and the fitted FWHM line widths indicate gas with velocities of 300--500 km s ^ 1 . The [ C ii ] and [ N ii ] lines in the central spaxel of both objects are well described by a single gaussian. However, the [ O i ] 63 m lines, where the PACS spectral resolution is best, have profiles indicative of weak (2--3 ) deviations from a single gaussian function. The [ O i ] line in A1068 hints at a two-component structure in the form of a narrow core component on top of a broad underlying component comparable to the CO(2-1) profile in Edge (2001).

sources.summary: The paper describes A1068 as a strong-MIR, compact far-infrared BCG near ULIRG luminosity, with AGN contribution, PACS detections, and lower PACS 70 micron flux than Spitzer MIPS.

quotes.quote: Abell 1068 and Zw3146 both have strong MIR emission (O'Dea et al. 2008, Egami et al. 2006) with a relatively bright CO detection (Edge 2001) and a weak central radio source (McNamara et al. 2004). A1068 lies just below the luminosity threshold of a ULIRG (10 ^ 12 L _ ) and exhibits some contribution from an AGN (Crawford et al. 1999, O'Dea et al. 2008).

A1068 has a relatively strong compact BCG in far infrared and so we performed the PSE to find that the flux estimates using AIPS and HIPE agree with each other to better than 5 . In the PACS photometry, A1068, A2597 and Zw3146 have been detected in all three bands. For A1068, 70 and 100 m values are slightly less than the IRAS 60 and 100 m measurements. This could be due to nearby sources that cannot be separated from the BCG in the much lower resolution IRAS observations but no sufficiently bright source is visible in our PACS imaging. There is a large difference between the Spitzer MIPS 70 m flux (Quillen et al. 2008) and our PACS 70 m flux, the PACS flux being a factor 1.7 lower than the MIPS flux.

sources.summary: The paper links the Abell 1068 cD galaxy to a central starburst, companion interaction, and metallicity enhancement aligned with the galaxy optical isophotes.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: optical position of the central cD galaxy (10 40 44.62 +39 57 10.2)

quotes.quote: In a companion paper (McNamara, Wise, Murray 2004, hereafter ``Paper 2''), we discuss the remarkable starburst properties of the Abell 1068 cD galaxy and present a detailed comparison between the X-ray and optical properties of the cD. In these papers, we show that 90 of the ultraviolet light from the starburst is emerging from the region of the cluster with the shortest cooling time, and that the star formation rates and local cooling rates agree. However, we stop short of presenting this fact as proof that the starburst is being fueled by the cooling flow, as there are independent data to suggest that the starburst may be associated with an ongoing dynamical interaction between the cD and a group of companion galaxies.

In Figure fig:amap , we show that there is a remarkable correlation between the location and shape of the Abell 1068 cD galaxy's optical isophotes and the isometallicity contours. The strongly flattened appearance of both the isophotes and the metallicity contours renders a chance coincidence unlikely. It suggests strongly that the presence of the cD and regions of enhanced metallicity are strongly coupled. The average cluster metallicity beyond the cD is Z_0 0.4 , in solar units, and rises to Z_ cD 0.8 at the location of the cD.

sources.summary: The paper includes A 1068 in the HST FUV data set and finds it undergoes regular periodic bursts since 6 Gyr ago at about 80 Myr intervals.

quotes.quote: Hubble Data The optical data for Hydra-A and RXC J1504 and the FUV data for PKS 0745-191, Hydra-A, A 1068, RXC J1504 and A 2199 were acquired as part of the HST Proposal 12220, ``Linking Star Formation with Intracluster Medium Cooling and AGN Heating in a Sample of Herschel Galaxy Clusters'' (PI: R.

While ZwCl 3146, A 1795, A 1835 and RXC J1504 seem to require continuous star formation, NGC 1275, PKS 0745, Hydra-A, A 1068 and A 2597 seem to require periodic starbursts with the interval ranging between 20 Myr and 200 Myr.
As per the results described in Table spprop , A 1068 seems to be undergoing regular bursts since 6 Gyr ago at intervals of about 80 Myr.

gmw_00004092.name: SDSS J103911-000057
gmw_00004092.overview: SDSS J103911-000057 is classified as a composite galaxy and a candidate true Type-2 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2109.02189]. The galaxy displays long-term optical variability and a reliable power-law continuum, but it lacks broad emission lines [arXiv:2109.02189].

sources.summary: The paper reports SDSS J103911-000057 as a composite galaxy and true Type-2 AGN candidate, based on narrow lines, an AGN continuum, variability, and absent broad emission.

quotes.quote: In the manuscript, the composite galaxy SDSS J103911-000057 (= ) is reported as a better candidate of true Type-2 AGN without hidden BLRs. None broad but only narrow emission lines detected in can be well confirmed both by the F-test technique and by the expected broad emission lines with EW smaller than 13.5 with 99 confidence level. Meanwhile, a reliable AGN power law component is preferred with confidence level higher than 7sigma in . Furthermore, the long-term variability of from CSS can be well described by the DRW process with intrinsic variability timescale 100 days , similar as normal quasars. And, based on BH mass in through the relation and on the correlation between AGN continuum luminosity and total H luminosity, the expected broad H , if there was, could be re-constructed with line width about 300-1000 km s^ 1 and with line flux about 666 10^ 17 erg s^ 1 cm^ 2 under the Virialization assumption to BLRs, providing robust evidence to reject the probability that the intrinsic probable broad H were overwhelmed by noises of the SDSS spectrum in .

In the manuscript, we report one new candidate of true Type-2 AGN in SDSS J103911-000057 (= ) through both its apparent long-term optical variability and high-quality spectroscopic features having no broad emission lines.

It is clear that the can be well confirmed as a better candidate of true Type-2 AGN but with unique properties among the reported true Type-2 AGN, especially its natural luminosity and natural Eddington ratio as normal quasars.

gmw_00004093.name: HSCJ234114+031506
gmw_00004093.overview: HSCJ234114+031506 is a candidate double-source-plane strong gravitational lens system [arXiv:2203.12805]. The system features a large northern lensed arc alongside several potential counterimages and nearby clumps [arXiv:2203.12805].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HSCJ234114+031506 as a grade A potential double-source-plane strong lens, describing its northern arc and possible counterimages that require follow-up spectroscopy.

quotes.quote: HSCJ234114+031506: A potential double source plane lens Based on the image configuration, HSCJ234114+031506 (grade A) is a potential double source plane lens (DSPL), in which there are two distinct sources at different redshifts. These systems are valuable due to the improved constraints on the lens model and the potential to constrain cosmological parameters (e.g., ), but are extremely rare.

The large northern arc in HSCJ234114+031506 is an obvious lensed feature, but it is unclear whether the compact image just south of the lens galaxy is the counterimage of this arc, or of the other nearby clump (northeast of the lens galaxy) interior to the arc. In addition, the more distant object to the west of the lens galaxy may be a counterimage of the northeast clump. Follow-up spectroscopy should reveal the nature of these objects.

gmw_00004094.name: SDSS J140910.47+275320.8
gmw_00004094.overview: SDSS J140910.47+275320.8 is a galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z=0.076 [arXiv:2204.12771, arXiv:2204.10864]. It is identified as the host galaxy of the gamma-ray burst GRB 211211A [arXiv:2204.12771, arXiv:2204.10864], which occurred at a projected offset of about 8 kiloparsecs from the galaxy [arXiv:2206.10710, arXiv:2204.10864].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J140910.47+275320.8 (G1) as the likely GRB 211211A host, giving its offset, magnitude, half-light radius, low chance-coincidence probability, redshift, and projected/normalized offset.

quotes.quote: A candidate of the optical counterpart of GRB 211211A is reportedly located very close to a Sloan Digital Sky Survey galaxy (SDSS J140910.47+275320.8; http://skyserver.sdss.org/dr12/en/tools/explore/summary.aspx ) with an offset of R_ off 5.50^ . This galaxy has a magnitude of m =19.53 mag in the band and a half-light radius of R_ 50 =0.61^ .

This value implies a random association between the optical counterpart and the galaxy can be ruled out at the 2.41 level. We note that such a PCC is much smaller than those of other galaxies near the position of the GRB. Therefore, we take this galaxy as the host galaxy of GRB 211211A.
Offset : Ref. has reported a redshift of z=0.076 of the host galaxy. Thus the offset in projection can be calculated as 8.19 kpc. And the normalized offset, defined by R_ off /R_ 50 , is 9.02.

sources.summary: The paper presents galaxy A as the z=0.076 host-galaxy candidate for GRB 211211A, placing the burst about 8 kpc from the host and noting hidden-host and supernova constraints.

quotes.quote: We suggest that there are at least two possibilities for the identity of the host galaxy of GRB 211211A. The first possibility is that, as advocated by , galaxy A is the host galaxy. With z=0.076 , this will put the GRB at projected distance of about 8 kpc from the host, at a position in which no apparent star formation is seen in the HST images. The deep HST image presented by exclude any underline host to F606W limiting magnitude of 27.8.

For a point source, this translate to absolute magnitude of about 10 at z=0.076 . For an extended source of size of about 1 kpc it may be possible to hide, in this position, a brighter galaxy (about 13 assuming 1 kpc size). A possible problem with this scenario is that it will be difficult to a hide a supernova that accompanies the GRB (see sec:SN ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J140910.47+275320.8 as the likely host of GRB 211211A, measures its redshift and offset, and models its low-mass, low-SFR stellar population.

quotes.quote: Motivated by GRB 211211A's gamma-ray light curve and its proximity to the bright ( = 19.4 mag) galaxy SDSS J140910.47+275320.8 (Figure fig:Hubble ), we initiated multi-wavelength follow-up observations. We obtained spectroscopy at the Nordic Optical Telescope (NOT; later confirmed with a Keck II spectrum, see Methods) that revealed the nearby galaxy is at a redshift z=0.0763 0.0002 (distance 350 Mpc). The modest offset between the galaxy and optical afterglow ( 5. ''44 0. ''02 ; 7.91 0.03 kpc in projection), their low probability of chance coincidence (1.4 , ), and the absence of any fainter, underlying host galaxy in late-time Hubble Space Telescope ( HST ) imaging provide compelling evidence that GRB 211211A originated in SDSS J140910.47+275320.8 (Figure fig:Hubble ).

We find that SDSS J140910.47+275320.8 has t_m = 4.00^ +0.65 _ 0.59 Gyr, (M_*/M_ ) = 8.84^ +0.10 _ 0.05 , A_V = 0.05^ +0.04 _ 0.03 mag, (Z_*/Z_ ) = -0.69^ +0.09 _ 0.20 , and log(Z _ gas /Z _ ) = 0.22^ +0.77 _ 0.34 . We show the Prospector SED fit compared to the observed data in Figure fig:prospectorSED . We determine an SED star formation rate (SFR) using Equation (1) in and find the galaxy has a low SED-inferred SFR =0.07 M _ yr ^ 1 and specific SFR (sSFR) 0.10 Gyr ^ 1 .

gmw_00004095.name: VCC 1488
gmw_00004095.overview: VCC 1488 is an early-type dwarf galaxy located in the Virgo cluster [arXiv:1707.01228]. It is classified as a blue-cored dwarf due to its unusually blue residual color and steep color gradient [arXiv:1707.01228].

sources.summary: The paper singles out VCC 1488 as a Virgo early-type dwarf with unusually blue residual color and includes it among quantitative blue-cored dwarfs.

quotes.quote: For example, galaxy VCC 1488 has a g- colour of 0.52 mag at the effective radius and an absolute magnitude of M_ = -17.0 mag.
The mean colour (g-) of all galaxies in the magnitude interval 17.5 mag < M_ < -16.5 mag is 0.62 mag and hence the residual g- colour of VCC 1488 is 0.10 mag.

We thus define all 12 galaxies 5 VCC 0021, VCC 0173, VCC 0209, VCC 0870, VCC 0951, VCC 1488, VCC 1499, VCC 1501, VCC 1512, VCC 1684, VCC 1779, and VCC 1912 with gradient values larger than 1 above the mean ( (g-) = 0.014 mag/ R_ eff ), i.e. with values above 0.10 mag/ R_ eff , as quantitative blue-cored early-type dwarf galaxies (indicated by the black horizontal tick in Fig. fig:gradients-gr-iH ) .

gmw_00004096.name: VCC 0209
gmw_00004096.overview: VCC 0209 is classified as a quantitative blue-cored early-type dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1707.01228]. It exhibits the strongest metallicity color gradient among its sample and contains dust detected by both WISE and Herschel [arXiv:1707.01228].

sources.summary: The paper classifies VCC 0209 as a quantitative blue-cored early-type dwarf, notes it was not previously classed that way, and reports dust detections.

quotes.quote: We thus define all 12 galaxies 5 VCC 0021, VCC 0173, VCC 0209, VCC 0870, VCC 0951, VCC 1488, VCC 1499, VCC 1501, VCC 1512, VCC 1684, VCC 1779, and VCC 1912 with gradient values larger than 1 above the mean ( (g-) = 0.014 mag/ R_ eff ), i.e. with values above 0.10 mag/ R_ eff , as quantitative blue-cored early-type dwarf galaxies (indicated by the black horizontal tick in Fig. fig:gradients-gr-iH ) .
All but one of them (VCC 0209) were already classified as blue-cored early-type dwarfs by the qualitative investigation of Lisker2006b , which was based on visual inspection of g-i colour maps.

Nonetheless, VCC 0209, the galaxy with the strongest metallicity colour gradient ( 0.42 mag/ R_ eff ) has been detected with WISE Boselli2014 and Herschel in all five bands Auld2013 and therefore contains a significant amount of dust.

gmw_00004097.name: HE0435 1223
gmw_00004097.overview: HE0435 1223 is a quadruply lensed quasar system with a source redshift of 1.693 [arXiv:1907.02533]. The primary lensing galaxy, located at a redshift of 0.4546, resides within a galaxy group containing at least 12 members [arXiv:1907.02533].

sources.summary: The paper describes HE0435 1223 as a quadruply lensed quasar with a lens galaxy at redshift 0.4546, group environment, measured velocity dispersion, and time delays.

quotes.quote: HE0435 1223: The \ system (J2000: 04 ^ h 38 ^ m 14 9, −12 17 14 4) is a quadruply-lensed quasar discovered by WisotzkiEtal02 . The main lensing galaxy is at a redshift of = 0.4546 MorganEtal05 , and the source redshift is = 1.693 SluseEtal12 . The lens resides inside a galaxy group that contains at least 12 galaxies, with a velocity dispersion =471 100 . e.g., MomchevaEtal06,WongEtal11,WilsonEtal16,SluseEtal17 . WongEtal17 measured the stellar velocity dispersion of the lensing galaxy to be =222 15 . The time delays of this system were measured by BonvinEtal17 with 6.5 uncertainties.

sources.summary: HE 0435 1223 is one of the lens galaxies analyzed for X-ray absorption and dust-to-gas work, with previous observations included and differential extinction estimated from CASTLES data.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we analyze \ observations of 10 gravitational lenses, HE 0047 1756 (Wisotzki et al. \ 2004; Ofek et al. \ 2006), QJ 0158 4325 (Maza et al. \ 1995; Morgan et al. \ 1999; Faure et al. \ 2009), SDSS 0246 0805 (Inada et al. \ 2005; Eigenbrod et al. \ 2006), HE 0435 1223 (Wisotzki et al. \ 2002; Ofek et al. \ 2006), SDSS 0924+0219 (Inada et al. \ 2003a; Ofek et al. \ 2006), SDSS 1004+4112 (Inada et al. \ 2003b), PG 1115+080 (Weymann et al. \ 1980; Kundi et al. \ 1997), Q 1355 2257 (Morgan et al. \ 2003; Eigenbrod et al. \ 2006), HE 1104 1805 (Wisotzki 1993; Lidman et al. \ 2000), and Q 2237+0305 (Huchra et al. \ 1985), to measure the differential X-ray absorption between the lensed images, the metallicity, and the dust-to-gas ratio of lens galaxies.
Observations of QJ 0158 4325, HE 0435 1223, SDSS 0924+0219, SDSS 1004+4112, HE 1104 1805, and Q 2237+0305 were reported in Chen et al. \ (2012).

The differential extinctions of QJ 0158 4325, Q 1355 2257, SDSS 1004 + 4112, and HE 0435 1223 are estimated using the optical/near-IR data from CASTLES (three filters: F160W, F555W, and F814W; Kochanek et al. \ 2007), while those for SDSS 0246 0825 and HE 0047 1756 are based on the flux ratios from Inada et al. \ (2005) and Wisotzki et al. \ (2004), respectively (both have five filters).

gmw_00004098.name: RXJ1131 1231
gmw_00004098.overview: RXJ1131 1231 is a quadruply lensed quasar [arXiv:1208.6009, arXiv:1907.02533] located at a redshift of 0.657 [arXiv:1907.02533]. The system features a foreground lensing galaxy at a redshift of 0.295 [arXiv:1208.6009, arXiv:1907.02533] that produces a full Einstein ring [arXiv:1208.6009].

sources.summary: The paper monitors the quadruply lensed quasar RX J1131 1231 for nine years, measuring near-zero A-B-C delays and a 91-day delay involving image D.

quotes.quote: RX J1131 1231 is one of the most spectacular lenses of our sample. The redshift of the lensing galaxy is z_ lens = 0.295 , while the quasar is at z_ QSO = 0.658 . This low quasar redshift means (1) that the photometric variations are fast, numerous, and strong because it is a lower-luminosity quasar see, e.g., MacLeod:2010ex , and (2) that the host galaxy of the lensed quasar is seen as a full Einstein ring with many spatially resolved structures in HST images.

We have been monitoring the quadruply lensed quasar RX J1131 1231 (J2000: 11^ h 31^ m 52^ s , 12 31 59 ) since December 2003 with three different telescopes in the R band ( 600 720 nm). Table monitoring summarizes the observational strategy and instrumental characteristics. The light curves presented in this paper cover nine observing seasons (2004 - 2012, see Fig. fig_lc ), with 707 monitoring epochs in total.

The best time-delay estimates of RX J1131 1231 are provided by the regression difference technique. It measures the 91-day delays between D and the other quasar images to a fractional 1 uncertainty of 1.5 .

sources.summary: The paper treats RXJ1131 1231 as a quadruply lensed quasar with stated system coordinates, lens and source redshifts, lens velocity dispersion, and measured time delays.

quotes.quote: RXJ1131 1231: The \ system (J2000: 11 ^ h 31 ^ m 52 ^ s , − 12 31 59 ) is a quadruply-lensed quasar discovered by SluseEtal03 . The spectroscopic redshifts of the lensing galaxy and the backgound source are at =0.295 SuyuEtal13 and =0.657 SluseEtal07 , respectively. SuyuEtal13 measured the stellar velocity dispersion of the lensing galaxy to be =323 20 . The time delays were measured by TewesEtal13a with 1.5 uncertainties.

gmw_00004099.name: 3XMM J215022.4 055108
gmw_00004099.overview: The source 3XMM J215022.4 055108 is located at the outskirts of a large barred lenticular galaxy [arXiv:2002.04618]. It is associated with a faint optical counterpart that may be an ultracompact dwarf galaxy remnant from an active minor merger [arXiv:2002.04618].

sources.summary: The paper presents 3XMM J215022.4 055108 as an HLX/IMBH candidate whose outburst is associated with an off-center compact counterpart, possibly a UCD remnant from minor merging.

quotes.quote: We reported our discovery of a new HLX candidate 3XMM J215022.4 055108 (J2150 0551 hereafter) in Lin18 hereafter listca2018 . The source exhibited a prolonged X-ray outburst of peak X-ray flux of 10^ 12 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 , lasting for more than a decade and X-ray spectra soft and purely thermal. The outburst was also detected in the optical. The source is located at the outskirts of a large barred lenticular galaxy (Gal1 hereafter) at z=0.055 , and we identified a faint optical counterpart based on the positional coincidence and the correlated optical/X-ray variability.

Therefore, J2150 0551 is most likely associated with Gal1. With a half-light radius of 27 pc, an absolute V band magnitude of 12.3 AB mag and a stellar mass of 10^7 , the counterpart could be a massive globular cluster or a UCD resulting from a minor merger nokafo2014 . The latter explanation is more likely, given that the host galaxy Gal1 seems to be in an epoch of active minor mergers (see the presence of a possible nearby minor merger of Gal2 with Gal1, Figure fig:hstimg2018 ). This compact system has a stellar mass around the limit above which stellar systems could only be explained as remnant nuclei of tidally disrupted galaxies, instead of true ancient star clusters novaka2019 .

gmw_00004100.name: Gal1
gmw_00004100.overview: Gal1 is a barred lenticular galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.055 [arXiv:2002.04618]. The galaxy's starlight affects counterpart fitting for the nearby source J2150 0551, which is highly likely to be associated with Gal1 [arXiv:2002.04618].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Gal1 as a barred lenticular galaxy at z=0.055 whose starlight affects counterpart fitting and whose proximity makes association with J2150 0551 likely.

quotes.quote: The source is located at the outskirts of a large barred lenticular galaxy (Gal1 hereafter) at z=0.055 , and we identified a faint optical counterpart based on the positional coincidence and the correlated optical/X-ray variability.

At the position of the source, there is star light from the host galaxy Gal1. Then there are also concerns about the size of the fitting region and how to model the background (sky plus the star light from Gal1).

Therefore, J2150 0551 should have an extragalactic origin and can be associated with either Gal1 or a background galaxy. The chance probability for J2150 0551 to be within 11.6 arcsec from the center of a bright galaxy like Gal1 is very small, only 0.01 (Lin18). The optical counterpart to J2150 0551 has a circularly symmetric compact profile, unlike most background galaxies. The high disk temperature of the outburst is also hard to explain if it is in a distant background galaxy, as will be discussed below. Therefore, J2150 0551 is most likely associated with Gal1.

gmw_00004101.name: SDSS J1106+1939
gmw_00004101.overview: SDSS J1106+1939 is a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:1211.6250]. Its spectrum exhibits a wide high-ionization outflow with absorption troughs associated with multiple ionic species [arXiv:1211.6250].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes SDSS J1106+1939 as a BAL quasar with a wide high-ionization outflow, deriving its location, energetics, ionization state, and electron density from VLT/X-shooter spectra.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we present the analysis of VLT/X-shooter spectra of two SDSS BAL quasars, SDSS J1106+1939 and SDSS J1512+1119, for which the presence of / troughs allows us to place constraints on the location and energetics of the outflow in SDSS J1106+1939 and of two separate outflows in SDSS J1512+1119.

We selected our targets SDSS J1106+1939 (J2000: RA=11 06 45.05, dec=+19 39 29.1, g=19.4) and SDSS J1512+1119 (J2000: RA=15 12 49.29, dec=+11 19 29.4, g=17.7) from an extensive flux limited search of the SDSS DR7 catalog for objects indicative of the presence of \ absorption on the basis of their SDSS spectrum. We observed these two objects with VLT/X-shooter as part of our program 87.B-0229 (PI: Benn) in April 2011 and March 2012, respectively.

BAL Column Density Measurements of SDSS J1106+1939 The X-shooter spectrum of SDSS J1106+1939 exhibits wide absorption troughs associated with , , , , , / and \ ionic species. The \ absorption trough (and most other troughs as well) satisfies the observational definition of a broad absorption line BAL, see Weymann91 .

gmw_00004102.name: SDSS J1512+1119
gmw_00004102.overview: SDSS J1512+1119 is a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:1211.6250, arXiv:1208.5910]. Spectroscopic analysis of the active galactic nucleus reveals a broad absorption line outflow containing two main kinematic components [arXiv:1211.6250] and phosphorus-bearing absorption troughs [arXiv:1208.5910].

sources.summary: The paper studies SDSS J1512+1119 as a BAL quasar with two main outflow components, measuring absorption kinematics, ionic columns, electron densities, and energetic constraints.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we present the analysis of VLT/X-shooter spectra of two SDSS BAL quasars, SDSS J1106+1939 and SDSS J1512+1119, for which the presence of / troughs allows us to place constraints on the location and energetics of the outflow in SDSS J1106+1939 and of two separate outflows in SDSS J1512+1119.

We selected our targets SDSS J1106+1939 (J2000: RA=11 06 45.05, dec=+19 39 29.1, g=19.4) and SDSS J1512+1119 (J2000: RA=15 12 49.29, dec=+11 19 29.4, g=17.7) from an extensive flux limited search of the SDSS DR7 catalog for objects indicative of the presence of \ absorption on the basis of their SDSS spectrum. We observed these two objects with VLT/X-shooter as part of our program 87.B-0229 (PI: Benn) in April 2011 and March 2012, respectively.

Column Density in SDSS J1512+1119 Our second target, SDSS J1512+1119, displays a \ absorption trough whose width is close to a BAL ( v 1700 km s ^ 1 , see Paper II). However, the \ lines are narrow enough and unblended ( v 2000 km s ^ 1 ) to be adopted as a template to identify the kinematic components in other ionic species. Using that template, we reported in Paper II the existence of five main kinematic components with centroids at velocities of v 2100, -1850, -1500, -1050, and -520 km s ^ 1 (labeled component 1--5). In this paper we concentrate on determining the distance and energetics associated with the two main components: component 2 and 4.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes the BAL outflow of quasar SDSS J1512+1119, measuring phosphorus-bearing absorption, constraining near-solar phosphorus abundance, and showing true optical depth is about 1000 times apparent depth.

quotes.quote: We present spectroscopic analysis of the broad absorption line outflow in quasar SDSS J1512+1119. In particular, we focus our attention on a kinematic component in which we identify \ and / absorption troughs. The shape of the unblended phosphorus doublet troughs and the three / troughs allow us to obtain reliable column density measurements for these two ions.

Photoionization modelling using these column densities and those of constrain the abundance of phosphorus to the range of 0.5--4 times the solar value. The total column density, ionization parameter and metalicity inferred from the \ and \ column densities leads to large optical depth values for the common transition observed in BAL outflows. We show that the true \ optical depth, is  1000 times greater in the core of the absorption profile than the value deduced from it's apparent optical depth.

gmw_00004103.name: PS J1721+8842
gmw_00004103.overview: PS J1721+8842 is a gravitationally lensed dual-AGN system at a redshift of approximately 2.37 [arXiv:2109.03253, arXiv:2206.04696]. It features two distinct quasars separated by about 6 kiloparsecs, which are lensed into six total images alongside a third stellar component involved in a potential triple merger [arXiv:2109.03253, arXiv:2209.03094].

sources.summary: The paper presents PS J1721+8842 as a gravitationally lensed dual-AGN system with two quasars, compact radio emission, a third red galaxy, and possible triple-merger or recoiling-black-hole interpretations.

quotes.quote: From these data, we find that PS J1721+8842 is comprised of two distinct quasars that are formed into two and four images, respectively. Both quasars are optically and radio luminous, and separated by 6.5 kpc in the source plane, making this object the first dual-AGN system that is also gravitationally lensed. Our letter is arranged as follows. In Sections sec:observations and sec:lens , we present the observations and lens modelling, respectively. In Section sec:discussion , we discuss the various scenarios that can explain the complex morphology of PS J1721+8842, and outline future work to be carried out on this intriguing system.

We have shown that PS J1721+8842 is comprised of two quasars that form a dual-AGN system, with a third stellar component that forms part of a triple-merger. There is clear evidence of AGN activity in the form of two quasars and possibly a very high level of concentrated star-formation or weak radio jets within the quasar hosts. There are two scenarios that can explain the observed properties of PS J1721+8842. First is a pre-merger scenario, wherein the active black holes (and their unseen host galaxies) are in the process of merging with the compact red galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1721+8842 as a z about 2.38 quadruply lensed binary quasar with two sources separated by 6.0 kpc and PDLA-bearing spectra.

quotes.quote: Through image deconvolution of StanCam Nordic Optical Telescope (NOT) monitoring data, we reveal two further point sources in the known, z 2.38 , quadruply lensed quasar (quad), J1721+8842. An ALFOSC/NOT long-slit spectrum shows that the brighter of these two sources is a quasar with z = 2.369 0.007 based on the C iii ] line, while the C iii ] redshift of the quad is z = 2.364 0.003 . Lens modelling using point source positions rules out a single source model, favouring an isothermal lens mass profile with two quasar sources separated by 6.0 kpc (0.73 ) in projection.

Given the resolving ability from lensing and current lensed quasar statistics, this discovery suggests a large population of undiscovered, unlensed sub-10-kpc binaries. We also analyse spectra of two images of the quad, showing narrow Ly emission within the trough of a proximate damped Lyman- absorber (PDLA). An apparent mismatch between the continuum and narrow line flux ratios provides a new potential tool for simultaneously studying microlensing and the quasar host galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes J1721+8842 as a complex lensed system with two similar-redshift quasar sources, six lensed images, damped Lyman alpha absorption, and caution-worthy astrometry.

quotes.quote: J1721+8842 The system was originally discovered by Lemon19 who confirmed the source to be at z 2.37, with strong absorption features. Gaia DR2 catalogues 5 detections, and an in-depth study of this system by lemon2022 show that there are two quasar sources at similar redshifts, with one being lensed into four images, and one into two images. They provide several mass models for the system, which we will compare to in Section lensmodels . The source is also unique in that the bright images A and C are confirmed to have a proximate damped Lyman alpha absorber.

however, we urge caution with regards to the results of J1721+8842, as this system is highly complex and, as shown in Figure fig:astrometry , we find small variances between our predicted image positions and the corresponding Gaia measurements.

sources.summary: PS J1721+8842 is described as an unusual six-image lens with two quasar sources; the paper models three bands and manually adds a second source component.

quotes.quote: PS J1721+8842 This is an unusual and interesting lens system with 6 quasar images. It is composed of two quasar sources at the same redshift, with one quasar lensed into a quad (images A, B, C, and D) and the other one lensed into a double (images E and F) Lemon2021 . Most of the arc light in this system comes from the double system, so we only include these regions for the arc light modeling and SSB reconstruction. We modeled this system in all three considered bands with two S e rsic profiles and a central point source component for the primary lens light. We picked three stars from the field to approximate the PSF in the F160W band, and one star in each of the F475X and F814W bands.

gmw_00004104.name: 3C 129.1
gmw_00004104.overview: 3C 129.1 is a double-lobed giant elliptical radio galaxy [arXiv:1412.5324] that serves as the brightest cluster galaxy of its host X-ray cluster [arXiv:2405.09738]. The galaxy hosts an active radio nucleus with dual jets [arXiv:1607.03636] and a small, thermal X-ray corona at its center [arXiv:2405.09738].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 129.1 as a double-lobed giant elliptical radio galaxy sharing the ZoA ridge and CIZA cluster environment with 3C 129.

quotes.quote: The most conspicuous is a ridge at about 160^ , v 6500 . Within this wall-like structure, two strong radio galaxies (3C 129 and 3C 129.1) are embedded which lie at the same distance as the ridge. They seem to form part of an X-ray cluster.

Their position and redshifts (6236 and 6655 km s ^ 1 respectively; ) confirm that they reside inside the galaxy concentration. These are the head-tail radio source (3C 129) and the double-lobed giant elliptical radio galaxy (3C 129.1). The presence of such radio sources with bent lobe morphology usually is indicative of a rich cluster environment.

The suspicion that 3C 129 and 3C 129.1 form part of a massive cluster was later substantiated through the identification of the X-ray cluster CIZA J0450.0+4501 ( ). The radio sources lie within the X-ray emission of the CIZA cluster and are at the same distance.

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 129.1 as the cluster BCG and radio AGN, describing its compact radio emission, small central X-ray corona, heated core, and cool-core-remnant context.

quotes.quote: The 3C 129.1 cluster, with a temperature of 5.6 keV Ikebe02 , contains two strong radio sources, i.e., 3C 129 and 3C 129.1. 3C 129.1 (or WEIN 051) is the BCG of this cluster. 3C 129.1 was observed by \ for 9.5 ksec in 2001 (ObsID 2219).

The previous studies showed that the system has a heated core and several candidates of ultraluminous X-ray sources (ULXs) Krawczynski2002, Krawczynski2003 . At the very centre, 3C 129.1 hosts a small, extended X-ray source and it is most likely a thermal corona Sun2007 . In fact, in this work we found the cluster core is heated, with an elevated metal abundance (see section 3c129_results ), which was proposed to be a signature of a cool core remnant e.g., Rossetti2010 .

The radio images of 3C 129.1, from \ at 240 MHz and 610 MHz, and \ at 5 GHz, are shown in Fig. radio . The radio emission of 3C 129.1 is relatively compact with a radio power at 1.4 GHz of 2 10^ 24 W Hz ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 129.1 as the companion wide-angle-tail source near the cluster center, with faint buoyant lobes but probably not the Crosspiece origin.

quotes.quote: The radio galaxy 3C 129 and its companion 3C 129.1 are part of an X-ray galaxy cluster at z=0.021 (4U 0446+44). The cluster lies in the galactic plane (b =0.5^ o , l =160^ o ) and is only known to include one other member galaxy (WEIN 048) which lies to the south of 3C 129 nil00 . Because of their latitude, the cluster and the radio sources have been excluded from many optical studies.

3C 129.1, although considerably smaller, is a wide-angle-tail (WAT) source, with a total flux density of 1.9 Jy at 1400 MHz condon98 . ROSAT X-ray data place the cluster center just to the southwest of 3C 129.1.

We suggest that these faint sources are two buoyant radio lobes produced by earlier activity of 3C 129.1. Their comparable sizes, luminosities, and similar distances to 3C 129.1 support this conclusion.
