The larger uncertainty applies to the case where the NC is weaker i.e., for quasars like 3C 179 and S5 1856+73: a small change of the BC translates into a much larger fractional change for the NC. This can also be seen from the intensity ratio between the NC and BC. For the two quasars with larger uncertainties (3C 179 and S5 1856+73), this ratio is, I _ NC /I _ BC 0.125 and 0.097, respectively.

The two sources (3C 179 and PKS 2208-137) with FWHM < 4000 have ( ) 0.64 and -0.75, respectively. These values are at the \ boundary between Pop. A and B, signifying a possible \ underestimate due to low S/N (i.e., loss of line wings in the case of 3C 179) or perhaps to a pole-on orientation of the emitting regions e.g., 1986ApJ...302...56W, 2003ApJ...597L..17S, 2003MNRAS.340.1298R, 2008MNRAS.387..856Z .

sources.summary: The paper detects an Fe K emission line in 0723+679, with residual excess near the redshifted line energy and possible implications for weaker beaming.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, we find evidence for possible Fe K line emission in two sources of our sample, 0723+679 and 1150+497, and possibly in a third object, 1642+690. Inspection of the residuals of the power law models shows excess emission around 3.5 keV, 4.8 keV, and 3.7 keV for 0723+679, 1150+497, and 1642+690, respectively. These energies are consistent with the redshifted Fe K line energy, 6.4 keV. To model the line, a Gaussian component was added to the power law models reported in Table 4 for each of the three sources. The significance of the ^2 improvement is 97 for 0723+679 and 1150+497, and 94 for 1642+690. In Figure 3 we show the best-fit model and data for 0723+679 and 1150+497, for which the line detections are more significant.

We detect an Fe K emission line in two sources (0723+679 and 1150+497) and possibly in a third (1642+690) source. The detection of such a line is very interesting di per se , because the presence of Fe K-shell emission from high-luminosity AGNs in general, and from radio-loud sources in particular, is still not established (see Sect. 1). While it is important to confirm these lines and study their profile with higher-quality X-ray spectra, we note that their measured rest-frame EWs (Table 6a) are consistent with the EWs of the other radio-sources with a double-lobe radio morphology and similar intrinsic X-ray luminosity recently studied by ASCA (Hasenkopf et al. 2002). Usually, dilution of the X-ray continuum by a beamed jet component would weaken the strength of the Fe K lines in radio-loud quasars. This could suggest weaker beaming in 0723+679 (indeed a SSRQ) and in 1150+497 and 1642+690 (both FSRQs).

gmw_00004147.overview: The source 1150+497 is a core-dominated, radio-loud flat-spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of approximately 0.334 [arXiv:1409.8101]. It features a kiloparsec-scale jet detected in radio, optical, and X-ray bands [arXiv:astro-ph/0606659, arXiv:1409.8101], which is surrounded by diffuse thermal X-ray emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0606659].

sources.summary: The paper treats 1150+497 as a powerful radio-loud quasar with a kpc-scale X-ray jet, diffuse thermal emission around its core, and curved X-ray continuum emission.

quotes.quote: Here we tackle this issue using new, deep \ and \ observations of the cores of three powerful radio loud quasars: 1136--135, 1150+497 ( ), and 0723+679 ( ), in the redshift range z =0.3--0.8. These sources are known from our previous \ snapshot survey to have kpc-scale X-ray jets. In 1136--135 and 1150+497, evidence is found for the presence of diffuse thermal X-ray emission around the cores, on scales of 40--50 kpc and with luminosity L _ 0.3-2 keV 10^ 43 , suggesting thermal emission from the host galaxy or a galaxy group. The X-ray continua of the cores in the three sources are described by an upward-curved (concave) broken power law, with photon indices _ soft 1.8-2.1 and _ hard 1.7 below and above 2 keV, respectively. There is evidence for an unresolved \ line with EW 70 eV in the three quasars.

Follow-up \ observations were carried out on April 16, 2003 for 1136--135 and on July 18, 2003 for 1150+497, with total exposures 81 ks and 70 ks, respectively. Screening was performed using standard criteria (S06). After screening, the effective exposure times are 70.2 ks for 1136--135 and 61.7 ks for 1150+497. The net count rates of the cores in the energy range 0.3--10 keV from an extraction circle with radius 1.5 \ are 0.27 0.02 /s for 1136--135 and 0.55 0.03 /s for 1150+497.

sources.summary: The paper finds soft X-ray excess emission and an Fe K line in 1150+497, interpreting the line strength as possibly reflecting weaker beaming.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, we find evidence for possible Fe K line emission in two sources of our sample, 0723+679 and 1150+497, and possibly in a third object, 1642+690. Inspection of the residuals of the power law models shows excess emission around 3.5 keV, 4.8 keV, and 3.7 keV for 0723+679, 1150+497, and 1642+690, respectively. These energies are consistent with the redshifted Fe K line energy, 6.4 keV. To model the line, a Gaussian component was added to the power law models reported in Table 4 for each of the three sources. The significance of the ^2 improvement is 97 for 0723+679 and 1150+497, and 94 for 1642+690. In Figure 3 we show the best-fit model and data for 0723+679 and 1150+497, for which the line detections are more significant.

We detect an Fe K emission line in two sources (0723+679 and 1150+497) and possibly in a third (1642+690) source. The detection of such a line is very interesting di per se , because the presence of Fe K-shell emission from high-luminosity AGNs in general, and from radio-loud sources in particular, is still not established (see Sect. 1). While it is important to confirm these lines and study their profile with higher-quality X-ray spectra, we note that their measured rest-frame EWs (Table 6a) are consistent with the EWs of the other radio-sources with a double-lobe radio morphology and similar intrinsic X-ray luminosity recently studied by ASCA (Hasenkopf et al. 2002). Usually, dilution of the X-ray continuum by a beamed jet component would weaken the strength of the Fe K lines in radio-loud quasars. This could suggest weaker beaming in 0723+679 (indeed a SSRQ) and in 1150+497 and 1642+690 (both FSRQs).

sources.summary: The paper presents coordinated multiwavelength observations of the blazar S4 1150+49, describing its redshift, core-dominated FSRQ nature, and prominent kiloparsec radio-optical-X-ray jet.

quotes.quote: Data and results from simultaneous and coordinated ray and multi-wavelength (MW) observations of the flat spectrum radio quasar (FSRQ) \ (also known as S4 1150+49, OM 484, SBS 1150+497 and GB1 1150+497), are presented. \ is a core-dominated, radio-loud FSRQ located at z = 0.334 lynds68,burbidge68,stepanian01 . The Sloan Digital Sky Survey SDSS; adelman DR7 and DR8 give values of z=0.3339 and of z=0.33364 , respectively. This blazar shows a kiloparsec-extent and one-sided, knotty and wiggling radio jet, with high surface brightness, sharp bends and resolved substructures see, e.g., owen84,akujor91,sambruna04,sambruna06a,sambruna06b . The jet, known to show constant low optical polarisation moore81 , has a twisted morphology with a corkscrew structure reminiscent of 3C 273 and, remarkably, is also well detected at X-ray and optical bands.

sources.summary: Deep Chandra, HST, and radio observations show 1150+497 has an FRII quasar jet with similar multiwavelength profile declines, no X-ray spectral variations, and stronger terminal radio emission.

quotes.quote: We present deep (70--80 ks) \ and multicolor \ ACS images of two jets hosted by the powerful quasars 1136--135 and 1150+497, together with new radio observations. The sources have an FRII morphology and were selected from our previous X-ray and optical jet survey for detailed follow up aimed at obtaining better constraints on the jet multiwavelength morphology and X-ray and optical spectra of individual knots, and to test emission models deriving physical parameters more accurately.

In 1150+497 the X-ray, optical, and radio profiles decrease in similar ways with distance from the core up to 7 , after which the radio emission increases more than the X-ray one. No X-ray spectral variations are observed in 1150+497.

gmw_00004148.name: UGCA 442
gmw_00004148.overview: UGCA 442 is an edge-on, gas-dominated late-type dwarf galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0411231, arXiv:1606.09251] located at a distance of approximately 4.27 Mpc [arXiv:astro-ph/0302045]. Its structure features an exponential surface brightness profile with central flattening [arXiv:1606.09251], and its thick-disk stellar populations exhibit weak color gradients [arXiv:astro-ph/0411231].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes UGCA 442 as a resolved edge-on dwarf disk and finds the sample's thick-disk stellar populations have weak color gradients and consistently red giant branches.

quotes.quote: In the present paper we analyze NGC 784, 1560, UGC 1281, and UGCA 442. They span a range of rotation velocities from 95 to 150 km/sec (21 cm line full width at 20 peak). WFPC2 on HST reaches below the tip of the red giant branch (RGB) in these galaxies, allowing us to diagnose the star formation history of their thick disk stellar populations.

The noteworthy feature of Figure 6 is the weakness of the color gradient in the disks of these galaxies out to 2 kpc from the plane. The average over the four galaxies is 0.06 0.02 mag/kpc in the sense that the thick disk is redder further from the thin disk at the 3 level. Column (2) of Table 2 records the weighted average V--I for the region of the disk beyond 900 pcs, and column (3) gives the reddening in the Milky Way according to NED and Schlegel (1998). The giant branches of the thick disk regions of these galaxies are consistently red, with V--I colors ranging from 1.39 to 1.54 (column 4).

sources.summary: The paper presents UGCA 442 as a gas-dominated late-type dwarf with an exponential profile, central flattening, slow-rising rotation curve, and dark matter needed at small radii.

quotes.quote: The Choice of the Characteristic Radius Figure fig:massmodels shows mass models for two galaxies with diametrically opposed properties: a bulge-dominated spiral galaxy (NGC 7814) and a gas-dominated dwarf galaxy (UGCA 442).

UGCA 442 (right) is a late-type dwarf (Sm). The surface brightness profile is well described by an exponential law, but there is a central flattening due to a cored light distribution. The rotation curve shows a slow rise in the central regions and a flat part beyond the stellar component. For _ =0.5 M_ / L_ baryons cannot fully explain the inner rise of the rotation curve and DM is needed down to small radii.
Note that the shape of V_ bar is mostly driven by the gas contribution and R_ bar occurs well beyond R_ eff or R_ 2.2 due to the gas dominance.

sources.summary: The paper reports H II-region imaging and spectroscopy for UGCA 442, finding extended H I, a bright-line oxygen abundance matching prior work, and adopted nitrogen abundance.

quotes.quote: UGCA 442 obtained \ imaging of this edge-on galaxy in the Scl group and found five H II regions. Spectroscopy for two H II regions in the southwest part of the disk ( 2 and 4 as labelled by miller96 ) did not reveal ; he derived an average bright-line oxygen abundance of 12 + log(O/H) = 7.90. These two H II regions appear to be in a part of the galaxy where found a local maximum in H I ; the H I extent is about a factor of five larger than the optical extent. Our spectrum of H II region 2 (labelling by miller96 ) also did not reveal , and our subsequent bright-line oxygen abundance (12 + log(O/H) = 7.85) agrees with the value obtained by . The adopted nitrogen-to-oxygen ratio is log(N/O) = 1.41 .

sources.summary: The paper describes UGCA 442 as an edge-on Im galaxy, larger than the WFPC2 field, resolved into blue and red stars with a TRGB distance of 4.27 Mpc.

quotes.quote: This is an edge-on galaxy of Im type with a radial velocity V_ LG = 299 km s ^ 1 and angular dimension 6 4 0 9 , much larger than the WFPC2 field. The galaxy seems to be well resolved into stars. Our HST photometry reveals about 5600 stars, both blue and red ones. The derived TRGB, I = 24 13 0 26 , corresponds to a distance of 4.27 0.52 Mpc.

gmw_00004149.name: SDSS J221241.53+003042.7
gmw_00004149.overview: SDSS J221241.53+003042.7 is a dwarf host galaxy with a low absolute magnitude of -14.8 mag [arXiv:1208.5900]. The galaxy exhibits measured emission lines [arXiv:1208.5900] and has a subsolar oxygen abundance indicating a metallicity of 0.2 to 0.3 solar metallicities [arXiv:1208.5900].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J221241.53+003042.7 as PTF12gzk's dwarf host, measuring the SN's central offset, SDSS emission lines, low absolute magnitude, and subsolar oxygen abundance.

quotes.quote: PTF12gzk was discovered on 2012 July 24.3 (UT dates are used herein) at (J2000) = 22 ^ h 12 ^ m 41.53 ^ s and (J2000) = +00^ 30'43.1'' , in the dwarf galaxy SDSS J221241.53+003042.7 (within the SDSS Stripe 82 footprint), as part of a coordinated PTF-JVLA The Jansky Very Large Array is operated by the National Radio Astronomy Observatory (NRAO), which is a facility of the National Science Foundation (NSF), operated under cooperative agreement by Associated Universities, Inc. (AUI). survey It was also independently discovered by the La-Silla Quest (LSQ) and Pan-Starrs1 (PS1) surveys, and designated LSQ 2012dwl and PS1-12baa, respectively. .

An image and a spectrum of the host galaxy obtained by the SDSS prior to explosion are shown in Figure 2. The SN exploded very close to the center of the host galaxy (offset 0.0 N and 0.4 E). We downloaded the host-galaxy spectrum from the SDSS 9th Data Release DR9; Ahn2012 . After correcting for the host-galaxy redshift, we measure integrated emission-line fluxes using standard procedures via splot in IRAF, and follow Perez-Montero Diaz (2003) to compute statistical errors The measured H , H , H , [ O III ] 4959, [ O III ] 5007, and [ N II ] 6584 emission-line fluxes are 34.1 3.3 , 88.5 3.6 , 290.9 3.4 , 81.5 3.2 , 232.7 4.8 , and 10.3 1.0 10^ 17 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 before extinction correction, respectively. Errors include statistical measurement uncertainties . Using the SDSS Petrosian magnitudes, correcting for Galactic and host-galaxy extinction (see below), and applying K corrections via kcorrect (v4.2, Blanton Roweis 2007) at the host-galaxy redshift, we derive an absolute magnitude of M_r=-14.8 mag.

For computing the oxygen abundance (Modjaz et al. 2011, and references therein), we correct the detected emission-line fluxes of [ O III ] , [ N II ] , H , and H for reddening, and employ the scales of Pettini Pagel (2004, PP04-O3N2) and of Kewley Dopita (2002, KD02) to obtain values of 12+ log(O/H) _ PP04-O3N2 =8.12^ +0.04 _ 0.02 and 12+ log(O/H) _ KD02 =8.13^ +0.05 _ 0.04 , respectively. We conclude that the metallicity of the host galaxy is 0.2--0.3 Z _ , having used the solar oxygen abundance of 12+ log(O/H) =8.69 Asplund2009 .

gmw_00004150.name: ID.1
gmw_00004150.overview: ID.1 is a compact starburst galaxy at z ~ 2.54 [arXiv:1607.06771, arXiv:2108.05596] that contains a rotating molecular gas disk [arXiv:2108.05596] and is likely interacting with a nearby western neighbor [arXiv:1903.09167]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1903.09167] with associated X-ray emission [arXiv:1607.06771] and a possible AGN-driven molecular outflow [arXiv:2103.05011].

sources.summary: The paper describes ID.1 as a compact z 2.543 ASPECS CO galaxy with two-color HST structure, compact starlight, nearby foreground galaxies, and modest Chandra X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: ID.1 (Tab. tab_sample ) is a compact galaxy at z 2.5 . momcheva16 report a grism redshift z=2.561 , based on the detection of the line in the 3D-HST data. This redshift is improved by our blind detection of three CO transitions (ASPECS .1, .1, .2; see Paper I), clearly pininng down the redshift to z=2.543 . The HST images show a blue component in the North and a red component in the South (or possibly a single, relatively blue component, partially reddened in the South by a thick dust lane).

A group of bright galaxies is present a few arcsec North of this galaxy, but their spectroscopic redshifts show that the group is in the foreground, with only one other source lying at z 2.5 (the galaxy 2'' West of ID.1). The starlight emission coincident with the CO detection is compact, with a scale radius R_e 1.7 kpc vanderwel12 . Chandra reveals X-ray emission associated with this galaxy. The measured X-ray flux is F_ X =5.7 10^ 17 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 , yielding an X-ray luminosity L_ X =3.0 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 xue11 .

sources.summary: The paper describes UDF3 as a z=2.544 DSFG with smooth CO kinematics consistent with a rotating gas disk, compact millimeter emission, intense star formation, and a starburst classification.

quotes.quote: UDF3 (J033238.53-274634.6) UDF3 is a DSFG at z_ spec =2.544^ +0.001 _ 0.002 (Tab. tab:galaxy_properties ), that we measured from a CO(3-2) line detected in a ALMA B3 ( _ obs 97.6 GHz; see Tabs. tab:COemission ).

The absence of interactions is confirmed by the velocity and dispersion velocity maps of CO(3-2) molecular line, that are smooth (i.e., not disturbed) and consistent with a rotating disc of molecular gas (confirmed by the double-peaked profile of the spectral line; see Fig. fig:panel_CO_3sources ) extending over a radius of 1.4 kpc. The (sub-)millimeter source is resolved in the ALMA B7 continuum map: we measure a circularized radius of _ ALMA 0.6 kpc (Tab. tab:continuum ). However, to confirm this scenario we need multi-wavelength images at higher spatial resolution.

In the analysis presented in Pantoni2021:2021MNRAS.504..928P , we found UDF3 to be a very young object (age 200-300 Myr), forming stars at a rate SFR 500 M _ yr ^ 1 (sSFR 5.8 Gyr ^ 1 ). Fuelled by the large amount of gas and dust (M _ H_2 =(1.5 0.3) 10^ 11 M _ and M _ dust 4 10^8 M _ ; Tabs. tab:CO_gas_mol and tab:galaxy_properties ), its intense burst of star formation (depletion timescale _ depl 100 Myr) must trigger the growth of the central BH, that we expect to become active and quench star formation in a few 10^8 yr. In the SFR M _ plot, UDF3 lies to the top-left side of the main-sequence at the corresponding redshift even if it is still consistent with the 2 scatter of the relation; see Speagle2014:2014ApJS..214...15S, Pantoni2021:2021MNRAS.504..928P . A similar result was found by Elbaz2018:2018A&A...616A.110E , who classify UDF3 as a starburst galaxy.

sources.summary: ASPECS-LP.01 is the field brightest CO emitter, an X-ray AGN, and a likely interacting pair whose redshift is confirmed by MUSE spectroscopy.

quotes.quote: ASPECS-LP.01: CO(3-2) at z=2.543 . The brightest CO line emitter in the field. It is a Chandra /X-ray detected AGN 718 Luo2017 and was already found in the line search at 3 mm and 1 mm in CO(3-2), CO(7-6) and CO(8-7) and continuum in the ASPECS-Pilot ( Walter2016, Decarli2016 , .1, .1, .2; Aravena2016 , C1) as well as at 1 mm and 5 cm continuum UDF3 Dunlop2017, Rujopakarn2016 . The MUSE spectrum (MUSE 35) reveals a high S/N continuum with a wealth of UV absorption features and \ emission, confirming the redshift ( fig:MUSE-spectra-1 ). The source is a (likely interacting) pair with the source 1 5 to the west, MUSE 24, at the same redshift ( v 76 km s ^ 1 ).

sources.summary: UDF3 is described as a highly star-forming ALMA galaxy requiring a burst in SED fits, with unusually disconnected dust and stellar emission and near-starburst status.

quotes.quote: Conversely, for two galaxies (UDF3 and GS6), less than 10 of the dust emission can be explained from the rest-frame UV to NIR fit. We found that a burst must be added to fit the full SED of these galaxies with any of our attenuation laws. We see in Section sec:sfr-mstar that both are the most active galaxies of our sample in star formation.

If we define a starburst as a galaxy with an SFR exceeding the MS by a factor at least four, only one galaxy is found to be starbursting (UDF3). UDF13 is still well below the MS and UDF3 very close to starbursting.

sources.summary: Source 1-BzK is a high-redshift BzK-selected source whose PAH features indicate z=2.55 and whose MIR SED resembles Source 1 without a prominent stellar bump.

quotes.quote: Two of the secondary objects in the slits are of great interest, and are indicated in the figure: (1) an X-ray source detected by Chandra \ in the soft-band only, but lacking an optical redshift, referred to hereafter as Source 2-x; and (2) a source lacking an optical redshift but selected to be at high redshift by the BzK \ selection technique Daddi 2004 , referred to hereafter as Source 1-BzK.

The PAH features in Source 1-BzK confirm that it is at high redshift, indicating z=2.55 0.03 . The uncertainty here is based upon the narrower 6.2 \ PAH emission feature, and excludes the broader 7.7 \ feature. This redshift is consistent with the expectation from the optical photometry.
We also find that source 1-BzK has a MIR SED similar to that of Source 1, lacking a prominent 1.6 \ bump.

sources.summary: The paper identifies UDF3 as an ALMA source whose CO, CI, and H2O lines confirm its redshift, affect its 1.3-mm flux estimate, and mark it as a radio-loud AGN.

quotes.quote: The redshift of one ALMA-identified source (UDF3) is confirmed independently from our ALMA observations, via the detection of 3 spectral lines from CO, CI and H _2 O in our ALMA datacube (see later, and Ivison et al., in preparation).

Finally, we note that, for source UDF3, we have detected molecular emission lines from H _2 O, CO and CI which, as well as confirming its spectroscopic redshift at mm wavelengths, also in this case make a significant contribution to the total flux density given in Table 2. Our best estimate is that removal of the line contribution reduces the flux density of UDF3 from S_ 1.3 = 863 84 Jy to S_ 1.3 = 717 134 Jy , and we use this latter value as approriate for star-formation rate estimates later in this paper.
In this context, we note from Tables 3 and 4 that there appear to be two radio-loud AGN in our sample, UDF3 and UDF7.

sources.summary: The paper identifies ASPECS C1 as the only z>2 millimeter detection, places it on the star-forming main sequence, and reports its dust emissivity and gas fraction.

quotes.quote: Similarly, the only millimeter detection at z>2 (ASPECS C1) is also well within the scatter of the main sequence at z=2.0-2.5 .
For the galaxy individually detected in the 1.2-mm and 3-mm maps (ASPECS C1), we find =1.3 0.2 .
Only the higher redshift galaxy in our sample, ASPECS C1 at z=2.5 , has a value of f_ gas comparable to literature sources at its redshift.

sources.summary: UDF3 is one of the sample sources with CO-derived gas information, and its broad, double-peaked CO profile is interpreted as a possible AGN-driven molecular outflow.

quotes.quote: In Tab. tab_decarli we list the CO-derived H_2 masses for four sources of our sample (UDF1, UDF3, UDF8, ALESS067.1) by Pantoni et al.
AGN feedback can be associate also to the broad ( v 500 km/s), double-peaked CO emission line profile observed for UDF3, UDF8 and ALESS067.1, that may indicate the presence of a molecular outflow.

For four objects we found some signatures of AGN feedback either in the radio band (UDF11), that appears more extended than the FIR one, or by the detection of possible AGN-driven molecular outflows (UDF3, UDF8, ALESS067.1).

sources.summary: The paper uses ALMA 48 as a two-band comparison source, finding its dust parameters remain constrained but with larger uncertainties than better sampled examples.

quotes.quote: We illustrate this for a concrete example in Figure sed_comp , where we compare the fits for ALMA 43, which has 5 ALMA measurements; ALMA 48, which has just 2; and ALMA 60, which is not included in the robust subset due to it only having ALMA 870 m and shorter wavelength data. We also show the marginalized likelihood distributions (histograms) and joint likelihood distributions (contour plot) for and T_d for each source.
For ALMA 48, we find =2.54^ +0.65 _ 0.65 and T_d = 32.2^ +10.4 _ 6.1 K; the parameters are still well-constrained with slightly higher errors.

gmw_00004151.name: ID.2
gmw_00004151.overview: ID.2 is a large, dusty star-forming disk galaxy at a redshift of approximately 1.55 [arXiv:2108.05596, arXiv:1607.06771]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1607.06771, arXiv:1903.09167] and exhibits a double-peaked CO emission line profile that may indicate an AGN-driven molecular outflow [arXiv:2103.05011].

sources.summary: The paper describes UDF8 as a z~1.6 DSFG resolved by ALMA/VLA; optical and CO data indicate a rotating disk, while X-rays show it hosts an AGN.

quotes.quote: UDF8 (J033239.74-274611.4) UDF8 is a DSFG at z _ spec 1.6 .

The source is resolved both in ALMA and VLA maps and the corresponding radii are _ ALMA 4.1 kpc (Tab. tab:continuum ) and _ VLA 2.1 kpc. This dusty galaxy, still compact, is however more extended in the millimeter than the bulk of high-z DSFGs (typical ALMA radius is found to be 1-2 kpc). In the rest-frame UV/optical the galaxy appears to be isolated (see Fig. fig:cont and Tab. tab:summary_resolved ), suggesting that the bulk of star formation can be traced back to local, in-situ condensation processes. The galaxy is more extended in the optical ( _ H 5.7 kpc) than in the millimeter continuum, consistently with what we expect in the gas compaction evolutionary phase. This is confirmed by the double-peaked spectral profile of its CO(2-1) line and the velocity and velocity dispersion maps (Fig. fig:panel_CO_3sources ), that suggest we are observing a rotating disc of molecular gas extending over an area of radius r_ , CO 3.5 kpc.

The source 2-10 keV luminosity is 3.5 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 , suggesting that UDF8 hosts an active galactic nucleus (see Tab. tab:galaxy_properties ), which dominates over the X-ray emission of the host galaxy Pantoni2021:2021MNRAS.504..928P .

sources.summary: The paper presents ID.2 as a large z=1.552 disk galaxy whose CO line matches the slit redshift, with an inclined 8.3 kpc disk and Chandra evidence for an AGN.

quotes.quote: ID.2 has a HST morphology consistent with a large disk galaxy at z =1.552. Its slit redshift z = 1.552 , kurk13 matches well our CO line detection (ASPECS .2), assuming CO(2-1). The disk has an inclination of 60^ (based on the aspect ratio, vanderwel12 ), with an effective radius of 8.3 kpc. The galaxy is detected with Chandra. xue11 report a flux of F_ X =3.6 10^ 15 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 but 2.6 10^ 15 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 in lehmer05 , yielding an X-ray luminosity L_ X =5.5 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 , suggesting that ID.2 hosts an AGN.

sources.summary: Source 2-x is a Chandra soft-band secondary source with a possible PAH-based redshift near 1.55 and an X-ray-to-infrared ratio similar to AGN-dominated SMGs.

quotes.quote: Two of the secondary objects in the slits are of great interest, and are indicated in the figure: (1) an X-ray source detected by Chandra \ in the soft-band only, but lacking an optical redshift, referred to hereafter as Source 2-x; and (2) a source lacking an optical redshift but selected to be at high redshift by the BzK \ selection technique Daddi 2004 , referred to hereafter as Source 1-BzK.

The other secondary source, Source 2-x, shows a single definite emission line at an observed wavelength of _ obs 16 \ , and possibly broad enhanced emission at _ obs 19 \ . If the isolated line were identified with the 6.2 \ PAH feature and the broad emission with the 7.7 \ PAH feature, the object would lie at z 1.55 ,. Identification of the isolated emission line as the 7.7 \ PAH feature (indicated z 1.1 ) is less likely, as that would center the broad emission at rest-frame 9.5 , which might only be explained by strong silicate emission, which is unusual in a source with PAH emission.
Source 2-x has a ratio of 0.007 \ (similar to that of an AGN-dominated SMG) if it is at the adopted redshift.

sources.summary: ASPECS-LP.05 is a massive z=1.550 CO(2-1) galaxy and X-ray AGN detected in multiple ASPECS-Pilot CO and continuum observations.

quotes.quote: ASPECS-LP.05: CO(2-1) at z=1.550 . A massive ( M_ * 10^ 11.5 ) galaxy and an X-ray classified AGN 748 Luo2017 . It was also detected by the ASPECS-Pilot in 1 mm continuum (C2, Aravena2016 ; cf. Dunlop2017 ), in CO(2-1) and also CO(5-4) and CO(6-5) ID.3, Decarli2016 , and in 5 cm continuum UDF8 Rujopakarn2016 . NIR spectroscopy from the SINS survey ForsterSchreiber2009 reveals , confirming the redshift we also find from MUSE, based on the \ and \ absorption features.

sources.summary: UDF8 is included among sources with CO-derived gas masses, and its broad, double-peaked CO profile is treated as evidence for possible AGN-driven molecular outflow.

quotes.quote: In Tab. tab_decarli we list the CO-derived H_2 masses for four sources of our sample (UDF1, UDF3, UDF8, ALESS067.1) by Pantoni et al.
AGN feedback can be associate also to the broad ( v 500 km/s), double-peaked CO emission line profile observed for UDF3, UDF8 and ALESS067.1, that may indicate the presence of a molecular outflow.

For four objects we found some signatures of AGN feedback either in the radio band (UDF11), that appears more extended than the FIR one, or by the detection of possible AGN-driven molecular outflows (UDF3, UDF8, ALESS067.1).

gmw_00004152.name: ID.3
gmw_00004152.overview: ID.3 is a high-redshift spiral galaxy located at a redshift of z = 1.382 [arXiv:1903.09167, arXiv:1607.06771]. It forms an overlapping pair with a foreground spiral galaxy, and it has an estimated radius of 8.3 kpc [arXiv:1607.06771].

sources.summary: ASPECS-LP.08 is a complex z=1.382 CO(2-1) system where foreground spirals may obscure the true CO source, making its association uncertain.

quotes.quote: ASPECS-LP.08: CO(2-1) at z=1.382 . The source has a more complex morphology which was already discussed in the ASPECS-Pilot program ( Decarli2016 , see their Fig. 3). The CO emission is spatially consistent with a system of spiral galaxies. MUSE reveals that the south-west spiral is in the foreground at z=1.087 . Careful examination of the MUSE cube reveals \ emission matching the CO redshift in an arc north of the galaxies and possibly towards the south-west, which is 1.8 away of peak of the CO emission ( 15 kpc at the redshift of the source).

sources.summary: The paper identifies ID.3 as the northern member of an overlapping spiral pair, associating its bright CO emission with z=1.382 and estimating an HST radius of 8.3 kpc.

quotes.quote: ID.3 and ID.4 are the two components of an apparent pair of overlapping spiral galaxies. The southern component exibits bright emission at 7784 (see Fig. fig_muse_dspiral ), clearly placing it at z = 1.088 (in agreement with the CO redshift of ASPECS .5); the northern component shows bright CO emission (ASPECS .3) which could be interpreted as CO(2-1) at z = 1.382 . Our careful analysis of the MUSE data around 8880 reveals faint emission (although contaminated by sky line emission), supporting the CO identification (see Fig. fig_muse_dspiral ).

The disk of ID.4 has a scale radius of 5.8 kpc based on HST imaging vanderwel12 ; for ID.3, the estimated radius is 8.3 kpc (but the overlap with the southern component may partially affect this estimate).

gmw_00004153.name: ID.4
gmw_00004153.overview: ID.4 is a spiral galaxy located at a redshift of z = 1.088 [arXiv:1607.06771]. It forms an overlapping galaxy pair with a northern companion and features a disk with a scale radius of 5.8 kpc [arXiv:1607.06771].

sources.summary: The paper treats ID.4 as the southern galaxy in an overlapping spiral pair, placing it at z=1.088 from emission lines and CO, with a 5.8 kpc disk and X-ray upper limit.

quotes.quote: ID.3 and ID.4 are the two components of an apparent pair of overlapping spiral galaxies. The southern component exibits bright emission at 7784 (see Fig. fig_muse_dspiral ), clearly placing it at z = 1.088 (in agreement with the CO redshift of ASPECS .5);

The disk of ID.4 has a scale radius of 5.8 kpc based on HST imaging vanderwel12 ; for ID.3, the estimated radius is 8.3 kpc (but the overlap with the southern component may partially affect this estimate). ID.4 appears as an upper limit in the X--ray catalog by xue11 ( F_ X <6.7 10^ 17 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 , L_ X <4.3 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 ).

gmw_00004154.name: ID.5
gmw_00004154.overview: ID.5 is a galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z=1.096 [arXiv:1903.09167] to z=1.098 [arXiv:1607.06771] in a crowded region of the sky [arXiv:1607.06771, arXiv:1903.09167]. It is classified as an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1903.09167] and is part of a redshift overdensity in the Hubble Ultra Deep Field [arXiv:1903.09167].

sources.summary: ASPECS-LP.15 is a z=1.096 CO(2-1) galaxy in a crowded field, X-ray classified as an AGN, and previously detected in ASPECS-Pilot CO.

quotes.quote: ASPECS-LP.15: CO(2-1) at z=1.096 . Part of an overdensity in the HUDF at the same redshift. The source lies in a very crowded part of the sky with multiple galaxies at different redshifts overlapping in projection. Detected in X-rays, classified as AGN 689 Luo2017 . Source was also covered by the ASPECS-Pilot program and detected in CO(2-1) and CO(4-3) ID.5 Decarli2016 .

sources.summary: The paper describes ID.5 as a crowded-field galaxy whose MUSE redshift revision to z=1.098 matches two marginal CO lines not selected by the blind search.

quotes.quote: ID.5 and ID.8 lie in a crowded region of our field. skelton14 report a spectroscopic redshift z = 1.047 for ID.5. However, the inspection of the MUSE data reveals two, clearly distinguished line sets of the doublet, at z = 1.038 and z = 1.098 . The latter matches the redshift of two CO lines which are slightly too faint to be selected in our blind search (S/N 4.8, see Paper I).

gmw_00004155.name: ID.6
gmw_00004155.overview: ID.6 is a high-redshift galaxy detected in the continuum [arXiv:1607.06771]. It is located four arcseconds east of ID.3 and likely belongs to a common physical structure at a redshift of approximately 1.09 [arXiv:1607.06771].

sources.summary: The paper places ID.6 near ID.3 in a likely common z~1.09 structure, reporting continuum detection and tentative CO(2-1) and CO(4-3) excesses.

quotes.quote: ID.6 is located 4'' East of ID.3, and probably belongs to a common physical structure (together with other galaxies with a spectroscopic z 1.09 ). It is detected in the continuum, and its CO spectrum shows a 3 excess at the frequency of the expected CO(2-1) line. The CO(4-3) transition is also detected with similar significance, although the best gaussian fit of the line suggests a velocity shift of 200 between the two transition. This is likely due to the poor S/N of the two lines.

gmw_00004156.name: ID.7
gmw_00004156.overview: ID.7 is a very compact galaxy located at a redshift of z = 1.221 [arXiv:1607.06771]. The galaxy hosts a bright active galactic nucleus detected in X-rays, but it is not detected in the dust continuum [arXiv:1607.06771].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes ID.7 as a very compact z=1.221 source with a bright Chandra AGN, no dust continuum detection, and a tentative CO(2-1) excess.

quotes.quote: ID.7 appears as a very compact source ( R_ e =0.7 kpc) at z = 1.221 . Its Chandra image reveals the presence of a bright AGN ( F_ X =1.01 10^ 14 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 in lehmer05 ; 8.3 10^ 15 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 in evans10 ; 6.3 10^ 15 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 in xue11 ), yielding an X-ray luminosity of L_ X =5.4 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 ). It is not detected in the dust continuum. A 3- excess is measured at the expected frequency of the CO(2-1) transition.

gmw_00004157.name: ID.8
gmw_00004157.overview: ID.8 is a galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.999 [arXiv:1607.06771]. It exhibits faint X-ray emission that is consistent with a starburst rather than an active galactic nucleus, though no CO emission has been detected at its position [arXiv:1607.06771].

sources.summary: The paper finds ID.8 at z=0.999 with no CO detection in the covered transition, but reports faint X-ray emission consistent with a starburst rather than an AGN.

quotes.quote: ID.8 on the other hand is found at another redshift ( z = 0.999 ). No CO emission is found at this position and frequency, although the lowest-J transition that we encompass is CO(4-3) at . ID.8 is detected in the X--rays xue11 . Its faintness ( F_ X =8.2 10^ 17 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 , L_ X =4.3 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 ) seems consistent with a starburst rather than an AGN ranalli03 .

gmw_00004158.name: ID.9
gmw_00004158.overview: ID.9 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 2.3 [arXiv:1607.06771]. It is a faint infrared source that structurally appears as a compact bulge with no detected CO transitions [arXiv:1607.06771].

sources.summary: The paper describes ID.9 as a faint z~2.3 sample galaxy just above the infrared luminosity cut, appearing as a compact bulge with no detected covered CO transitions.

quotes.quote: ID.9 and ID.10 are both at z 2.3 . They are among the faintest galaxies in our sample in terms of L_ IR , just above the 10^ 11 cut. ID.9 appears as a compact bulge.
ASPECS data cover 3 CO transitions in these galaxies: CO(3-2), CO(7-6), and CO(8-7). None of these lines is detected.

gmw_00004159.name: ID.10
gmw_00004159.overview: ID.10 is a faint, infrared-selected galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 2.3 [arXiv:1607.06771]. It exhibits a spiral structure with a disturbed morphology, but it shows no detections in the three carbon monoxide transitions covered by ASPECS [arXiv:1607.06771].

sources.summary: The paper describes ID.10 as a faint z~2.3 infrared-selected galaxy with disturbed spiral morphology and no detections in the three CO transitions covered by ASPECS.

quotes.quote: ID.9 and ID.10 are both at z 2.3 . They are among the faintest galaxies in our sample in terms of L_ IR , just above the 10^ 11 cut.
ID.10 appears as a spiral galaxy with disturbed morphology. ASPECS data cover 3 CO transitions in these galaxies: CO(3-2), CO(7-6), and CO(8-7). None of these lines is detected.

gmw_00004160.name: UM 408
gmw_00004160.overview: UM 408 is a compact H II galaxy with a projected size of approximately one kiloparsec [arXiv:0904.1966, arXiv:1109.2081]. Its central starburst is resolved into giant star-forming regions arranged in a ring-like geometry [arXiv:0904.1966, arXiv:1109.2081], which exhibit a chemically homogeneous oxygen abundance [arXiv:0904.1966, arXiv:1309.6231].

sources.summary: GMOS-IFU data resolve UM 408's central starburst into coeval giant HII regions, with displaced dust and oxygen abundances showing no significant gradient, consistent with chemical homogeneity.

quotes.quote: We present Integral Field Unit GMOS-IFU data of the compact HII galaxy UM 408, obtained at Gemini South telescope, in order to derive the spatial distribution of emission lines and line ratios, kinematics, plasma parameters, and oxygen abundances as well the integrated properties over an area of 3 4 .4 equivalent with 750 1100 pc located in the central part of the galaxy. The starburst in this area is resolved into two giant regions of about 1 .5 and 1 ( 375 and 250 pc) diameter, respectively and separated 1.5-2 ( 500 pc). The extinction distribution concentrate its highest values close but not coincident with the maxima of H emission around each one of the detected regions. This indicates that the dust has been displaced from the exciting clusters by the action of their stellar winds. The ages of these two regions, estimated using H equivalent widths, suggest that they are coeval events of 5 Myr with stellar masses of 10 ^ 4 M _ .

The spatial distribution of oxygen abundance does not show any significant gradient across the galaxy. On the other hand, the bulk of data points are lying in a region of 2 dispersion (with =0.1 dex) around the average value, confirming that this compact HII galaxy as other previously studied dwarf irregular galaxies is chemically homogeneous. Therefore, the new metals processed and injected by the current star formation episode are possibly not observed and reside in the hot gas phase, whereas the metals from previous events are well mixed and homogeneously distributed through the whole extent of the galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes UM 408 as a compact galaxy with two young central giant regions, well-mixed ISM metallicity, ring-like star-forming regions, and weak regular Br emission.

quotes.quote: UM 408 is a compact galaxy with a projected size of 1kpc. Although this galaxy was resolved as a single H ii region in previous studies e.g., G03 , two giant regions in the central part of the galaxy were detected by L09 , with ages of 5 Myr and stellar masses of 10 ^4 M _ . Using GMOS-IFU observations L09 showed that the metal content in the ISM of this galaxy is well mixed and homogeneously distributed throughout the galaxy, in the same way as in other dwarf galaxies.

The galaxy UM 408 appears quite compact and regular, and the K _ p band image shows that the star-forming regions are distributed in a clearly defined ring-like geometry, composed of five almost regularly spaced regions where the current SF is occurring. The largest region is located in the eastern part of the galaxy. We observe the presence of another region outside the central part of the galaxy. The Br emission in this galaxy is very regular and weak.

sources.summary: The review reports spatially resolved abundance work on UM 408, finding a marginal outward abundance decrease but no statistically significant localized oxygen overabundance from young star clusters.

quotes.quote: In we compare the spatial distribution of 12+log(O/H), found in , with the position of the star cluster/complexes detected in the galaxy UM 408 by using high resolution near-IR K _ p band images. We found that the variation of the observed data points (see Figure 9 in and Figure 1 in ) may not be statistically significant, indicating that these regions have identical chemical properties within the errors. It is interesting to note that we observed a marginal gradient of decreasing abundance from the center outward in UM 408, indicating that the highest abundance values are found near the peak of H emission and extinction (H ), and coincident with the position of the brightest star cluster/complex .

In summary, the results obtained in our studies suggest that the chemical properties (O, N and N/O) across H ii /BCD galaxies are fairly uniform, although a slight gradient of O and N are observed in the ISM of UM 408 and Tol 2146-391, respectively.

gmw_00004161.name: CLASS B1152+199
gmw_00004161.overview: CLASS B1152+199 is a strong gravitational lens system where a foreground star-forming galaxy at redshift 0.439 lenses a background radio-loud quasar [arXiv:2007.01440, arXiv:2401.04475]. The foreground lensing galaxy exhibits a high dust-to-gas ratio [arXiv:1306.0008] and produces two lensed images of the background quasar's jet [arXiv:1811.06053].

sources.summary: The paper uses CLASS B1152+199, a galaxy gravitational lens system with a foreground star-forming lens galaxy, to constrain axion-like particles through polarization and time-delay measurements.

quotes.quote: The lens system CLASS B1152+199 is a highly suitable candidate for probing ALPs and has been studied in detail in the literature providing relevant information. CLASS B1152+199 is a galaxy gravitational lens system wherein a foreground star-forming galaxy at redshift z_ gal =0.439 lenses a background linearly polarised quasar at redshift z_ qso =1.019 . Using an isothermal sphere mass distribution model for the lensing galaxy, the best-fit time delay t_ obs is estimated to be 27.8 days , which corresponds to t = 13.3 day in the frame of the quasar Note that, computed t_ obs = 39.7 day for the assumed Hubble-Lema itre constant H_0 = 100 km s^ 1 Mpc^ 1 . Here, we have corrected the estimated time delay using H_0 = 67.4 km s^ 1 Mpc^ 1 obtained from the Planck satellite's data and used t_ obs = 27.8 day in our calculations. . Broad-band polarisation observations of this system, covering the frequency range 1 to 8 GHz, were performed using the Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array (VLA) to constrain the magnetic field geometry in the lensing galaxy .

It was found that the polarised emission of each of the images are composed of two polarisation emitting components which undergo Faraday rotation and depolarisation in the magnetised plasma of the lensing galaxy. These polarised components, denoted as component 1 and 2, remained unresolved in the VLA observations at up to 0.5 arcsec . From milli-arcsec resolution observations , these polarised components are physically separated by <30 pc.

sources.summary: The paper fits B 1152+199 with a variable-metallicity absorption model, updates its dust-to-gas ratio, and finds that ratio high compared with the redshift-based evolution track.

quotes.quote: We also fit this second absorption model to an archival spectrum of B 1152+199 (Dai Kochanek 2009) which has a similarly high S/N. We are able to independently constrain N_H and the metallicity, with the results given in Table tab:spectra . For these three lenses, we show the joint confidence contours for the differential absorption N_H and the metallicity in Figure fig:total_metal . The metallicity is still degenerate with the hydrogen column density, especially in B 1152+199. The best fit metallicities differ from the solar value but are still consistent with it given the uncertainties.
We also updated the dust-to-gas ratio of B 1152+199 using the new value of differential absorption column density obtained from a spectral fit allowing varying metallicity.

However, the dust-to-gas ratio of B 1152+199 is about a factor of 2 higher than the value predicted by the evolution track corresponding to its redshift z=0.439, and is inconsistent with the model of Inoue (2003) by more than 3 .

sources.summary: The paper revisits B1152+199, a strongly lensed quasar with two jet images, using multi-epoch VLBI data and finds no convincing milliarcsecond curvature, favoring beam artifacts over millilensing.

quotes.quote: Previous Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) observations of the quasar B1152+199 at 5 GHz has revealed two images of a strongly lensed jet with seemingly discordant morphologies. Whereas the jet appears straight in one of the images, the other exhibits slight curvature on milliarcsecond scales. This is unexpected from the lensing solution and has been interpreted as possible evidence for secondary, small--scale lensing (millilensing) by a compact object with a mass of 10^5 - 10^7 \ M_ located close to the curved image.

Here, we revisit the case for millilensing in B1152+199 by combining new global--VLBI data at 8.4 GHz with two datasets from the European VLBI Network (EVN) at 5 GHz (archival) and at 22 GHz (new dataset), and the previously published 5 GHz Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) data. We find that the new data with a more circular synthesized beam, exhibits no apparent milliarcsecond--scale curvature in image B. Various observations of the object spanning 15 years apart enable us to improve the constraints on lens system (thanks also to the improved astrometry resulting from 22 GHz observations) to the point that the only plausible explanation left for the apparent curvature is the artifact due to the shape of the synthesized beam.

sources.summary: The paper describes CLASS B1152+200 as a galaxy-scale strong lens, giving its discovery context, lens or source properties, image configuration, and relevant environmental or model information for the survey.

quotes.quote: CLASS B1152+200 (herein B1152) is a two-image gravitational lens discovered in the Cosmic Lens All-Sky Survey CLASS, browne03, myers03 . A z_s=1.0189 0.0004 quasar is lensed by a z_l=0.4386 0.0008 galaxy. The lens galaxy spectrum shows prominent [ OII ] emission, suggesting that the lens is a late-type galaxy. The image separation is 1.6 . The lens galaxy is faint and difficult to de-convolve from the lensed images, thus little is known about the lens itself and lens models are very unconstrained.

sources.summary: The paper identifies B1152+200 as a strong-radio QSO in the NOEMA sample and suggests its central AGN may have been active longer than most targets.

quotes.quote: Three of our sources, B1608+656, B1152+200, and B1600+434, have strong jet--dominated radio emission browne2003 .
These two sources, together with B1152+200, are QSOs with strong radio emission Stacey2018 .

Instead, it is likely that the cumulative effects of several episodes of AGN activity are necessary to significantly depress the gas fractions piotrowska2022 , and could indicate that the central AGN in B1608+656, B1152+200 and B1600+434 have been active for a longer period of time compared to the rest of the sample.

gmw_00004162.name: J2240-0927
gmw_00004162.overview: J2240-0927 is a compact, spherical Seyfert-2 galaxy [arXiv:1211.7098, arXiv:1607.06481] powered by an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1211.7098]. It features an exceptionally luminous, galaxy-wide narrow-line region [arXiv:1211.7098] and exhibits globally disturbed gas kinematics [arXiv:1211.7098].

sources.summary: The paper presents J2240-0927 as the Green Bean prototype, a Seyfert-2 galaxy with an exceptionally luminous, galaxy-wide NLR, AGN-powered ionization, disturbed gas kinematics, and unusual extended emission.

quotes.quote: The main focus of this paper is on a detailed analysis of the GB prototype galaxy J2240-0927 ( z=0.326 ). Its NLR extends over 26 44 kpc and is surrounded by an extended narrow-line region (ENLR). With a total [ O III ] 5008 luminosity of (5.7 0.9) 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 , this is one of the most luminous NLR known around any type-2 galaxy.

Using VLT/XSHOOTER we show that the NLR is powered by an AGN, and we derive resolved extinction, density and ionization maps. Gas kinematics is disturbed on a global scale, and high velocity outflows are absent or faint. This NLR is unlike any other NLR or extended emission line region (EELR) known.

sources.summary: J2240 0927 is treated with 3D spectroscopy and XSHOOTER data; the paper describes a compact 9 kpc spherical galaxy and models its stellar and nebular continuum.

quotes.quote: dst15 have presented a 3D spectroscopic study of J2240 0927. The continuum maps reveal a compact spherical galaxy with 9 kpc diameter. Our VLT/XSHOOTER spectrum sdh13 of the nucleus covers the 2500-18800 restframe range. It is corrected for galactic extinction using a R_V=3.1 dust model and the extinction maps of scf11 , de-redshifted, and we subtract the nebular continuum for a 15000 K hydrogen helium gas mix in ionization equilibrium (see also Section nebcontinuum ).

gmw_00004163.name: XID 7
gmw_00004163.overview: XID 7 is a high-redshift active galactic nucleus located near the Spiderweb Galaxy [arXiv:2203.02208]. It has a spectroscopic redshift of z=2.1447, a Compton-thin X-ray spectrum with high intrinsic absorption, and a luminosity in the quasar range [arXiv:2203.02208].

sources.summary: XID 7 is identified as a candidate H emitter near the Spiderweb Galaxy, with spectroscopic redshift z=2.1447, high intrinsic X-ray absorption, and QSO-range luminosity.

quotes.quote: XID 7 This source has been identified as a candidate H emitter in 2004aKurk (ID=272), and a photometric redshift of z=2.18^ +0.08 _ 0.05 (ID=493) has been measured by 2013Tanaka . 2011Kuiper investigated the NIR spectrum with SINFONI (see their ID 12), and found a spectroscopic redshift of z=2.1447 0.0023 from the O [ III ] line, observed with a flux of 8.2 1.5 10^ 17 erg/s/cm ^2 / . In the X-ray, this source shows a Compton thin spectrum with the highest intrinsic absorption in the sample ( 4 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 ), and a luminosity in the QSO range ( L_ 0.5-10 keV 7.5 10^ 44 erg/s). It is the X-ray source closest to the Spiderweb Galaxy.

gmw_00004164.name: XID 9
gmw_00004164.overview: XID 9 is a high-redshift Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy [arXiv:2203.02208]. Spectroscopic observations have detected both Lyman-alpha and NV emission lines from the source [arXiv:2203.02208].

sources.summary: XID 9 is a Ly-emitter counterpart confirmed by optical and Keck spectroscopy, with measured Ly flux, revised line width, and detected NV emission.

quotes.quote: XID 9 The optical counterpart of source XID 9 has been originally selected as a candidate Ly emitter. The optical spectrum is shown in 2002Pentericci (ID=1612), where the Ly line has been measured to have a flux of 3.9 10^ 17 erg/s/cm ^2 , with an EW>30 and a FWHM = 490 km/s This source has been confirmed also with Keck spectroscopy by 2005Croft (ID=L675) and the Ly has been revised to have FWHM = 200 30 km/s with a flux of 6.4 10^ 17 erg/s/cm ^2 . The NV line is also detected with FWHM = 200 30 km/s for a flux of 4.1 10^ 17 erg/s/cm ^2 .

gmw_00004165.name: XID 12
gmw_00004165.overview: XID 12 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z=2.1628 [arXiv:2203.02208]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus in the Seyfert-like regime with negligible intrinsic X-ray absorption and potentially extended emission associated with radio jets [arXiv:2203.02208].

sources.summary: XID 12 has Subaru redshift z=2.1628, negligible intrinsic X-ray absorption, the lowest member X-ray luminosity but still Seyfert-like, and possibly extended emission related to radio jets.

quotes.quote: XID 12 The redshift of XID 12 (z=2.1628) has been obtained with Subaru in 2014Shimakawa (ID=71). The X-ray spectrum is consistent with negligible intrinsic absorption, while the total luminosity L_ 0.5-10 keV 2.2 10^ 43 erg/s is the lowest among the X-ray protocluster member, but still in the Seyfert-like regime. This source has the largest offset from its optical counterpart, due to the extended nature of its emission as noted in the text. Possible association of the extended emission with radio jets will be investigated when the wide-field, high-resolution radio images are available.

gmw_00004166.name: XID 34
gmw_00004166.overview: XID 34 is a dusty, far-infrared-luminous galaxy located at a redshift of z=2.1690 [arXiv:2203.02208]. It is associated with a possible subgroup and is tentatively classified as a Compton-thick active galactic nucleus due to its extremely flat X-ray spectrum [arXiv:2203.02208].

sources.summary: XID 34 is a dusty FIR-luminous z=2.1690 source associated with DKB12a, showing high star-formation estimates and a tentative Compton-thick AGN classification from its flat X-ray spectrum.

quotes.quote: XID 34 This source has been identified with APEX LABOCA in 2014Dannerbauer (ID=DKB12a) with a flux S_ 870 =5.0 1.4 mJy for a S/N= 3.6 . It also has a strong detection at 24 with S_ 24 = 303.4 5.0 Jy and significant detection in the range 160 - 500 Jy, but it only have an upper limit at 1.4 GHz with JVLA. There are four H emitters consistent with the LABOCA position, however DKB12a is the closest to the X-ray position. The H and FIR-SED derived SFR are 240 and 860 M_ /yr, respectively, suggesting the presence of dust.

Eventually, a redshift of z=2.1690 was measured with Subaru in 2014Shimakawa (ID 29). 2018Shimakawa identified a possible subgroup at the position of this source. The source was also detected in the CO line in the COALAS survey see their Table 3 2021Jin , with an offset of 2 arcsec between the ATCA and X-ray centroids. The high FIR luminosity is consistent with the X-ray tentative classification as a Compton-thick AGN, due to an extremely flat (if not inverted) power-law spectrum.

gmw_00004167.name: XID 36
gmw_00004167.overview: XID 36 is classified as a quasar with Ly̑, SiIV, and CIV emission lines in its optical spectrum [arXiv:2203.02208]. The source exhibits high X-ray luminosity with moderate X-ray absorption, and its Ly̑ and CIV lines display double-peaked profiles caused by neutral, metal-enriched gas absorption [arXiv:2203.02208].

sources.summary: XID 36 is classified as a QSO with Ly, SiIV, and CIV emission, absorption-shaped line profiles, high X-ray luminosity, and moderate X-ray absorption.

quotes.quote: XID 36 The optical counterpart of source XID 36 was originally selected as a Ly emitter with B=24.8 . The optical spectrum is shown in 2002Pentericci , where the source was classified as a QSO (ID=1687). Its spectrum shows Ly , SiIV and CIV emission lines. The Ly line was measured to have a flux of 56 10^ 17 erg/s/cm ^2 , with an EW=164 and a FWHM = 5800 km/s. The Ly and CIV lines have a double-peaked profile due to absorption by neutral, metal-enriched gas.

The QSO nature of this source is confirmed by the high X-ray luminosity ( L_ 0.5-10 keV 3.1 10^ 44 erg/s). The source shows a moderate absorption in the X-ray at the level of 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 , but also shows hints of a strong Fe line with rest-frame EW 0.22 keV, despite with large uncertainties.

gmw_00004168.name: XID 73
gmw_00004168.overview: XID 73 is an extremely red object candidate and an unconfirmed member of an X-ray-emitting protocluster [arXiv:2203.02208]. In the X-ray band, the source appears as an obscured Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:2203.02208].

sources.summary: XID 73 is an ERO candidate and unconfirmed X-ray-emitting protocluster member whose X-ray properties make it appear as an obscured Seyfert galaxy.

quotes.quote: XID 73 The source was identified with an ERO candidate in 2004aKurk with ID=463. It is the only X-ray-emitting protocluster member in our catalog that has not been spectroscopically confirmed. In the X-ray, it appears as an obscured Seyfert galaxy.

gmw_00004169.name: XID 74
gmw_00004169.overview: XID 74 is a quiescent galaxy at redshift z=2.172 that experienced an intense burst of star formation in its past [arXiv:2203.02208]. It currently exhibits a low star formation rate and hosts an active galactic nucleus with an X-ray spectrum consistent with a Type I or moderately obscured Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:2203.02208].

sources.summary: XID 74 is described as a quiescent z=2.172 galaxy with an old burst-formed stellar population, low present star formation, and a Type I or moderately obscured Seyfert-like X-ray spectrum.

quotes.quote: XID 74 This source is classified as a quiescent galaxy despite it being detected in H probably due to the presence of the AGN that nevertheless does not contribute significantly to the overall SED. This source is investigated in detail in 2010Doherty where they measure it to have z=2.172 (ID 456) and to be consistent with being formed in an intense burst of star formation, with a derived age of 1.6^ +1.1 _ 0.7 Gyr. The low current star formation rate <1.0 M_ /yr derived from the SED fit confirms that the galaxy is now in a quiescent phase. The X-ray spectrum is consistent with a Type I or moderately obscured Seyfert galaxy. Also, the neutral Fe emission line is detected with EW 0.5 keV, despite with significant uncertainties.

gmw_00004170.name: XID 75
gmw_00004170.overview: XID 75 is an active galactic nucleus with an X-ray spectrum typical of an unobscured quasar [arXiv:2203.02208]. The galaxy exhibits a very broad H-alpha emission line that is not associated with star formation [arXiv:2203.02208].

sources.summary: XID 75 is an H-emitter AGN whose very broad H line is not attributed to star formation, and whose X-ray spectrum is typical of an unobscured QSO.

quotes.quote: XID 75 This source was selected as an H emitter candidate in 2004aKurk and eventually spectroscopically confirmed 2004bKurk (ID=215). Its spectrum shows continuum emission plus a very broad line which almost covers the full spectral range, and it is identified with H . For this AGN the N [ II ] line is blended with the very broad H line and impossible to discern. The measured FWHM of the line is 5300 800 km/s for a flux of 46.2 8.8 10^ 17 erg/s/cm ^2 and an EW=150 30 . The H emission is therefore not considered to be associated to star formation rate.
The X-ray spectrum is typical of an unobscured QSO, with L_ 0.5-10 keV 5.2 10^ 44 erg/s, consistent with the presence of the broad optical line.

gmw_00004171.name: XID 80
gmw_00004171.overview: XID 80 is an active galactic nucleus at a redshift of z=2.149 that is classified as an obscured Type II quasar [arXiv:2203.02208]. The galaxy is detected in submillimeter, infrared, and radio bands, exhibiting high star formation rates and a very strong, extended Lyman-alpha emission [arXiv:2203.02208].

sources.summary: XID 80 is an AGN with broad Ly and multiple emission lines, submillimeter, infrared, and radio detections, high inferred star formation, and an obscured Type II QSO X-ray spectrum.

quotes.quote: XID 80 Source XID 80 has been identified with source ID 16 in 2002Pentericci as a candidate Ly emitter encompassed by a very strong and extended Ly emission, and therefore classified as an AGN. Eventually its spectrum was obtained by 2005Croft at z=2.149 (ID X16/L778d). The spectrum shows a broad Ly (rest-frame deconvolved FWHM 890 km/s), and several emission lines such as NV ( 1240), CIV ( 1549), HeII ( 1640), CIII ] ( 1909), [ CII ] ( 2326), and MgII ( 2798), all with FWHM ranging from 1200 to 2000 km/s see Table 2 of 2005Croft . This source was also identified with APEX LABOCA at 870 m with S_ 870 m =4.2 1.4 mJy for a S/N= 2.9 in 2014Dannerbauer (ID=DKB16). Despite the faint signal, this source is clearly detected in IR and radio bands (from S_ 24 =572.1 5.0 Jy to S_ 1.4GHz =76.2 19.0 Jy with the VLA (see their Table 2).

The estimated SFR is 1140 M_ /yr and 830 M_ /yr from the H and FIR SED, respectively, and the total FIR luminosity is measured to be 4.8 10^ 12 L_ . The X-ray spectrum shows that this source is an obscured Type II QSO with with an unobscured luminosity L_ 0.5-10 keV 7.4 10^ 44 erg/s. Finally, this source has the second-largest offset from its optical counterpart due to a possible extended nature of the emission as noted in the text.

gmw_00004172.name: XID 86
gmw_00004172.overview: XID 86 is a faint, extended extremely red object located at a redshift of z=2.162 [arXiv:2203.02208]. It exhibits faint Lyman-alpha emission and is classified as a candidate Compton-thick active galactic nucleus based on its hard X-ray spectrum and neutral iron emission line [arXiv:2203.02208].

sources.summary: XID 86 is a faint extended ERO at the Spiderweb redshift, spectroscopically confirmed with Ly emission, and interpreted from hard X-rays and Fe emission as a Compton-thick AGN candidate.

quotes.quote: XID 86 Source XID 86 is identified in 2002Pentericci (Source ID=5) with a faint extended ERO with R-K = 5.8 ( I-K = 5.3 ) consistent with having a redshift close to the Spiderweb Galaxy. Eventually, the source was spectroscopically confirmed by 2005Croft at z=2.162 (ID 5Xd). Faint but significant Ly emission (rest-frame deconvolved FWHM 400 km/s) has been detected. It was not possible to classify this source as a starburst or an AGN, but the X-ray luminosity provides strong evidence for the presence of an AGN, as already noted in the literature 2002Pentericci,2005Croft .

In the X-ray, this source 86 shows a flat spectrum ( 1 ), with hardness ratio HR 0 . We consider it a candidate Compton-thick source, with the X-ray spectrum dominated by a reflection component, and an estimated intrinsic luminosity L_ 2-10 keV 3.4 10^ 44 erg/s. The detection of the neutral Fe emission line with EW 0.50 keV strengthen the Compton-thick hypothesis.

gmw_00004173.name: XID 87
gmw_00004173.overview: XID 87 is an extremely red object and hydrogen-alpha emitter located within a protocluster [arXiv:2203.02208]. The galaxy hosts a Type I active galactic nucleus and has been detected in both CO(1-0) and CO(3-2) molecular gas lines [arXiv:2203.02208].

sources.summary: XID 87 is an ERO and H-emitter protocluster source with ALMA and COALAS CO detections; its steep X-ray spectrum is typical of a Type I AGN.

quotes.quote: XID 87 Source XID 87 was selected as an ERO candidate in 2004aKurk with ID=284 and identified as an H emitter in 2018Shimakawa (ID=48). The redshift was measured thanks to a ALMA CO(3–2) observation presented in 2019Tadaki . The CO(3-2) line has a redshift of 2.157 with a FWHM=232 km/s. This source was also detected in the CO(1-0) line in the COALAS survey see their Table 3 2021Jin . The X-ray spectrum shows the steepest slope in the sample, and is typical of a Type I AGN, with L_ 0.5-10 keV 3.3 10^ 43 erg/s.

gmw_00004174.name: XID 90
gmw_00004174.overview: XID 90 is an H-emitter candidate that has been detected in the CO line [arXiv:2203.02208]. Its X-ray spectrum is typical of a Type II active galactic nucleus with a Seyfert-like luminosity, and it has two possible counterparts associated with a protocluster [arXiv:2203.02208].

sources.summary: XID 90 is an H-emitter candidate with CO detection and two possible protocluster counterparts; its X-ray spectrum is typical of a Type II AGN with Seyfert-like luminosity.

quotes.quote: XID 90 This source was originally selected as an H emitter candidate in 2004aKurk with ID=154. It was also detected in the CO line in the COALAS survey see their Table 3 2021Jin . We note that the X-ray centroid is closer to a nearby source that nevertheless has similar color and preliminary photo-z consistent with being in the protocluster. This is the only X-ray source in our sample that has two possible counterparts, both consistent with being in the protocluster, but with only one spectroscopically confirmed. The X-ray spectrum is typical of Type II AGN, with a Seyfert-like unabsorbed luminosity of L_ 0.5-10 keV 4.6 10^ 43 erg/s.

gmw_00004175.name: ID: 25
gmw_00004175.overview: ID: 25 is a Seyfert galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of z = 0.3822, where it is associated with a galaxy cluster [arXiv:2210.06482]. While its host galaxy is visible in NIRCam imaging, MIRI detects only the active galactic nucleus point source, with colors consistent with a star-forming galaxy [arXiv:2210.06482].

sources.summary: ID: 25 is a cluster-associated z=0.3822 Seyfert galaxy whose host is seen in NIRCam, while MIRI detects the AGN point source and colors resembling a star-forming galaxy.

quotes.quote: We detect one Seyfert galaxy ( L_ 4 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 at 10^ 14 Hz) with a spectroscopic redshift which is associated with the cluster ( z = 0.3822 ; ID: 25; marked with the red circle in Figure fig: rgb ). While the host galaxy is visible in NIRCam imaging, only the AGN can be see in MIRI imaging as a point source. The location of this galaxy in every color-color diagram is consistent with that of a SFG at that redshift. This is in agreement with the recent MIRI MRS spectra of Seyfert galaxies 2022arXiv220811620G,2022arXiv220906741L,2022arXiv221004647D which demonstrated that AGN PAH emission can closely resemble that of SFGs.

gmw_00004176.name: J 0659+1629
gmw_00004176.overview: J 0659+1629 is a strong gravitational lens system featuring a lensed background quasar at a redshift of approximately 3.09 [arXiv:2206.04696, arXiv:2206.07714]. The lensing galaxy, located at a redshift of 0.766 [arXiv:2206.04696], is accompanied by a nearby satellite component [arXiv:2209.03094] and produces four lensed quasar images [arXiv:2106.11060, arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper uses Keck adaptive-optics imaging of strong lens J 0659+1629 to reconstruct the PSF, subtract quasar images, model lens light, and reach sub-milliarcsecond astrometric precision.

quotes.quote: We analyze adaptive optics (AO) images of the strong lens system J 0659+1629 obtained with the W. M. Keck Observatory using the laser guide star AO system. We show that by using a reconstructed point spread function we can i) obtain astrometric precision of < 1 mas, which is more than sufficient for time-delay cosmography; and ii) subtract all point-like images resulting in residuals consistent with the noise level. The method we have developed is not limited to strong lensing, and is generally applicable to a wide range of scientific cases that have multiple point sources nearby.

In fig:J0659_residuals , we show the imaging data, the model (lensing galaxy light and lensed quasars), and the normalized residuals given PSF-R, PSF-CS and PSF-R+PSF-CS, where PSF-R+PSR-CS means that we use PSF-R as an initial PSF and then perform PSF corrections on it. PSF-CS use concentric multiple gaussians as the intial PSF and perform the PSF corrections. While there are some residuals in the center of the lensed quasars images using PSF-R, we can build accurate PSF structures and model the data down to the noise level, except a very small region right in the center of the PSF core, once we apply the PSF-CS method by exploiting the fact that the four lensed quasar images share the same PSF structure.

We analyze adaptive optics images of the strong lens J 0659+1629 obtained with the W.M.Keck Observatory using the laser guide star adaptive optics system to examine the astrometric requirements for time-delay cosmography under the mass-sheet transformation. We show that by combining two techniques of PSF reconstruction (PSF-R from telemetry and PSF-CS from the data themselves), we can (1) reconstruct both the core and wings of the AO PSF, (2) subtract the point-like multiple images with residuals consistent with noise, and (3) obtain astrometric precision of 0.3 mas in the source plane which is more then sufficient to meet the requirements even in the most stringent cases, with JWST spatially-resolved kinematics data are available.

sources.summary: The paper describes J0659+1629 as a Gaia-selected lensed quasar at z about 3.09 whose published models struggle with flux ratios and may require a missing nearby galaxy.

quotes.quote: J0659+1629 This system was discovered by Lemon et al. in prep as a triple detection in Gaia DR2 around a photometric quasar candidate. They confirm the source redshift to be 3.09, and an SIE + shear model requires only a modest shear of 0.06, however the predicted flux of image D is 60 fainter than observed, suggesting variability over the time delay as a possible cause for this discrepancy. The system was also independently selected by delchambre2019 using the astrometry of the three Gaia DR2 detections, and stern2021 also spectroscopically confirm that the source redshift is 3.083, and the lens redshift is 0.766. stern2021 model the system as an SIS + shear, however their flux ratios are poorly reproduced and the ellipticity is unrealistic. stern2021 suggest this is indicative of a missing nearby galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper presents J0659+1629 as a short-axis quad with a large Einstein radius, z=3.09 source spectra, high model magnification, and an anomalously bright image D.

quotes.quote: J0659+1629 This lens is a short axis quad, with a large Einstein radius, 2.3 . We obtained spectra of the two brightest images, confirming the source to be a quasar at z = 3.09. The best fit SIE+shear model has a magnification of 25, and a modest shear of 0.06. The model predicts image D to be 60 fainter than observed. Since this system has a large time delay between image D, we expect that variability over the time delay can explain this discrepancy.

sources.summary: PS J0659+1629 was modeled in F160W with a satellite component; the paper finds possible second-source counterimages, underfitted quasar amplitudes, and the largest mass-light mismatch in the sample.

quotes.quote: PS J0659+1629 This lensing system was modeled in the IR F160W band because of insufficient arc light in the other bands. We also include an SIS profile for the satellite mass and a S e rsic profile for the satellite light. The satellite is very close to the northern-most quasar image, thus we boost the error map in the satellite region in the same way as the quasar images in the last modeling step. The PSF was estimated with four stars from the field. We found two very faint light clumps in the residuals, which are at positions that suggest them to be counter images of a second, lensed background source at a similar redshift as the first source.

gmw_00004177.name: PGC044257
gmw_00004177.overview: PGC044257 is a cD4 elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1701.07036] that serves as the brightest galaxy in the cluster A1644 [arXiv:1701.07036]. It exhibits a slight spatial offset from its optical line emission [arXiv:1303.0884], and its gaseous and stellar components are kinematically decoupled [arXiv:1303.0884].

sources.summary: The paper presents first IFU observations of PGC044257, noting its offset from optical line emission, high stellar velocity range, decoupled gas recession velocity, and LINER-like centre.

quotes.quote: PGC044257 This CCG is one of the rare cases (occurs in 3 per cent of CCGs; Hamer et al. \ 2012) where the CCG is slightly offset from the optical line emission (Johnson et al. \ 2010). The rarity of such offsets points to a large event in cluster evolution, a major cluster merger or possibly a powerful AGN outburst. Whatever the reason for the separation the gas cooling at the X-ray peak will continue and cooled gas will be deposited away from the CCG (Hamer et al. \ 2012). IFU observations of this galaxy has not been presented in the literature before this study. The stellar line-of-sight velocity is blue-and redshifted by 225 km s ^ 1 which is higher than derived from long slit spectroscopy (20 16 km s ^ 1 Paper 1).

Both the stellar and gaseous components show morphologies (Figures fig:PGC044kinematics and fig:PGC044kinematics2 ) that are elongated and aligned. However the recession velocity of the gas is 200 km s ^ 1 , which is higher than that of the stars, suggesting that the gaseous and stellar components are decoupled. The rotation of the warm gas agrees with the long-slit observations by McDonald et al. \ (2012).

sources.summary: J125711-172434 is a cD4 elliptical and brightest A1644 source with new H i absorption; its X-ray activity may be AGN-related and its narrow and broad components trace unsettled gas.

quotes.quote: J125711-172434 (z = 0.0475) We find a new detection against this flat spectrum radio source, which is classified as a cD4 elliptical galaxy with a very faint yet significantly extended corona deVaucouleurs1991 . It is also the brightest source in the cluster A1644 Hoessel1980, Postman1995 . This cluster is the host of a few X-ray sources, including 3XMM J125712.0-172431 which has a X-ray flux of 1.39 10 ^ 13 ergs cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 Rosen2015 . This X-ray activity could be attributed to the AGN in the host galaxy, or with the cluster's hot intergalactic gas.

The galaxy was found to only have absorption line features within its optical spectrum Mahony2011 , which contrasts with the X-ray emission detected. We find two separate components in our spectral line modelling. The first is a narrow feature centred slightly blueward ( 35 km s ^ 1 ) of the optical spectroscopic redshift with v_ FWHM = 133.5 ^ +100.4 _ 64.5 , and a broader component slightly redshifted by 24 km s ^ 1 with v_ FWHM = 154.1 ^ +84.3 _ 85.1 km s ^ 1 (Table 3). As this is an elliptical host galaxy whose mid-infrared WISE colours indicate a low star formation rate (Section 5.1.1), the features here may be tracing gas close to the AGN, and as we find a broad line, the gas may be unsettled to be distributed across such scales.

gmw_00004178.name: NGC 4651
gmw_00004178.overview: NGC 4651 is an Sc spiral galaxy [arXiv:2306.09854] that is considered a low-stellar-mass evolutionary analogue of the Milky Way [arXiv:2306.09854]. It features an asymmetric neutral hydrogen distribution [arXiv:1610.05316], a molecule-poor disk [arXiv:2303.07549], and tidal debris that includes a stellar tail, a stick feature, and shell-like structures [arXiv:1610.05316, arXiv:1603.04656].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4651 as a Milky Way twin candidate whose radius, oxygen abundance distribution, and rotation curve agree with the Milky Way, but whose stellar mass is lower.

quotes.quote: The galaxy NGC 4651 is an Sc galaxy (morphological type code T = 5.1 0.6). The inclination angle of NGC 4651 is i = 53 , and the position angle of the major axis is PA = 77 Epinat2008 . The optical radius is 1.99 arcmin RC3 . At the distance of d = 19.0 Mpc Foster2014 , the physical optical radius is R_ 25 = 11.00 kpc. The stellar mass is M_ = 2.61 10 ^ 10 M_ , or log( M_ /M_ ) = 10.42 Leroy2019 (or M_ = 1.7 10 ^ 10 M_ or log( M_ /M_ ) = 10.23 Foster2014 ).

The radial distributions of the oxygen abundances (panel (a) of Fig. figure:ngc4651--oh ) and the rotation curves (panel (b) of Fig. figure:ngc4651--oh ) in those galaxies agree satisfactorily to each other. While the rotation velocity of NGC 4651 is rather close to that of the Milky Way, the value of the stellar mass of NGC 4651 (log( M_ /M_ ) = 10.42) is appreciably lower (by a factor of approximately two, or even more) than the stellar mass of the Milky Way (log( M_ /M_ ) = 10.716). If the estimations of the stellar mass of NGC 4651 are correct, then NGC 4651 can be considered a low(-stellar) mass evolutionary analogue of the Milky Way.

sources.summary: The paper discusses NGC 4651 as a quasar-galaxy-pair foreground galaxy whose outer disk and asymmetric H I distribution were probed for 21-cm absorption along radio sightlines.

quotes.quote: The authors also report \ 1 \ emission along the quasar sightline from the outer disk of NGC 4651 at the level of \ 3 10 ^ 19 \ from VLA D-configuration observations. Combining this with their optical depth measurement they constrain the \ gas in the outer \ disk of NGC 4651 to be warmer than 500 K.

All three radio sightlines are at b 12 kpc from NGC 4651. chung2009 have estimated the isophotal \ diameter (where the azimuthally averaged \ surface density falls to 1 M_ pc ^ 2 ) and the effective \ diameter (encompassing 50 of the total flux) of NGC 4651 to be 30.3 kpc and 15.6 kpc, respectively. From the high resolution and high sensitivity \ emission map of NGC 4651 presented in chung2009 , the \ distribution is asymmetric and extended to the west, i.e. away from the radio sightlines. In the opposite side of the \ tail i.e. to the north-east, a stellar tail is found in the optical images (see Fig. fig:gmrtcontours1 ).

sources.summary: The paper confirms significant extended X-ray emission from NGC 4651 while finding no compact jet-like structure in the HRI image.

quotes.quote: The nearby (Virgo cluster) galaxy NGC 4651 is adjacent to the radio-loud quasar 3C 275.1 ( z=0.557 ). In this case the quasar is a reasonably strong X-ray source, while the galaxy shows some weaker extended emission. Hardcastle Worrall (1999) found the PSPC emission from the quasar to be consistent with that of a point source, although the HRI observations of the source suggest some extension on small scales, consistent with emission from a cluster (Hardcastle Worrall 1999, Crawford \ 1999). NGC 4651 has an optical jet or tail; Arp (1996) views this as evidence for ejection, while Wehrle, Keel Jones (1997) suggest that the optical colours are evidence for a tidal origin.
We can safely conclude that the extension of NGC 4651 is likely to be real. However, there is no evidence for a compact jet-like structure in NGC 4651 in the HRI image.

sources.summary: The chapter describes NGC 4651, the Umbrella Galaxy, as a distant stream system with Subaru imaging revealing a stick, broad arc, shell features, and tidal debris about 1/50 of its stellar mass.
